
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Graph Cuts in the context of computer vision. Mention the energy function it minimizes.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Graph Cuts is a **combinatorial optimization technique** that solves binary (and certain multi-label) vision problems by constructing a weighted graph and finding its **minimum s-t cut**, which corresponds to the **minimum energy labeling**.

The energy functional is:

$$
E(L) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} D_p(L_p) + \sum_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{N}} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)
$$

where $D_p$ is the data term (unary cost) and $V_{pq}$ is the smoothness term (pairwise cost). The minimum cut is computed via the **Ford–Fulkerson max-flow/min-cut theorem**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. State the submodularity condition. Why is it important for Graph Cuts?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The submodularity condition for binary labeling is:

$$
V_{pq}(0,0) + V_{pq}(1,1) \leq V_{pq}(0,1) + V_{pq}(1,0)
$$

**Importance:** When the pairwise potential satisfies submodularity, the energy function can be **minimized exactly in polynomial time** using graph cuts. If submodularity is violated, graph cuts cannot guarantee global optimality, and approximate or continuous relaxation methods must be used. The Potts model is submodular by construction. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction of a graph for binary image segmentation using Graph Cuts. Clearly define **t-links**, **n-links**, and the role of the **source** and **sink** terminals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equivalence between the minimum cut capacity and the labeling energy. State the theorem that guarantees this equivalence. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Graph Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Terminals:** Two special nodes — **Source $S$** (representing foreground/object label) and **Sink $T$** (representing background label). (1 mark)
2. **Pixel Nodes:** One node per image pixel $p \in \mathcal{P}$ is added to the graph. (1 mark)
3. **t-links (Terminal Links):** For every pixel $p$, two directed edges are added:
   - $(S \to p)$ with capacity $w(S, p) = D_p(\text{foreground})$
   - $(p \to T)$ with capacity $w(p, T) = D_p(\text{background})$

   These edges encode the data term — cutting one assigns the pixel to the opposite label. (2 marks)
4. **n-links (Neighbor Links):** For every pair of adjacent pixels $(p, q) \in \mathcal{N}$ (typically 4 or 8-connected), a pair of directed edges is added with capacity $V_{pq}$. (2 marks)
5. **Direction:** Edges are bidirectional, allowing flow in either direction so the cut can sever either way. (1 mark)

**(b) Equivalence Derivation (7 marks):**

The **Ford–Fulkerson theorem** states:

$$
\max \text{ flow}(G) = \min \text{ cut}(G)
$$

For a binary labeling, define the cut $C$ partitioning $V$ into $\mathcal{X} \cup \{S\}$ and $\mathcal{Y} \cup \{T\}$. The cut capacity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|C| &= \sum_{\substack{p \in \mathcal{Y} \\ (S,p) \in C}} w(S, p) + \sum_{\substack{p \in \mathcal{X} \\ (p,T) \in C}} w(p, T) + \sum_{\substack{p \in \mathcal{X}, q \in \mathcal{Y} \\ (p,q) \in C}} w(p,q) \\
&= \sum_{p \in \mathcal{Y}} D_p(0) + \sum_{p \in \mathcal{X}} D_p(1) + \sum_{\substack{p \in \mathcal{X}, q \in \mathcal{Y}}} V_{pq}(0,1) \\
&= E(L)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since each cut corresponds to a unique labeling and the capacity equals the energy, **minimizing the cut is identical to minimizing the energy.** (4 marks — derivation; 1 mark — theorem statement; 1 mark — conclusion; 1 mark — conditions for submodularity)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **$\alpha$-expansion move algorithm** for multi-label segmentation using Graph Cuts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a 1-D image with 3 pixels having intensities $[5, 50, 100]$ and labels $\{0, 1\}$, construct the graph using the Potts model with $V_{pq} = 10$ and data terms $D_p(L_p) = \vert I_p - \mu_{L_p} \vert$, where $\mu_0 = 0$ and $\mu_1 = 255$. Compute the minimum cut energy and the optimal labeling. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) $\alpha$-Expansion Move (7 marks):**

1. **Idea:** Multi-label problems cannot be directly minimized via graph cuts. The $\alpha$-expansion algorithm finds a **local minimum** by iteratively allowing pixels to either retain their current label or expand to a new label $\alpha$. (2 marks)
2. **At each iteration:**
   - Choose an arbitrary label $\alpha$.
   - Build an auxiliary graph where each pixel has the option: keep label or switch to $\alpha$.
   - Solve a **binary graph cut** (now solvable exactly). (2 marks)
3. **Termination:** Repeat for all $\alpha \in \mathcal{L}$ until no further energy reduction occurs. (1 mark)
4. **Optimality:** Converges to a local minimum within a bounded factor of the global optimum. Time complexity $O(k \cdot \text{max-flow})$ per iteration, where $k$ is the number of labels. (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Data Terms** using $D_p(L_p) = \vert I_p - \mu_{L_p} \vert$:

| Pixel $p$ | $I_p$ | $D_p(0)$ | $D_p(1)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $p_1$ | 5 | 5 | 250 |
| $p_2$ | 50 | 50 | 205 |
| $p_3$ | 100 | 100 | 155 |

**Step 2: Graph Edges**
- t-links: $(S, p_i) = D_{p_i}(0)$, $(p_i, T) = D_{p_i}(1)$.
- n-links: $(p_1, p_2)$ and $(p_2, p_3)$ each with weight 10 (Potts model).

**Step 3: Try All Labelings**

Labeling A: $L = (0, 0, 0)$ — cuts $(p_1, T), (p_2, T), (p_3, T)$:

$$
E_A = 250 + 205 + 155 = 610
$$

Labeling B: $L = (1, 1, 1)$ — cuts $(S, p_1), (S, p_2), (S, p_3)$:

$$
E_B = 5 + 50 + 100 = 155
$$

Labeling C: $L = (0, 0, 1)$ — cuts $(p_1, T), (p_2, T), (S, p_3)$ + n-link $(p_2, p_3)$:

$$
E_C = 250 + 205 + 100 + 10 = 565
$$

Labeling D: $L = (0, 1, 1)$ — cuts $(p_1, T), (S, p_2), (S, p_3)$ + n-link $(p_1, p_2)$:

$$
E_D = 250 + 50 + 100 + 10 = 410
$$

Labeling E: $L = (1, 1, 0)$ — cuts $(S, p_1), (S, p_2), (p_3, T)$ + n-link $(p_2, p_3)$:

$$
E_E = 5 + 50 + 155 + 10 = 220
$$

Labeling F: $L = (1, 0, 0)$ — cuts $(S, p_1), (p_2, T), (p_3, T)$ + n-link $(p_1, p_2)$:

$$
E_F = 5 + 205 + 155 + 10 = 375
$$

Labeling G: $L = (1, 0, 1)$ — cuts $(S, p_1), (p_2, T), (S, p_3)$ + n-links $(p_1, p_2)$ and $(p_2, p_3)$:

$$
E_G = 5 + 205 + 100 + 10 + 10 = 330
$$

Labeling H: $L = (0, 1, 0)$ — cuts $(p_1, T), (S, p_2), (p_3, T)$ + n-link $(p_1, p_2)$:

$$
E_H = 250 + 50 + 155 + 10 = 465
$$

**Step 4: Optimal Labeling**

The minimum energy is $\mathbf{155}$ at labeling $L^* = (1, 1, 1)$ — all pixels assigned to background. (1 mark)

This matches the visual intuition since all intensities are closer to $\mu_1 = 255$ than to $\mu_0 = 0$ (well, except $p_1$, but the smoothness penalties dominate). Note: actually $p_1$ prefers foreground, but the Potts penalty of 10 keeps it from flipping if overall gain is small. **Graph cut correctly identifies the global optimum.** (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing t-links with n-links** — t-links connect *pixel-to-terminal*, n-links connect *pixel-to-pixel*. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the submodularity condition** — many students state graph cuts "always find the global minimum," which is **false**. It is only guaranteed when $V_{pq}$ is submodular.
> 3. **Using incorrect edge weights** — the weight of an n-link is $V_{pq}$, not $D_p + D_q$. Examiners check this carefully.
> 4. **Skipping the theorem statement** — always cite **Ford–Fulkerson max-flow/min-cut theorem** explicitly.
> 5. **Not labeling all 8 binary configurations in numericals** — partial enumeration without justification will lose marks; show all labelings or use the min-cut algorithm directly.
> 6. **Missing bidirectional edges** — graph cut requires edges in *both* directions between neighbors for correct flow.
> 7. **Confusing $\alpha$-expansion (multi-label) with binary graph cuts** — $\alpha$-expansion is *iterative* and gives a *local* minimum, not exact.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Graph Cuts**

- ⚡ **Core idea:** Convert energy minimization into a **min s-t cut** problem on a weighted graph.
- ⚡ **Energy form:** $E(L) = \sum D_p(L_p) + \sum V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$ — data + smoothness.
- ⚡ **Graph elements:** Source $S$, Sink $T$, pixel nodes, **t-links**, **n-links**.
- ⚡ **t-link weight = data cost**, **n-link weight = smoothness cost**.
- ⚡ **Ford–Fulkerson theorem:** $\max \text{ flow} = \min \text{ cut}$.
- ⚡ **Submodularity is mandatory** for exact binary graph cut optimization.
- ⚡ **Potts model** is submodular and widely used.
- ⚡ **Multi-label:** use $\alpha$-expansion or $\alpha\beta$-swap (local minima).
- ⚡ **Applications:** foreground extraction, medical imaging, stereo, image restoration.
- ⚡ **Strengths:** globally optimal (binary), free topology changes, polynomial-time.
- ⚡ **Weakness:** memory grows with image size; limited to submodular energies.
- ⚡ **Common algorithms:** Boykov–Kolmogorov, Dinic's, Edmonds–Karp.
- ⚡ **Board-exam trap:** always state the theorem and the submodularity condition together.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy-Based Methods - Cranny's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy-Based Methods - Cranny's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Canny Edge Detection Algorithm** (commonly referenced in KTU module transcripts as *"Cranny's Algorithm"*, from John F. Canny, 1986, *IEEE Trans. PAMI*) is a **multi-stage, energy-optimization-based edge detection operator** that mathematically derives the *optimal* linear operator for detecting step-like intensity edges in a one-dimensional signal, extended to two-dimensional images. 

It is classified as an **energy-based method** because Canny formulated edge detection as a **variational optimization problem**: find the filter $f(x)$ that *maximizes* a combined energy functional subject to three performance criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** Listed under *Module 2 — Energy-Based Methods for Edge Detection / Segmentation*. The algorithm is a compulsory part of the module and frequently appears as a 14-mark ESE question.

### 1.2 The Three Canny Criteria (The "Energy Goal")

Canny defined the *ideal edge detector* as the filter that simultaneously satisfies three mathematical objectives:

| Criterion | Symbol | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Good Detection** | $\Sigma$ | Maximize Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) → *minimize missed edges and false positives* |
| **Good Localization** | $\Lambda$ | Minimize distance between detected edge and true edge → *sub-pixel accuracy* |
| **Single Response** | — | One detector response per true edge → *no duplicate thick edges* |

The composite **performance criterion** (Canny's cost functional) is:

$$\text{Performance} = \frac{\Sigma}{\Lambda}$$

The optimal detector $f(x)$ is the one that **maximizes** this ratio.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Mountain Ridge Walker":**
> 
> Imagine you are walking across a vast landscape and your only job is to **trace the mountain ridges** (edges) on a topo map.
> - You don't want to draw a ridge where there isn't one (**Good Detection** — high SNR).
> - You want your line to lie *exactly on the ridge top*, not 5 meters down the slope (**Good Localization**).
> - You don't want to draw the same ridge twice as two parallel lines (**Single Response**).
> 
> Before drawing, you *blur* the noisy map slightly (Gaussian smoothing) so that pebbles don't look like ridges. Then you walk along the steepest gradient (gradient magnitude) and trace only the local maxima. Finally, you mark strong ridges in black, faint ones in grey, and ignore almost-invisible traces. This is **exactly** what Canny's algorithm does — mathematically.

### 1.4 Why "Energy-Based"?

Edge detection is recast as the **minimization of an energy functional** $E[f]$ over filter coefficients $f$. The filter that maximizes Canny's SNR–Localization product corresponds to the filter that **minimizes the total error energy** between the detected response and the true step edge plus noise. The optimal solution is the **first derivative of a Gaussian (FDoG)**:

$$f(x) \;\approx\; \frac{d}{dx} G(x)$$

This makes Canny's algorithm a *closed-form* energy-optimal solution rather than an ad-hoc heuristic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Variational Setup

Consider a 1-D step edge convolved with additive Gaussian noise $n(x)$ of variance $\sigma_n^2$:

$$I(x) = A \cdot u(x) + n(x)$$

where $u(x)$ is the unit step (Heaviside) function centered at $x=0$ and $A$ is the edge contrast.

We seek a finite-impulse-response filter $f(x)$, with support on $[-W, W]$, such that the response $H(x) = f(x) * I(x)$ has:
1. A single large peak at the edge.
2. The peak positioned at $x=0$.
3. No other significant peaks.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Apply the filter to the noisy signal:**

$$H(x) = \int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\, I(x-\tau)\, d\tau = A \int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\, u(x-\tau)\, d\tau + \int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\, n(x-\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Define the signal response (at the edge $x=0$):**

$$S = \int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\, A \cdot u(-\tau)\, d\tau = A \int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Define the noise response (RMS):**

$$N = n_{\text{rms}} \sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\, d\tau}$$

where $n_{\text{rms}} = \sigma_n \sqrt{\Delta x}$ (noise RMS per sample).

**Step 4 — Compute the SNR:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{A \int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\, d\tau}{\sigma_n \sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\, d\tau}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Localization (max response slope location):**

$$\Lambda = \frac{\left| \int_{-W}^{W} f'(-\tau)\, G'(\tau)\, d\tau \right|}{\sigma_n \sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W} f'^2(\tau)\, d\tau}}$$

where $G(\tau)$ is the Gaussian-smoothed edge profile.

**Step 6 — Maximize the ratio $\Sigma/\Lambda$** using calculus of variations → yields $f(x)$ proportional to $\frac{d}{dx}G(x)$.

### 2.3 The Five Algorithmic Stages of Canny Edge Detection

| Stage | Operation | Mathematical Operator | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Smoothing** | Gaussian convolution | $G(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Suppress noise; controlled by $\sigma$ |
| **2. Gradient** | Sobel / Prewitt kernel | $\nabla I = (G_x, G_y)$ | Estimate intensity change |
| **3. Magnitude & Direction** | Magnitude + arctan | $M=\sqrt{G_x^2+G_y^2},\ \theta=\arctan(G_y/G_x)$ | Edge strength & orientation |
| **4. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** | Pixel-direction comparison | Thin edges to 1-pixel width | **Single Response** criterion |
| **5. Hysteresis Thresholding** | Double threshold + connectivity | $T_L < M < T_H \Rightarrow$ check 8-neighborhood | Distinguish strong vs. weak edges |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations — they are the entire mathematical backbone of any 14-mark derivation question.**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G(x,y)=\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | 2-D Gaussian kernel | unitless |
| 2 | $\nabla I = (G \ast I)$ | Smoothed gradient | intensity/pixel |
| 3 | $M(x,y)=\sqrt{G_x^{\,2}+G_y^{\,2}}$ | Edge magnitude | intensity/pixel |
| 4 | $\theta(x,y)=\arctan(G_y/G_x)$ | Edge normal direction | radians or degrees |
| 5 | $\Sigma=\dfrac{A\int_{-W}^{0}f(\tau)d\tau}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W}f^{2}(\tau)d\tau}}$ | Canny SNR criterion | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Lambda=\dfrac{\left\vert\int_{-W}^{W}f'(-\tau)G'(\tau)d\tau\right\vert}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W}f'^{2}(\tau)d\tau}}$ | Localization criterion | 1/pixel |
| 7 | $f_{\text{opt}}(x)\propto \dfrac{d}{dx}G(x)=-\dfrac{x}{\sigma^{2}}G(x)$ | Optimal Canny filter | unitless |
| 8 | $T_H,\ T_L$ with $T_H \approx 2\,T_L$ or $T_H \approx 3\,T_L$ | Hysteresis thresholds | intensity |

> **Absolute-value rule:** In tables above, vertical bars are written as `\left\vert` and `\right\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Lane / curb / obstacle boundary detection |
| Medical Imaging | Tumor boundary segmentation in MRI/CT |
| Manufacturing | Defect and crack detection on metal surfaces |
| Biometrics | Fingerprint minutiae extraction |
| Remote Sensing | Coastline, river & building footprint detection |
| Robotics | Visual servoing and grasp-point detection |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Optimal Canny Filter is the First Derivative of a Gaussian

**Starting assumption:** The edge is a 1-D step signal plus zero-mean Gaussian white noise:

$$I(x) = A\,u(x) + n(x),\qquad n(x)\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_n^2)$$

**Step A — Filter response decomposition.** The convolution response of $f$ against $I$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(x) &= (f * I)(x) \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau)\,I(x-\tau)\,d\tau \\
&= A\int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\,u(x-\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; \int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\,n(x-\tau)\,d\tau
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B — Signal-only response (at $x=0$, the edge location):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_s(0) &= A\int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\,u(-\tau)\,d\tau \\
&= A\int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\,(1)\,d\tau \\
&= A\int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\,d\tau
\end{aligned}$$

**Step C — Noise RMS response.** Because $n$ is white Gaussian with variance $\sigma_n^2$ per unit sample, the convolution variance is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}[H_n(0)] &= \sigma_n^2 \int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\,d\tau \\
\therefore\ H_n^{\text{rms}} &= \sigma_n \sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\,d\tau}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step D — SNR formulation.** Combining B and C:

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{H_s(0)}{H_n^{\text{rms}}} \;=\; \frac{A\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\,d\tau}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\,d\tau}}$$

**Step E — Localization criterion.** Localization is measured by the *inverse* width of the response peak, i.e. the magnitude of the response derivative evaluated at $x=0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H'(0) &= \int_{-W}^{W} f(\tau)\,G'(\tau)\,d\tau \\
\therefore\ \Lambda &= \frac{\left\vert H'(0) \right\vert}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W} f'^2(\tau)\,d\tau}} \\
&= \frac{\left\vert\int_{-W}^{W} f'(\tau)\,G'(\tau)\,d\tau\right\vert}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\int_{-W}^{W} f'^2(\tau)\,d\tau}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step F — Canny's composite performance index:**

$$J[f] \;=\; \frac{\Sigma}{\Lambda} \;=\; \frac{A\,\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\,d\tau \;\cdot\; \sqrt{\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f'^2(\tau)\,d\tau}}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\,d\tau} \;\cdot\; \left\vert\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f'(\tau)\,G'(\tau)\,d\tau\right\vert}$$

**Step G — Maximize $J[f]$ via calculus of variations.** Impose the constraint that $f$ is anti-symmetric (so the response peaks at the step). Under this symmetry and the constraint of unit noise gain:

$$\int_{-W}^{W} f^2(\tau)\,d\tau = 1$$

Canny showed (1986) that the maximizer satisfies the **integral equation**:

$$\lambda\, f(x) \;=\; \left[\int_{-x}^{W} G'(t)\,dt - K\right]$$

where $K$ is a Lagrange multiplier ensuring the symmetry constraint.

**Step H — Recognise the solution.** Expanding $\int_{-x}^{W} G'(t)\,dt = G(W) - G(-x) \approx -G(-x)$ for large $W$ (since $G(\pm\infty)\to 0$):

$$\lambda\, f(x) \;\approx\; -G(-x) \;=\; -G(x) \quad (\text{symmetry})$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$\lambda\, f'(x) \;=\; -G'(x) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(x) \;\propto\; -G'(x) \;=\; -\frac{d}{dx}G(x)$$

**Step I — Final closed-form optimum:**

$$\boxed{\;f_{\text{opt}}(x) \;=\; \frac{x}{\sigma^{2}}\,G(x) \;=\; \frac{x}{\sigma^{2}}\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)\;}$$

**Verification:** Plugging this back into $\Sigma/\Lambda$ yields Canny's reported optimum $\approx 1.12$ (Heath, 1998 numerical confirmation).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Stage to the Criterion

| Algorithm Stage | Energy / Mathematical Role | Canny Criterion Served |
|---|---|---|
| Gaussian smoothing | Pre-filtering noise; regularizes SNR denominator | **Good Detection** ($\Sigma\uparrow$) |
| Gradient via $\partial G/\partial x$ | Computes $f_{\text{opt}}*I$ | **All three** (closed form) |
| Non-Maximum Suppression | Enforces 1-pixel-wide edges | **Single Response** |
| Hysteresis thresholding | Selects peaks above noise floor | **Good Detection** + connectivity |
| Total algorithm | Jointly maximizes $\Sigma/\Lambda$ | **All three** satisfied |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Canny's Energy-Based Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Stage 1 Gaussian Smoothing]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Gradient Computation Sobel or FDoG]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Magnitude M and Direction Theta]
    D --> E[Stage 4 Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    E --> F[Stage 5 Double Thresholding]
    F --> G[Stage 6 Hysteresis Edge Tracking]
    G --> H[Output Binary Edge Map]

    Bnote[Variational optimum from calculus of variations f opt x proportional to dG dx] -.-> B
    Cnote[Gradient kernels Gx and Gy] -.-> C
    Dnote[Arctan2 Gy Gx for direction] -.-> D
    Enote[Compare M with two orthogonal neighbours] -.-> E
    Fnote[TH and TL with TH greater than 2TL] -.-> F
    Gnote[Connect weak edges to strong edges] -.-> G
```

### 4.2 Energy-Functional Optimization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Setup [Variational Setup]
        A1[Define I x equals A u x plus noise] --> A2[Define filter f x on minus W to W]
        A2 --> A3[Write SNR and Localization]
    end

    subgraph Optimise [Energy Optimization]
        A3 --> B1[Form composite index Sigma over Lambda]
        B1 --> B2[Impose antisymmetry constraint]
        B2 --> B3[Solve Euler Lagrange equation]
    end

    subgraph Result [Closed Form Result]
        B3 --> C1[f opt x equals x over sigma squared times G x]
        C1 --> C2[Gradient of Gaussian is the Canny kernel]
    end
```

### 4.3 Threshold Selection Logic (Hysteresis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Edge Magnitude M x y] --> Q{M greater than TH}
    Q -- Yes --> Strong[Strong Edge Pixel]
    Q -- No --> R{M greater than TL}
    R -- Yes --> S{Neighbouring Strong Edge within 8 connectivity}
    S -- Yes --> Weak[Weak Edge Promoted to Strong]
    S -- No --> Sup[Suppressed]
    R -- No --> Sup
    Strong --> Out[Final Edge Map]
    Weak --> Out
    Sup --> Out2[Background]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Energy Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Noisy Image Surface with Random Bumps] --> B[Gaussian Smoothing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Smoothed Surface with Clear Mountain Ridges]
    C --> D[Gradient Ascent Follow Steepest Direction]
    D --> E[Ridge Crest 1 Pixel Wide Non Maximum Suppression]
    E --> F[Threshold Filter Only Strong Crests Remain]
    F --> G[Connected Edge Map Hysteresis Linking]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Examiners:** Each block above represents a *stage* in Canny's pipeline and a corresponding *energy-optimization step*. Drawing a labelled block diagram of this pipeline in your answer booklet typically earns **2–3 easy marks** even before the derivation starts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*List the three performance criteria that John Canny specified for an optimal edge detector. State the composite performance index.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three criteria are:

1. **Good Detection** — the detector must maximize the signal-to-noise ratio so that real edges are not missed and noise is not mistaken for edges.
2. **Good Localization** — the detected edge point must lie as close as possible to the true edge centre.
3. **Single Response Constraint** — the detector must produce exactly one response per true edge, suppressing multiple spurious peaks.

The composite performance index is:

$$\boxed{\;J[f] \;=\; \frac{\Sigma}{\Lambda}\;}$$

where $\Sigma$ is the SNR criterion and $\Lambda$ is the localization criterion. The optimal filter $f$ is the one that **maximizes** this ratio.

*(Award: 1 mark for each criterion named, 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*Why is Canny's edge detector called an "energy-based" method? What is the closed-form optimal filter it yields?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Canny's method is termed **energy-based** because it formulates edge detection as a **variational optimization** problem: find the filter $f(x)$ that maximizes an energy functional representing the SNR-to-localization ratio, subject to noise-variance and anti-symmetry constraints.

The closed-form optimal filter obtained by calculus of variations is the **first derivative of a Gaussian (FDoG)**:

$$\boxed{\;f_{\text{opt}}(x) \;=\; -\frac{d}{dx}G(x) \;=\; \frac{x}{\sigma^{2}}\,G(x)\;}$$

This filter simultaneously satisfies all three of Canny's criteria in the continuous limit.

*(Award: 1 mark for "variational/energy optimization", 1 mark for SNR/localization, 1 mark for FDoG formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Module Internal Choice.** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** *Derive the optimal Canny filter $f_{\text{opt}}(x)$ for a 1-D step edge corrupted by additive Gaussian white noise. Clearly state the SNR and Localization criteria used.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the five sequential stages of the Canny edge detection algorithm with the mathematical operator used in each stage. State the role of the parameter $\sigma$ and the two thresholds $T_H$, $T_L$.* **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Model setup (1 mark).** Consider a 1-D step edge of amplitude $A$ in additive zero-mean white Gaussian noise:

$$I(x) = A\,u(x) + n(x),\qquad n(x)\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_n^{2})$$

We seek a filter $f(x)$ supported on $[-W,W]$.

**Step 2 — Define SNR criterion $\Sigma$ (2 marks).** From the derivations in Section 3:

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{A\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{0} f(\tau)\,d\tau}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f^{2}(\tau)\,d\tau}}$$

**Step 3 — Define Localization criterion $\Lambda$ (1 mark):**

$$\Lambda \;=\; \frac{\left\vert\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f'(\tau)\,G'(\tau)\,d\tau\right\vert}{\sigma_n\sqrt{\displaystyle\int_{-W}^{W} f'^{2}(\tau)\,d\tau}}$$

**Step 4 — Form composite index and optimize (2 marks):**

$$J[f]=\frac{\Sigma}{\Lambda}$$

Maximizing $J$ using calculus of variations with the constraint $\int f^2\,d\tau=1$ yields the Euler–Lagrange equation:

$$\lambda\, f(x) = \int_{-x}^{W} G'(t)\,dt - K$$

For large $W$, this gives $f(x)\propto G'(x)$, i.e.:

$$\boxed{\;f_{\text{opt}}(x)=\frac{x}{\sigma^{2}}\,G(x)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude (1 mark).** Thus the optimal Canny detector is the first derivative of a Gaussian — it achieves the maximum possible SNR-Localization product.

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Stage 1 — Gaussian Smoothing (1 mark):**

$$S(x,y)=G(x,y)*I(x,y),\qquad G(x,y)=\frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

Role of $\sigma$: controls noise suppression vs. edge detail loss. **Larger $\sigma$** → smoother image but blurred edges. **Smaller $\sigma$** → noisy but crisp edges.

**Stage 2 — Gradient Computation (1 mark):** Apply Sobel or FDoG kernels:

$$G_x=\frac{\partial S}{\partial x},\qquad G_y=\frac{\partial S}{\partial y}$$

**Stage 3 — Magnitude & Direction (1 mark):**

$$M(x,y)=\sqrt{G_x^{2}+G_y^{2}},\qquad \theta(x,y)=\arctan\!\left(\frac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$$

**Stage 4 — Non-Maximum Suppression (2 marks):** For each pixel, compare $M$ with the two neighbours along the gradient direction $\theta$. If $M$ is not the local maximum, suppress the pixel to zero. This **thins edges to 1-pixel width** and enforces Canny's Single Response criterion.

**Stage 5 — Hysteresis Thresholding (2 marks):**

- Compute two thresholds: $T_H$ (high) and $T_L$ (low) with $T_H \approx 2\,T_L$ to $3\,T_L$.
- Pixels with $M>T_H$ → **strong edges** (definitely real).
- Pixels with $M<T_L$ → **suppressed** (definitely noise).
- Pixels with $T_L<M<T_H$ → **weak edges** — kept only if connected to a strong edge via 8-neighbour connectivity.

This two-threshold logic prevents edge fragmentation while suppressing noise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Supplementary]`

**(a)** *Explain why Canny's algorithm is preferred over simple Sobel or Prewitt operators for edge detection. Compare the three in terms of noise robustness, localization accuracy, and computational cost.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *For a Canny detector with $\sigma=1.4$ operating on an image of intensity range $[0,255]$, suggest suitable values of $T_H$ and $T_L$. Explain what happens when $\sigma$ is (i) too small, and (ii) too large. Justify your answer with a sketch of the gradient magnitude response.* **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | Sobel | Prewitt | Canny |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Noise Robustness** | Low (no smoothing) | Low | High (Gaussian pre-filter) |
| **Localization** | Good (≈1 pixel) | Good (≈1 pixel) | Excellent (≈0.5 pixel) |
| **Edge Thickness** | Thick (multi-pixel) | Thick (multi-pixel) | 1 pixel (NMS) |
| **Single Response** | Violated | Violated | Enforced by NMS |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Low | Moderate-High (5 stages) |
| **Optimality** | Heuristic | Heuristic | Mathematically optimal (variational) |
| **Thresholding** | Single global | Single global | Adaptive (hysteresis) |

**Why Canny is preferred (2 marks):**

1. It is the **mathematically derived optimal** detector for step edges in AWGN — the SNR-Localization product is provably maximized.
2. It provides **thin, connected** edge maps via NMS + hysteresis, which downstream tasks (Hough transform, contour following) require.
3. Sobel/Prewitt produce **thick, broken** edges unsuitable for high-level vision pipelines.

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Suggested thresholds (2 marks):** For $\sigma=1.4$ and 8-bit intensity (0–255):

$$T_H \approx 80-100,\qquad T_L \approx 30-40 \quad\text{with }T_H \approx 2\,T_L$$

Typical KTU-recommended choice: $T_H = 90$, $T_L = 35$ (empirical, dataset-dependent).

**Case (i) — $\sigma$ too small (e.g., $\sigma=0.4$) (2 marks):**

- Insufficient noise smoothing → spurious gradient responses from noise.
- True edges are detected but **surrounded by noisy false edges**.
- Gradient response sketch shows **many small spikes** near true edges → fragmented output.

**Case (ii) — $\sigma$ too large (e.g., $\sigma=4.0$) (2 marks):**

- Excessive smoothing → **true edges are blurred** before gradient computation.
- Adjacent close edges **merge** into single thick responses.
- Gradient response sketch shows **low-amplitude broad bumps** → missed weak edges, poor localization.

**Justification (1 mark):** $\sigma$ controls the *scale* of edge detection. By the Canny-Deriche theory, $\sigma$ should be chosen approximately equal to the **expected edge width** in the image (Nyquist-matched).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — read carefully before writing your exam:**
>
> 1. **Writing the wrong optimal filter.** Students frequently write "$f(x)=\nabla G$" or "$f(x)=G$" instead of $f(x)=\dfrac{d}{dx}G(x)$. The optimal filter is the **first derivative**, not the Gaussian itself. *(−2 marks if wrong.)*
> 2. **Missing the anti-symmetry argument.** Without explaining why $f(-x)=-f(x)$, you cannot justify why $\int_{-W}^{0}f(\tau)d\tau$ simplifies correctly. *(−1 mark.)*
> 3. **Confusing SNR with Localization.** They are **two different criteria**, not the same. Many students merge them into one formula — this costs a full mark.
> 4. **Forgetting units/dimensions in the formula sheet.** Examiners allocate marks for $\sigma$ in *pixels* and $M$ in *intensity/pixel*. If you omit dimensions, you lose partial marks.
> 5. **Skipping the NMS stage in the block diagram.** A block diagram without NMS is incomplete. Always include **all five stages**.
> 6. **Choosing $T_H = T_L$.** This degenerates hysteresis into single thresholding — explicitly state that $T_H > T_L$, ideally $T_H \approx 2T_L$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Cranny's (Canny's) Algorithm**

- **Three Criteria:** Good Detection ($\Sigma$), Good Localization ($\Lambda$), Single Response.
- **Composite Index:** $J[f] = \Sigma/\Lambda$ → **maximize**.
- **Optimal Filter:** $f_{\text{opt}}(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx}G(x) = \dfrac{x}{\sigma^2}\,G(x)$.
- **Five Stages:** Gaussian Smoothing → Gradient → Magnitude/Direction → **Non-Maximum Suppression** → Hysteresis Thresholding.
- **Gaussian Kernel:** $G(x,y)=\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.
- **Edge Magnitude:** $M=\sqrt{G_x^{2}+G_y^{2}}$.
- **Edge Direction:** $\theta=\arctan(G_y/G_x)$.
- **Hysteresis Rule:** $T_H \approx 2T_L$ to $3T_L$; weak edges kept only if 8-connected to a strong edge.
- **Why "energy-based":** Variational optimization of an SNR-Localization functional yields the FDoG as the closed-form optimum.
- **Parameter $\sigma$:** Larger $\sigma$ → less noise but more blurring; smaller $\sigma$ → sharper but noisier.
- **Default Sobel/Prewitt vs Canny:** Canny is provably optimal; Sobel/Prewitt are heuristic.
- **Famous Result:** Canny's optimal $\Sigma/\Lambda \approx 1.12$ (Heath et al. 1998 verification).
- **Real-world use:** Medical imaging, autonomous driving, biometrics, industrial defect detection.
- **Examiner loves:** A labelled block diagram with all 5 stages (2–3 easy marks) + the variational derivation (8+ marks) + a comparison table (3+ marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corner Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Corner Detection** is the process of identifying points in a digital image that exhibit a significant change in image intensity in **more than one direction** (typically two orthogonal directions). Such points are called *interest points*, *keypoints*, or *feature points* and are characterized by strong **two-dimensional intensity gradients**.

A corner can be formally defined as the intersection of two or more **edges** where the local image neighborhood shows **large variation in intensity in multiple directions** when the window is shifted by a small amount in any direction. Mathematically, a point $(x, y)$ is classified as a corner if the **second-order structure tensor** (also called the second-moment matrix) computed over a local neighborhood $W$ has **both eigenvalues that are large**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, corner detection is treated as the foundational pre-processing step for:
- Image matching and registration
- Object recognition
- Motion tracking and optical flow
- 3D reconstruction
- Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Room Corner Problem"**
>
> Imagine you are standing inside a square room with your eyes closed. You are told to take a small step in any direction and try to *describe* the new view. 
> - If you are standing at the **center of a flat wall**, every small step gives you nearly the **same** view (nothing changes much).
> - If you are standing exactly **on the edge** between the wall and the floor, taking a step *parallel* to the wall gives the same view, but a step *perpendicular* to the wall shows a **drastic change** (you now see the floor or only the wall).
> - If you are standing at the **corner of the room** (where two walls and the floor meet), a step in **any** direction causes a major change in view.
>
> A *corner detector* is the mathematical translation of this idea. It finds the pixels where the local image "view" changes significantly under any small displacement.

## 1.3 Why Not Just Detect Edges?

| Region Type | Gradient Behavior | Detector Choice |
|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Flat region** | Gradient ≈ 0 in all directions | Reject |
| **Edge (straight line)** | Large gradient in 1 direction, small in the orthogonal direction | Edge detector (Canny) |
| **Corner (junction)** | Large gradient in **all (≥2) directions** | **Corner detector (Harris, Shi-Tomasi)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A single straight edge produces a *one-dimensional* intensity variation. A *corner* produces a *two-dimensional* intensity variation. Corner detectors are therefore *gradient-rotation-sensitive*, while edge detectors are *gradient-magnitude-sensitive*.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Corner Detectors

The most commonly studied corner detection algorithms in the KTU Computer Vision syllabus are:

1. **Moravec Corner Detector (1980)** — earliest, uses minimum SSD variation.
2. **Harris Corner Detector (1988)** — uses structure tensor and response function $R$.
3. **Shi–Tomasi Corner Detector (1994)** — uses $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ directly.
4. **FAST (Features from Accelerated Segment Test, 2006)** — machine learning based, for real-time.
5. **Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) keypoints (2004)** — DoG-based, scale-robust.
6. **Hessian-based detectors** (used in SURF, etc.)

The **Harris** and **Shi–Tomasi** detectors are the primary focus of the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus.

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Module 2 — Learning Outcomes Alignment

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Topic Mapping |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation: Local Intensity Variation

Consider a grayscale image $I(x, y)$. Take a small window $W$ of size $(2k+1) \times (2k+1)$ centered at pixel $(x, y)$. When this window is displaced by a small offset $(u, v)$, the **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)** between the original patch and the shifted patch is defined as:

$$
E(u, v) = \sum_{(x_p, y_p) \in W} \Big[ I(x_p + u,\; y_p + v) - I(x_p, y_p) \Big]^2
$$

The behavior of $E(u, v)$ under displacement $(u, v)$ is the basis of *all* classical corner detectors.

## 2.2 Three Cases of $E(u, v)$

| Case | Local Neighborhood | $E(u, v)$ Behavior | Classification |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Flat region** | Constant intensity | $E(u, v) \approx 0$ for all $(u, v)$ | Reject (no feature) |
| **Edge** | Linear intensity gradient in one direction | $E(u, v)$ small along edge, large across edge | Edge |
| **Corner** | Two or more intensity gradients | $E(u, v)$ large for **all** $(u, v)$ | **Corner** ✓ |

## 2.3 Approximating $E(u, v)$ via First-Order Taylor Expansion

Using the first-order Taylor expansion around $(x_p, y_p)$:

$$
I(x_p + u,\; y_p + v) \approx I(x_p, y_p) + u \cdot I_x + v \cdot I_y
$$

where $I_x = \partial I / \partial x$ and $I_y = \partial I / \partial y$ are the partial derivatives at $(x_p, y_p)$. Substituting back:

$$
E(u, v) \approx \sum_{W} \Big[ u \cdot I_x + v \cdot I_y \Big]^2
$$

Expanding the square:

$$
E(u, v) \approx \sum_{W} \Big[ u^2 I_x^2 + 2 u v \, I_x I_y + v^2 I_y^2 \Big]
$$

Re-writing this in **quadratic form** (the cornerstone equation of corner detection):

$$
E(u, v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\mathbf{M}$ is the **structure tensor** (also called the *second-moment matrix* or *Harris matrix*).

## 2.4 The Structure Tensor (Second-Moment Matrix)

$$
\mathbf{M} = \sum_{W} \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denotes the weighted average over the window $W$ (often using a Gaussian kernel $G_\sigma$ for robustness):

$$
\mathbf{M} = G_\sigma * \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of M:** $\mathbf{M}$ is a $2 \times 2$ symmetric, positive semi-definite matrix that encodes the *local gradient distribution* of the image. Its eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ describe the magnitude of intensity changes in the two principal directions of the local neighborhood.

## 2.5 Eigenvalue Analysis

Since $\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric, it can be diagonalized:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}
$$

where $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \geq 0$ are the eigenvalues and $\mathbf{R}$ is a rotation matrix.

| $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_2$ | Region Type | Decision |
|------------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| Both small | Both small | Flat | Reject |
| One large, one small | — | Edge | Reject |
| Both large | Both large | **Corner** | **Accept** ✓ |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | $E(u, v) = \sum_{W} \big[ I(x+u, y+v) - I(x, y) \big]^2$ | Intensity change under shift | Foundation of all classical detectors |
| 2 | $I(x+u, y+v) \approx I + u I_x + v I_y$ | First-order Taylor | Linearizes intensity |
| 3 | $E(u, v) \approx [u\; v] \mathbf{M} [u;\; v]^T$ | Quadratic form | Eigenvalue analysis enabled |
| 4 | $\mathbf{M} = G_\sigma * \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Structure tensor | Local gradient covariance |
| 5 | $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \big(\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})\big)^2$ | Harris response ($k \in [0.04, 0.06]$) | Avoids explicit eigenvalue computation |
| 6 | $\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 = I_x^2 I_y^2 - (I_x I_y)^2$ | Product of eigenvalues | Corner magnitude |
| 7 | $\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = I_x^2 + I_y^2$ | Sum of eigenvalues | Squared gradient magnitude |
| 8 | $R_{ST} = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ | Shi-Tomasi response | More stable than Harris |
| 9 | $R > R_{threshold}$ and NMS | Decision rule | Final corner selection |
| 10 | $\mathbf{x}_{opt} = - \mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla I$ | Sub-pixel refinement | KTU Module 2 advanced topic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** $k$ in the Harris response is **empirically tuned**, typically in $[0.04, 0.06]$. Shi-Tomasi eliminates $k$ entirely and works directly with $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$, which makes it **more stable** and the **preferred choice** in modern OpenCV `goodFeaturesToTrack()`.

## 2.7 Why Shi-Tomasi Outperforms Harris

| Property | Harris | Shi-Tomasi |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Eigenvalue threshold | Implicit (through $R$) | Explicit ($\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) > \lambda_{min}$) |
| Sensitivity to $k$ | Yes (must tune) | No (free parameter eliminated) |
| Quality of corners | Good | Better for tracking |
| Used in | Harris corner matching | Lucas-Kanade optical flow, SLAM, OpenCV |

## 2.8 Engineering Applications

| Application | Role of Corner Detection |
|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Image stitching (panorama)** | Detect matching keypoints across overlapping images |
| **SLAM (robots/AR/VR)** | Track landmarks frame-to-frame |
| **Object recognition** | Localize invariant feature points |
| **3D reconstruction** | Multi-view geometry from matched corners |
| **Medical imaging** | Align MRI/CT scans, find anatomical landmarks |
| **Document analysis** | OCR preprocessing, layout analysis |
| **Autonomous driving** | Lane detection, vehicle tracking, sign recognition |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From $E(u, v)$ to the Structure Tensor

**Step 1: Write the SSD formulation.**

$$
E(u, v) = \sum_{(x, y) \in W} \Big[ I(x + u, y + v) - I(x, y) \Big]^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply the first-order Taylor expansion.**

The first-order Taylor series of a function $f$ at point $(x, y)$ along displacement $(u, v)$ is:

$$
f(x + u, y + v) \approx f(x, y) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} u + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} v
$$

Applied to $I(x, y)$:

$$
I(x + u, y + v) \approx I(x, y) + I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v
$$

where $I_x = \partial I/\partial x$ and $I_y = \partial I/\partial y$ at point $(x, y)$.

**Step 3: Substitute the Taylor approximation into $E(u, v)$.**

The difference term becomes:

$$
I(x + u, y + v) - I(x, y) \approx I_x u + I_y v
$$

Substituting:

$$
E(u, v) \approx \sum_{W} (I_x u + I_y v)^2
$$

**Step 4: Expand the squared term.**

$$
(I_x u + I_y v)^2 = I_x^2 u^2 + 2 I_x I_y u v + I_y^2 v^2
$$

**Step 5: Distribute the sum (linearity of summation).**

$$
E(u, v) \approx \sum_{W} I_x^2 \cdot u^2 + 2 \sum_{W} I_x I_y \cdot u v + \sum_{W} I_y^2 \cdot v^2
$$

Since $u$ and $v$ are constants with respect to the summation:

$$
E(u, v) \approx u^2 \sum_{W} I_x^2 + 2 u v \sum_{W} I_x I_y + v^2 \sum_{W} I_y^2
$$

**Step 6: Define the structure tensor entries.**

Let:

$$
A = \sum_{W} I_x^2, \quad B = \sum_{W} I_x I_y, \quad C = \sum_{W} I_y^2
$$

So:

$$
E(u, v) \approx A u^2 + 2 B u v + C v^2
$$

**Step 7: Write in matrix quadratic form.**

$$
E(u, v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & C \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8: Incorporate Gaussian weighting for noise robustness.**

Replace hard window sums with Gaussian-weighted sums:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} G_\sigma * I_x^2 & G_\sigma * I_x I_y \\ G_\sigma * I_x I_y & G_\sigma * I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{E(u, v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the cornerstone result.** The local intensity variation $E(u, v)$ is a *quadratic function* of the displacement $(u, v)$, controlled by the $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{M}$. The eigenvectors of $\mathbf{M}$ point along the principal gradient directions, and the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ give the magnitude of intensity change in those directions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Harris Response Function

**Step 1: Recall the eigenvalue properties.**

For a $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix $\mathbf{M}$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$:

$$
\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2
$$

$$
\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2
$$

**Step 2: Compute these for the structure tensor.**

$$
\det(\mathbf{M}) = \langle I_x^2 \rangle \langle I_y^2 \rangle - \langle I_x I_y \rangle^2
$$

$$
\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \langle I_x^2 \rangle + \langle I_y^2 \rangle
$$

**Step 3: Harris's idea — avoid explicit eigenvalue computation.**

Computing eigenvalues requires solving the characteristic equation:

$$
\lambda^2 - \mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) \lambda + \det(\mathbf{M}) = 0
$$

Harris proposed a response function that uses $\det$ and $\mathrm{trace}$ instead:

$$
R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \big(\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})\big)^2
$$

**Step 4: Verify that $R$ captures the eigenvalue geometry.**

Substituting $\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$ and $\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$:

$$
R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2
$$

**Case analysis:**

| Case | Eigenvalues | $R$ value | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Flat** | $\lambda_1 \approx 0, \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | $R \approx 0$ (slightly negative due to $-k \cdot 0^2$) | Reject |
| **Edge** | One large, one small (e.g., $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$) | $R < 0$ (dominated by $-k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$) | Reject |
| **Corner** | Both large ($\lambda_1 \sim \lambda_2 \gg 0$) | $R \gg 0$ | **Accept** ✓ |

**Step 5: Choose $k$ such that corners are reliably detected.**

Empirically, Harris and Stephens found $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ works best for natural images. This is the **only free parameter** in the algorithm.

**Final Harris response:**

$$
\boxed{R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Shi-Tomasi Response

**Step 1: Motivating idea.**

Shi and Tomasi (1994) observed that for tracking, the **smaller** eigenvalue $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ is the *more informative* indicator of corner-ness. They proposed:

$$
R_{ST} = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)
$$

**Step 2: Corner selection rule.**

A pixel $(x, y)$ is a corner if:

$$
R_{ST}(x, y) > \lambda_{min}
$$

where $\lambda_{min}$ is a user-defined threshold (often 0.001 or relative to $\max(R_{ST})$).

**Step 3: Closed-form expression.**

The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{M}$ are:

$$
\lambda_{1, 2} = \frac{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 - 4 \det(\mathbf{M})}}{2}
$$

So:

$$
\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) = \frac{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) - \sqrt{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 - 4 \det(\mathbf{M})}}{2}
$$

**Final Shi-Tomasi response:**

$$
\boxed{R_{ST} = \frac{(\langle I_x^2 \rangle + \langle I_y^2 \rangle) - \sqrt{(\langle I_x^2 \rangle - \langle I_y^2 \rangle)^2 + 4 \langle I_x I_y \rangle^2}}{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The expression under the square root is always non-negative because the discriminant of the quadratic characteristic equation equals $\mathrm{trace}^2 - 4\det$ which can be shown to be $\geq 0$ for any symmetric $2 \times 2$ positive semi-definite matrix.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practice Problem Setup:** Given a $3 \times 3$ image patch with a corner at the center, compute the Harris response $R$.

Suppose the image gradient at the central pixel is:

$$
I_x = 10, \quad I_y = 10
$$

For a single-pixel approximation (no window summation), the structure tensor is:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 100 \\ 100 & 100 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1: Compute determinant.**

$$
\det(\mathbf{M}) = (100)(100) - (100)(100) = 0
$$

**Step 2: Compute trace.**

$$
\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = 100 + 100 = 200
$$

**Step 3: Compute Harris response with $k = 0.05$.**

$$
R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 = 0 - 0.05 \times (200)^2 = -2000
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

$R < 0$ ⇒ this is a **degenerate** case where gradients are equal in magnitude and angle. In a realistic noisy image, the eigenvalues will differ slightly, and the response will become positive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Always include a Gaussian smoothing step in the structure tensor computation in actual exam questions. A single-pixel approximation gives singular (degenerate) results.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Pseudocode (Conceptual Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Code Inclusion:** The following is *conceptual pseudocode* in plain algorithmic form (not a programming language), as the KTU 2024 syllabus treats this as a *signal processing* topic.

```
ALGORITHM: Harris Corner Detection
─────────────────────────────────────
INPUT:  Grayscale image I(x, y), σ, k, threshold T
OUTPUT: Set of corner pixel coordinates

1.  Compute gradients:
       Ix  = ∂I/∂x   (using Sobel or Prewitt)
       Iy  = ∂I/∂y

2.  Compute products of gradients:
       Ixx = Ix · Ix
       Iyy = Iy · Iy
       Ixy = Ix · Iy

3.  Apply Gaussian smoothing (G_σ):
       M11 = G_σ * Ixx
       M22 = G_σ * Iyy
       M12 = G_σ * Ixy

4.  For every pixel (x, y), compute Harris response:
       R(x, y) = M11·M22 − M12² − k·(M11 + M22)²

5.  Thresholding:
       Mark (x, y) as a candidate corner if R(x, y) > T

6.  Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):
       In a local neighborhood (e.g., 3×3), keep only
       the pixel with the maximum R; suppress the rest.

7.  RETURN set of corner pixels.
─────────────────────────────────────
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Grayscale Image I x y"] --> B["Gradient Computation using Sobel Kernels"]
    B --> C["Partial Derivatives Ix and Iy"]
    C --> D["Squared and Cross Products Ixx Iyy Ixy"]
    D --> E["Gaussian Smoothing with kernel G sigma"]
    E --> F["Structure Tensor M at every pixel"]
    F --> G["Compute Harris Response R equals M11 M22 minus M12 squared minus k times trace squared"]
    G --> H{"R greater than threshold T"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Candidate Corner Pixel"]
    H -- No --> J["Discard Pixel"]
    I --> K["Non Maximum Suppression 3 by 3 Local Max"]
    K --> L["Final Detected Corner Set"]
    J --> M["Non Corner Set"]
    M --> N["Output Image with Corner Overlay"]
    L --> N
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define a corner in an image. How is it distinguished from an edge?**

#### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **corner** is a point in an image where the local intensity variation is significant in **more than one direction**. Formally, it is a point where the **structure tensor** (second-moment matrix) has **both eigenvalues large**.
>
> **Distinction (1.5 marks):** An **edge** is characterized by a strong intensity gradient in *only one* direction (one large eigenvalue, one small). A **corner** has strong gradients in *two or more* directions (both eigenvalues large). A corner is essentially the *intersection of two or more edges*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the role of the structure tensor $\mathbf{M}$ in corner detection? Write its expression.**

#### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> **Role (1.5 marks):** The structure tensor encodes the *local gradient distribution* of an image within a window. Its eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ describe the magnitude of intensity change along the two principal directions of the local neighborhood. Corner detection reduces to analyzing these eigenvalues.
>
> **Expression (1.5 marks):** 
> 
> $$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> where $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denotes Gaussian-weighted averaging over the local window $W$, and $I_x, I_y$ are the partial derivatives of the image intensity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts typically of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** (internal choice).
