### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Harris Corner Detection

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 2 Carry-Over, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Starting from the sum of squared differences (SSD) formulation $E(u, v)$, derive the structure tensor $\mathbf{M}$ using first-order Taylor approximation. Explain the role of Gaussian smoothing in the derivation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Harris corner response function** $R$ from the eigenvalues of the structure tensor. Show the three-case analysis (flat region, edge, corner) with appropriate diagrams. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define SSD between original and shifted windows:
> 
> $$E(u, v) = \sum_{(x, y) \in W} \Big[ I(x + u, y + v) - I(x, y) \Big]^2$$
> 
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply first-order Taylor expansion:
> 
> $$I(x + u, y + v) \approx I(x, y) + u \cdot I_x + v \cdot I_y$$
> 
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute and expand:
> 
> $$E(u, v) \approx \sum_{W} (u^2 I_x^2 + 2 u v I_x I_y + v^2 I_y^2)$$
> 
> **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Write in matrix quadratic form:
> 
> $$E(u, v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Define the structure tensor $\mathbf{M}$:
> 
> $$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> **Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Discuss Gaussian smoothing: replacing hard window sums with $G_\sigma$-weighted sums suppresses noise. $G_\sigma$ gives higher weights to pixels near the window center, producing a more stable and rotation-invariant $\mathbf{M}$. Final form:
> 
> $$\mathbf{M} = G_\sigma * \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** State that $\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric $\Rightarrow$ has real non-negative eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$.
> 
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Express determinant and trace:
> 
> $$\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2, \quad \mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$$
> 
> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** State Harris's response function (avoids explicit eigenvalue computation):
> 
> $$R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \big(\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})\big)^2 = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$$
> 
> where $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ is an empirical constant.
> 
> **Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Three-case analysis:
> 
> | Case | Eigenvalues | $R$ value | Classification |
> |------|-------------|-----------|----------------|
> | **Flat** | $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both small | $R \approx 0$ (slightly negative) | Reject |
> | **Edge** | $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | $R < 0$ (large negative due to $-k(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^2$) | Reject |
> | **Corner** | $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both large | $R \gg 0$ | **Accept** |
> 
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Final corner selection: pixels where $R > T$ (threshold) and which are local maxima of $R$ (non-maximum suppression) are marked as corners.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Shi-Tomasi and Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 2, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Derive the **Shi-Tomasi corner response function** from the structure tensor. Explain why it is considered more robust than the Harris detector. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare the Harris and Shi-Tomasi corner detection algorithms in a tabular form. Explain the role of **non-maximum suppression** in corner detection pipelines. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** The structure tensor $\mathbf{M}$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ given by the characteristic equation:
> 
> $$\lambda^2 - \mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) \lambda + \det(\mathbf{M}) = 0$$
> 
> **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Solving for $\lambda_{1,2}$:
> 
> $$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 - 4 \det(\mathbf{M})}}{2}$$
> 
> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Shi-Tomasi proposed to use the *smaller* eigenvalue directly as the response:
> 
> $$R_{ST} = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) = \frac{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) - \sqrt{\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 - 4 \det(\mathbf{M})}}{2}$$
> 
> **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Pixel is a corner if $R_{ST} > \lambda_{min}$ (threshold).
> 
> **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Robustness arguments:
> - Harris uses the empirical constant $k$ which requires tuning; Shi-Tomasi eliminates $k$ entirely.
> - $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ has a direct geometric interpretation (the *minimum* principal curvature of the local image surface).
> - Shi-Tomasi gives more uniformly distributed, higher-quality corners that are more stable under viewpoint and illumination changes — empirically proven (Shi-Tomasi 1994 paper).
> - Shi-Tomasi is preferred for **tracking** applications (e.g., Lucas-Kanade optical flow).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

> **Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**
> 
> | Property | Harris | Shi-Tomasi |
> |----------|--------|-----------|
> | Response function | $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot (\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}))^2$ | $R_{ST} = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ |
> | Free parameters | $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ | Threshold $\lambda_{min}$ |
> | Eigenvalue computation | Avoided (uses det, trace) | Required (explicit) |
> | Sensitivity to $k$ | Yes | No |
> | Quality of corners for tracking | Good | **Better** |
> | Computational cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
> | OpenCV function | `cornerHarris()` | `goodFeaturesToTrack()` |
> 
> **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Explanation (3.5 marks):**
> 
> After computing the response $R$ at every pixel and thresholding to get candidate corners, neighboring pixels often both exceed the threshold. **NMS** ensures that only the *local maximum* of $R$ in a small neighborhood (typically $3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$) is retained as a corner; all other pixels in that neighborhood are suppressed.
> 
> **Algorithm:**
> 1. For each candidate pixel $(x, y)$ with $R(x, y) > T$:
> 2. Compare $R(x, y)$ with all neighbors in a $W \times W$ window.
> 3. If $R(x, y)$ is the *strict maximum*, retain it; else, suppress it.
> 
> **Why NMS is essential:**
> - Prevents clustered / redundant corner detections.
> - Produces well-separated, distinct keypoints.
> - Reduces the number of features passed to downstream matchers (faster, more accurate matching).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $E(u, v)$ with a gradient magnitude** — $E(u, v)$ is the *change in SSD* under a displacement, not the gradient. The gradient appears only inside the Taylor expansion of $E(u, v)$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Gaussian smoothing** — Examiners specifically check whether you applied $G_\sigma$ to the structure tensor entries. Without smoothing, $\mathbf{M}$ is highly sensitive to noise and gives spurious corners.
> 
> 3. **Using the wrong sign convention for $R$** — Harris response is **positive for corners**, **negative for edges**, **near zero for flat regions**. Do not reverse these signs.
> 
> 4. **Hard-coding $k$ value incorrectly** — The standard range is $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$. If you write $k = 0.4$ or $k = 0.004$, expect a 0.5 to 1 mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the non-maximum suppression step** — NMS is a *mandatory* part of the corner detection pipeline. A response that does not include NMS is incomplete.
> 
> 6. **Confusing Harris with Canny** — Canny detects **edges** (single-direction gradient). Harris detects **corners** (multi-direction gradient). Do not interchange them.
> 
> 7. **Missing the connection between $\det(\mathbf{M})$, $\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})$ and eigenvalues** — Examiners often ask: *"Express $\det$ and $\mathrm{trace}$ in terms of eigenvalues."* Students forget: $\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$ and $\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$.
> 
> 8. **Neglecting to discuss eigenvalue-based case analysis** — The flat / edge / corner classification is what gives Harris its semantic meaning. Skipping this analysis loses at least 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Corner Definition:** Point with *two or more* significant intensity gradient directions.
- [ ] **Edge vs. Corner:** Edge = 1 direction; Corner = 2+ directions.
- [ ] **SSD Formulation:** $E(u, v) = \sum_W [I(x+u, y+v) - I(x, y)]^2$ — the foundation.
- [ ] **Taylor Approximation:** $I(x+u, y+v) \approx I + u I_x + v I_y$.
- [ ] **Quadratic Form:** $E(u, v) \approx [u\; v] \mathbf{M} [u;\; v]^T$.
- [ ] **Structure Tensor:** $\mathbf{M} = G_\sigma * \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$.
- [ ] **Properties of M:** Symmetric, $2 \times 2$, positive semi-definite, real eigenvalues.
- [ ] **Eigenvalue Geometry:** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \geq 0$; eigenvectors = principal gradient directions.
- [ ] **Three Cases:** (i) Both small → flat; (ii) One large, one small → edge; (iii) Both large → **corner**.
- [ ] **Harris Response:** $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$, $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$.
- [ ] **$\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 = \langle I_x^2 \rangle \langle I_y^2 \rangle - \langle I_x I_y \rangle^2$**.
- [ ] **$\mathrm{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = \langle I_x^2 \rangle + \langle I_y^2 \rangle$**.
- [ ] **Shi-Tomasi Response:** $R_{ST} = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) > \lambda_{min}$ — preferred for tracking.
- [ ] **Gaussian Smoothing:** $G_\sigma$ applied to $\mathbf{M}$ entries reduces noise sensitivity.
- [ ] **NMS (Non-Maximum Suppression):** Retain only local maxima of $R$ in a $3 \times 3$ window.
- [ ] **Thresholding:** $R > T$ for Harris; $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2) > T$ for Shi-Tomasi.
- [ ] **Moravec Detector:** Predecessor of Harris; uses minimum SSD in 4 directions (horizontal, vertical, two diagonals).
- [ ] **Harris vs. Shi-Tomasi:** Harris uses empirical $k$; Shi-Tomasi is parameter-free (except threshold) and more stable.
- [ ] **Practical Tip:** `cv2.goodFeaturesToTrack()` in OpenCV uses Shi-Tomasi; `cv2.cornerHarris()` uses Harris.
- [ ] **Application Domains:** SLAM, image stitching, object recognition, optical flow, AR/VR, medical imaging, autonomous vehicles.
- [ ] **Exam Mantra:** *SSD → Taylor → Quadratic Form → Structure Tensor → Eigenvalues → Response Function → Thresholding → NMS → Corners.*

---

**END OF NOTES — Corner Detection | Module 2 | PEEET754 Computer Vision | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harris Corner Detection Algorithm, Hough Transform - Line and Curve Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Harris Corner Detection Algorithm, Hough Transform - Line and Curve Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Harris Corner Detection — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harris Corner Detection (Harris & Stephens, 1988)** is a **rotation-invariant**, **scale-variant** feature detection algorithm that mathematically identifies interest points in a digital image where the local neighbourhood exhibits significant intensity variation in **more than one dominant direction**. It is formally derived from the **second-moment matrix** (also called the **structure tensor** or **auto-correlation matrix**).

Mathematically, a **corner** is defined as a pixel location $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$ where a small displacement $[\Delta x, \Delta y]^T$ produces a **large change in image intensity** in *all* directions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Corner"?** A flat region has no change in any direction; an edge has no change along the edge direction; only a **corner** has intensity change along *every* direction — making it a uniquely identifiable, repeatable, and robust landmark.

### 1.2 Hough Transform — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hough Transform (Hough, 1962; Duda & Hart, 1972)** is a **global feature extraction technique** that maps a difficult **global pattern detection problem in image space** into a **localized peak detection problem in a parameter space** using a **voting (accumulator) procedure**. It is robust to **noise, occlusions, and partial shape information**, making it indispensable for detecting analytical curves such as **straight lines, circles, ellipses, and arbitrary parametric shapes**.

**Two canonical variants** are covered under the KTU 2024 syllabus:
1. **Hough Line Transform** — Parametric form: $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$
2. **Hough Circle Transform** — Parametric form: $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Harris Corner Analogy — "The Crossroads Puzzle"**
Imagine you are a **postman in a small town** trying to find a memorable meeting spot. You stand at the **centre of a town square (flat region)**: looking in any direction, you see the *same* set of buildings — no visual change. Walk along the *middle of a road (edge)*: looking forward and backward, the scenery is unchanged, but looking left/right shows different buildings. Stand at a **T-junction or crossroads (corner)**: looking in *every* direction, the scene is different! That uniquely identifiable location is exactly what Harris detects — **pixels that look different in ALL directions** (a high "self-difference" score).

**Hough Transform Analogy — "The Concert Voting Booth"**
Suppose you are trying to find the **most popular time slot** for a concert. Each of 10,000 fans (edge points) holds a small piece of cardboard with their *guess* of the time. Instead of trusting one fan's opinion, you hand them a **ballot sheet (accumulator array)** divided into hourly cells, and each fan drops a pebble in their guessed cell. After all votes are cast, the **cell with the most pebbles** is the agreed-upon time. The Hough Transform does exactly this: each **edge pixel "votes"** for every possible line/circle that could pass through it, and the **parameters receiving maximum votes** win — yielding the most likely geometric shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Harris is a **local** detector (operates on small windows), whereas Hough is a **global** detector (uses all edge pixels simultaneously). The two are often used in **pipeline combination**: Harris finds interest points → nearby edges → Hough fits geometric primitives.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Harris detector constant $k$**: empirically tuned in the range $\mathbf{0.04 \leq k \leq 0.06}$ (commonly **$k = 0.04$ to $0.05$**).
- **Corner response $R$**: dimensionless scalar (the higher, the more "corner-like").
- **Hough line parameter $\rho$**: perpendicular distance from origin to line, measured in **pixels**.
- **Hough line parameter $\theta$**: angle of the perpendicular to the line, measured in **radians** (range typically $\mathbf{[0, \pi]}$).
- **Hough circle radius $r$**: measured in **pixels** (range determined by application).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Harris Corner Detection — Operational Decomposition

The algorithm proceeds in **6 logical stages**:

1. **Spatial Derivative Computation**
   Compute image gradients $I_x = \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}$ and $I_y = \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}$ using a **Sobel operator** (or simple finite differences).
   $$I_x \approx I * G_x, \quad I_y \approx I * G_y$$
   where $*$ denotes convolution and $G_x, G_y$ are Sobel kernels.

2. **Quadratic Product Formation**
   Compute the **squared and cross products** of the gradients:
   $$I_x^2, \quad I_y^2, \quad I_x I_y$$

3. **Gaussian Smoothing of Products**
   Apply a **Gaussian window** $W(u,v)$ to the products to provide **noise suppression and localization stability**:
   $$S(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} S_{xx} & S_{xy} \\ S_{xy} & S_{yy} \end{bmatrix} = \sum_{u,v} W(u,v) \cdot \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

4. **Eigenvalue Analysis (Theoretical Foundation)**
   The **second-moment matrix $M$** captures the local intensity structure. Its **eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$** describe intensity variation along two principal directions:
   - If both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are **small** $\Rightarrow$ **Flat region**.
   - If one eigenvalue is **large**, one is **small** $\Rightarrow$ **Edge**.
   - If **both eigenvalues are large** $\Rightarrow$ **Corner**.

5. **Corner Response Function**
   To avoid expensive eigenvalue decomposition, Harris uses the **determinant–trace relationship**:
   $$R = \det(M) - k \cdot \left[\operatorname{trace}(M)\right]^2$$
   where:
   - $\det(M) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 = S_{xx} S_{yy} - S_{xy}^2$
   - $\operatorname{trace}(M) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = S_{xx} + S_{yy}$

6. **Thresholding & Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**
   - Mark pixels where $R > T$ (threshold) as corner candidates.
   - Apply NMS in a local window to retain only **local maxima** — yielding **unique corner points**.

### 2.2 Hough Line Transform — Operational Decomposition

1. **Edge Detection Stage**
   Apply **Canny or Sobel edge detector** to obtain a binary edge map $E(x,y)$.

2. **Parametric Mapping**
   Convert the slope-intercept form $y = mx + c$ (problematic: vertical lines $\Rightarrow m \to \infty$) to the **normal form**:
   $$\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$$
   where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from origin to the line, and $\theta$ is the angle of the normal.

3. **Accumulator Initialization**
   Create a 2D accumulator $A(\rho, \theta)$ of dimensions $\rho_{max} \times \theta_{max}$ and zero-initialize.

4. **Voting Loop**
   For every edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$, iterate $\theta$ over discrete values and compute the corresponding $\rho$, incrementing the accumulator:
   $$A(\rho(\theta), \theta) \mathrel{+}= 1$$

5. **Peak Detection**
   Identify local maxima in $A(\rho, \theta)$ above a threshold — each peak corresponds to one detected line.

### 2.3 Hough Circle Transform — Operational Decomposition

A circle is parameterized as $(a, b, r)$ — 3 parameters — leading to a **3D accumulator** $A(a, b, r)$.

1. Apply edge detection.
2. For each edge pixel, iterate over a **range of radii** $r_{min} \leq r \leq r_{max}$.
3. For each $r$, compute candidate centres:
   $$a = x - r\cos\theta, \quad b = y - r\sin\theta$$
   (for each $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$) and increment $A(a, b, r)$.
4. Peaks in the 3D accumulator yield detected circles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Note:** The 3D accumulator makes circle detection memory-intensive. **Hough Gradient Method** (OpenCV implementation) restricts the search by using **gradient direction** at each edge pixel, reducing the vote to a single centre candidate per pixel.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Gradient | $I_x = \partial I / \partial x, \; I_y = \partial I / \partial y$ | Intensity/pixel | Edge orientation |
| 2 | Structure Tensor $M$ | $M = \sum W \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_xI_y \\ I_xI_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Pixel sums, dimensionless | Local structure |
| 3 | Corner Response $R$ | $R = \lambda_1\lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^2$ | $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ | Corner scoring |
| 4 | Compact Harris Form | $R = S_{xx}S_{yy} - S_{xy}^2 - k(S_{xx}+S_{yy})^2$ | Dimensionless | Direct computation |
| 5 | Region Classification | $R < 0$: edge, $R \approx 0$: flat, $R > T$: corner | Scalar | Decision rule |
| 6 | Line Normal Form | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | $\rho$ in pixels, $\theta$ in rad | Line parameterization |
| 7 | Hough Vote (Line) | $A(\rho,\theta) \mathrel{+}= 1$ | Discrete accumulator | Voting procedure |
| 8 | Circle Equation | $(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 = r^2$ | $a, b, r$ in pixels | 3D parameter space |
| 9 | Hough Vote (Circle) | $a = x - r\cos\theta,\; b = y - r\sin\theta$ | Pixel units | Centre estimation |
| 10 | Threshold Rule | $R(x,y) > T \Rightarrow \text{corner}$ | $T > 0$ user-set | Final selection |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Harris Corners:** Used in **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**, **panoramic image stitching (SURF/ORB precursors)**, **motion tracking in autonomous vehicles**, **augmented reality (AR) marker detection**, and **medical image registration**.
- **Hough Lines:** Applied in **lane detection (autonomous driving)**, **railway track inspection**, **document/OCR skew correction**, and **PCB defect detection**.
- **Hough Circles:** Used in **iris/pupil detection (biometric authentication)**, **coin/ball detection in robotics**, **traffic sign recognition (circular signs)**, and **astronomical object detection**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Harris Corner Response Function

**Starting Point — Intensity Change Function $E$:**

For a window centred at $(x,y)$, the **change in intensity** when shifted by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ is:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{(u,v) \in W} \bigl[ I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y) - I(u,v) \bigr]^2
$$

**Step 1 — Taylor Series Approximation:**

Expand $I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y)$ using a first-order Taylor expansion around $(u,v)$:

$$
I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y) \approx I(u,v) + I_x(u,v)\Delta x + I_y(u,v)\Delta y
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $E$:**

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) \approx \sum_{(u,v) \in W} \bigl[ I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y \bigr]^2
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Square:**

$$
\bigl[ I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y \bigr]^2 = I_x^2 (\Delta x)^2 + 2 I_x I_y \Delta x \Delta y + I_y^2 (\Delta y)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange as a Quadratic Form in $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$:**

Group the terms to extract the matrix:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) \approx \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix} \left( \sum_{W} \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \right) \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Second-Moment Matrix $M$:**

$$
M = \sum_{(u,v) \in W} W(u,v) \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{xx} & S_{xy} \\ S_{xy} & S_{yy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Eigenvalue Decomposition of $M$:**

$M$ is a real symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. Diagonalize it:

$$
M = R^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 \end{bmatrix} R
$$

Substituting back, the intensity change becomes:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \lambda_1 (\Delta x')^2 + \lambda_2 (\Delta y')^2
$$

where $\Delta x', \Delta y'$ are the shifts along the principal axes.

**Step 7 — Region Classification via Eigenvalues:**

| Case | Eigenvalues | Surface $E$ | Region Type |
|------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\lambda_1 \approx 0, \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Flat | **Flat region** |
| 2 | $\lambda_1 \gg 0, \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Ridge | **Edge** |
| 3 | $\lambda_1 \gg 0, \lambda_2 \gg 0$ | Bowl | **Corner** |

**Step 8 — Avoid Eigenvalue Computation via Determinant–Trace Trick:**

Since eigenvalue decomposition is computationally expensive, Harris proposed using the algebraic invariants:

$$
\det(M) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2, \quad \operatorname{trace}(M) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2
$$

and the **Harris corner response function**:

$$
R = \det(M) - k \cdot [\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2
$$

**Step 9 — Substitution to Get the Final Operational Form:**

$$
R = S_{xx} S_{yy} - S_{xy}^2 - k \cdot (S_{xx} + S_{yy})^2
$$

**Step 10 — Decision Rule:**

- $R > T$ (positive, large) $\Rightarrow$ **Corner**.
- $R < 0$ (negative, large magnitude) $\Rightarrow$ **Edge**.
- $\vert R \vert$ small $\Rightarrow$ **Flat region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the constant $k$?** The term $-k \cdot [\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2$ **subtracts the squared sum of eigenvalues**, suppressing the response for edges (one large eigenvalue inflates the trace term). Empirically, $k \approx 0.04$ to $0.06$ provides the best corner-vs-edge discrimination on natural images.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hough Line Parameterization

**Motivation:** The familiar form $y = mx + c$ fails for vertical lines ($m \to \infty$).

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:**

Consider a line $L$ in the image. Let $\rho$ be the **perpendicular (shortest) distance** from the origin to $L$, and $\theta$ the **angle of the perpendicular** with the positive $x$-axis.

**Step 2 — Projection of Point $(x,y)$ onto the Normal:**

A point $(x,y)$ lies on $L$ if and only if its projection onto the unit normal vector $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ equals $\rho$:

$$
x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho
$$

**Step 3 — Invertibility:**

For any image point $(x_0, y_0)$, the set of all $(\rho, \theta)$ pairs satisfying the equation forms a **sinusoidal curve** in the $(\rho, \theta)$ parameter space.

**Step 4 — Voting Interpretation:**

For an edge pixel $(x_0, y_0)$, iterate $\theta$ from $0$ to $\pi$; the corresponding $\rho$ values trace a **sine wave**. Each $(\rho, \theta)$ point on this curve receives one vote.

**Step 5 — Peak Detection:**

All edge pixels on the same image line produce sine waves that **intersect at a common $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ point** — the line's parameters. This intersection point is the **peak** in the accumulator.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Harris Response Calculation

**Given:** A $3 \times 3$ image patch with the following intensity values and precomputed gradients:

| Pixel | $I$ | $I_x$ | $I_y$ |
|-------|-----|-------|-------|
| (1,1) | 100 | 10 | 5 |
| (1,2) | 110 | 12 | 6 |
| (1,3) | 120 | 14 | 7 |
| (2,1) | 105 | 11 | 5 |
| (2,2) | 115 | 13 | 6 |
| (2,3) | 125 | 15 | 7 |
| (3,1) | 110 | 12 | 5 |
| (3,2) | 120 | 14 | 6 |
| (3,3) | 130 | 16 | 7 |

Assume a **uniform $3 \times 3$ window** $W(u,v) = 1/9$.

**Step 1 — Compute Quadratic Products:**

| $(u,v)$ | $I_x^2$ | $I_y^2$ | $I_x I_y$ |
|---------|---------|---------|-----------|
| (1,1) | 100 | 25 | 50 |
| (1,2) | 144 | 36 | 72 |
| (1,3) | 196 | 49 | 98 |
| (2,1) | 121 | 25 | 55 |
| (2,2) | 169 | 36 | 78 |
| (2,3) | 225 | 49 | 105 |
| (3,1) | 144 | 25 | 60 |
| (3,2) | 196 | 36 | 84 |
| (3,3) | 256 | 49 | 112 |
| **Sum** | **1551** | **330** | **714** |

**Step 2 — Apply Gaussian Weighting ($W = 1/9$):**

$$
S_{xx} = \frac{1551}{9} = 172.33
$$

$$
S_{yy} = \frac{330}{9} = 36.67
$$

$$
S_{xy} = \frac{714}{9} = 79.33
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Determinant and Trace:**

$$
\det(M) = S_{xx} S_{yy} - S_{xy}^2 = (172.33)(36.67) - (79.33)^2 = 6318.78 - 6293.25 = 25.53
$$

$$
\operatorname{trace}(M) = S_{xx} + S_{yy} = 172.33 + 36.67 = 209.00
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Harris Response (using $k = 0.04$):**

$$
R = \det(M) - k \cdot [\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2 = 25.53 - 0.04 \cdot (209.00)^2
$$

$$
R = 25.53 - 0.04 \cdot 43681 = 25.53 - 1747.24 = -1721.71
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since $R = -1721.71 \ll 0$, this patch is classified as an **EDGE** (not a corner) — the trace dominates because gradients vary strongly in one direction ($I_x$ large) but modestly in the other ($I_y$ small).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Hough Line Voting

**Given:** Three edge points: $P_1 = (1, 1)$, $P_2 = (2, 2)$, $P_3 = (3, 3)$. Quantize $\theta$ into values $\{0, \pi/4, \pi/2, 3\pi/4\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho$ for each point and each $\theta$ using $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$:**

| Point | $\theta = 0$ | $\theta = \pi/4$ | $\theta = \pi/2$ | $\theta = 3\pi/4$ |
|-------|--------------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| (1,1) | 1.00 | 1.41 | 1.00 | 0.00 |
| (2,2) | 2.00 | 2.83 | 2.00 | 0.00 |
| (3,3) | 3.00 | 4.24 | 3.00 | 0.00 |

**Step 2 — Construct Accumulator Array $A(\rho, \theta)$:**

| | $\theta = 0$ | $\theta = \pi/4$ | $\theta = \pi/2$ | $\theta = 3\pi/4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho = 0$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | **3** |
| $\rho = 1$ | **2** | 0 | **2** | 0 |
| $\rho = 1.41$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $\rho = 2$ | **2** | 0 | **2** | 0 |
| $\rho = 2.83$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $\rho = 3$ | **2** | 0 | **2** | 0 |
| $\rho = 4.24$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Peak Identification:**

The cell $A(0, 3\pi/4) = 3$ has the **maximum vote count**, indicating that the **diagonal line $y = x$** (in normal form: $\rho = 0$, $\theta = 3\pi/4$) is the dominant line through the three collinear points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The line $y = x$ has perpendicular distance $\rho = 0$ from origin (it passes through origin) and the normal angle $\theta = 3\pi/4$ (pointing into the second quadrant). The Hough Transform successfully recovered it from just 3 collinear edge points.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Harris Corner Detection — Algorithmic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Convert to Grayscale]
    B --> C[Apply Gaussian Smoothing]
    C --> D[Compute Gradients Ix and Iy via Sobel]
    D --> E[Form Products Ix^2, Iy^2, IxIy]
    E --> F[Apply Gaussian Windowing W to Products]
    F --> G[Build Structure Tensor M = Sxx Sxy / Sxy Syy]
    G --> H[Compute R = det M minus k times trace M squared]
    H --> I{R greater than Threshold T}
    I -- Yes --> J[Mark Pixel as Corner Candidate]
    I -- No --> K[Discard Pixel]
    J --> L[Apply Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    L --> M[Output Final Corner Set]
    K --> M
```

### 4.2 Hough Transform — Line Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Grayscale Conversion]
    B --> C[Edge Detection: Canny or Sobel]
    C --> D[Binary Edge Map E]
    D --> E[Initialize Accumulator A rho theta to zero]
    E --> F[For Each Edge Pixel xi yi]
    F --> G[Loop theta from 0 to pi]
    G --> H[Compute rho equals xi cos theta plus yi sin theta]
    H --> I[Increment A rho theta]
    I --> J{More theta values}
    J -- Yes --> G
    J -- No --> K{More edge pixels}
    K -- Yes --> F
    K -- No --> L[Threshold Accumulator A]
    L --> M[Identify Local Maxima as Lines]
    M --> N[Output Detected Lines with rho and theta]
```

### 4.3 Hough Transform — Circle Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Edge Detection]
    B --> C[Compute Gradient Direction per Edge Pixel]
    C --> D[For Each Radius r in r_min to r_max]
    D --> E[For Each Edge Pixel x y]
    E --> F[Compute Centre Candidates a and b along gradient]
    F --> G[Increment 3D Accumulator A a b r]
    G --> H{More Edge Pixels}
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- No --> I{More Radii}
    I -- Yes --> D
    I -- No --> J[Threshold 3D Accumulator]
    J --> K[Find Peaks: Output Circles with a b r]
```

### 4.4 Eigenvalue-Based Region Classification (Harris)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute M and Eigenvalues lambda1 lambda2] --> B{Both lambda1 and lambda2 are small}
    B -- Yes --> C[FLAT REGION: No interest point]
    B -- No --> D{One lambda large, other small}
    D -- Yes --> E[EDGE: Anisotropic variation]
    D -- No --> F{Both lambda1 and lambda2 large}
    F -- Yes --> G[CORNER: Strong interest point retained]
    F -- No --> H[Uncertain: Check R value]
```

### 4.5 Functional Block Architecture — Harris + Hough Integrated System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Image Input] --> B[Preprocessing Block]
    B --> B1[Noise Filtering]
    B1 --> B2[Grayscale Conversion]
    B2 --> C[Feature Detection Block]
    C --> C1[Harris Corner Detector]
    C1 --> C2[Interest Point Map]
    C2 --> D[Edge Detection Block]
    D --> D1[Sobel or Canny Operator]
    D1 --> D2[Binary Edge Map]
    D2 --> E[Shape Fitting Block]
    E --> E1[Hough Line Transform]
    E --> E2[Hough Circle Transform]
    E1 --> F1[Detected Lines: rho theta]
    E2 --> F2[Detected Circles: a b r]
    F1 --> G[Output: Annotated Image with Features]
    F2 --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a "corner" in the context of image feature detection. Explain how the Harris Corner Detection algorithm mathematically distinguishes a corner from a flat region and an edge using eigenvalues of the second-moment matrix.*

**Model Answer:**

A **corner** is an image point whose local neighbourhood exhibits **significant intensity variation in multiple directions**. In Harris detection, the **second-moment matrix** $M = \begin{bmatrix} S_{xx} & S_{xy} \\ S_{xy} & S_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$ is computed from the image gradients over a local window. Its **eigenvalues** $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ describe intensity variation along two principal directions.

- **Flat region:** $\lambda_1 \approx 0$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 0$ — both small, no dominant direction.
- **Edge:** $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2$ (or vice versa) — one large, one small.
- **Corner:** $\lambda_1 \gg 0$ and $\lambda_2 \gg 0$ — both large, change in every direction.

This is captured compactly by the response $R = \det(M) - k[\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2$: positive $R$ for corners, negative for edges, near-zero for flat regions.

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the slope-intercept form $y = mx + c$ unsuitable for the Hough Line Transform? How does the normal form $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ overcome this limitation? Mention the practical range of $\theta$.*

**Model Answer:**

The form $y = mx + c$ is unsuitable because **vertical lines have unbounded slope** ($m \to \infty$) and thus cannot be represented in a finite accumulator.

The **normal form** $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ parameterizes a line by the **perpendicular distance $\rho$ from origin** and the **angle $\theta$ of the normal**. This eliminates singularity issues — both $\rho$ and $\theta$ remain finite for all line orientations. The practical range is $\rho \in [-\rho_{max}, \rho_{max}]$ (where $\rho_{max} = \sqrt{W^2 + H^2}$ for an image of width $W$ and height $H$) and $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ (the full range covers every line orientation exactly once).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #1:** Confusing the trace and determinant in the Harris response — students often write $R = \operatorname{trace}(M) - k \cdot \det(M)$. The **correct** order is $R = \det(M) - k[\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2$. (**Lose up to 2 marks**)
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #2:** Forgetting to apply **Gaussian windowing** to the products $I_x^2, I_y^2, I_x I_y$ before forming $M$. (**Lose up to 1.5 marks**)
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #3:** In Hough Transform, students often forget that $\theta$ ranges from $0$ to $\pi$, not $0$ to $2\pi$, for the line transform. Writing wrong ranges costs marks.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #4:** In numerical problems, students forget to **square the trace** — writing $R = \det(M) - k \cdot \operatorname{trace}(M)$ instead of $R = \det(M) - k \cdot [\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2$.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #5:** In circle Hough, students confuse the **centre** $(a, b)$ with the **edge pixel** $(x, y)$. Always label clearly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a) Derive the Harris Corner Detection response function $R = \det(M) - k[\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2$ from the intensity change function $E(\Delta x, \Delta y)$. Explain the role of the constant $k$ and its typical range.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define the intensity change when the window is shifted by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{(u,v) \in W} \bigl[ I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y) - I(u,v) \bigr]^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply first-order Taylor expansion:

$$
I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y) \approx I(u,v) + I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into $E$ and expand the square:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) \approx \sum_{W} \bigl[ I_x^2 (\Delta x)^2 + 2 I_x I_y \Delta x \Delta y + I_y^2 (\Delta y)^2 \bigr]
$$

**Step 4:** Express as a quadratic form:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix} \underbrace{\left( \sum_W W \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \right)}_{M} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Diagonalize $M$ via eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \lambda_1 (\Delta x')^2 + \lambda_2 (\Delta y')^2
$$

**Step 6:** Use the identity that for any $2 \times 2$ matrix:
- $\det(M) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$
- $\operatorname{trace}(M) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$

**Step 7:** Form the corner response to avoid expensive eigenvalue computation:

$$
R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2 = \det(M) - k[\operatorname{trace}(M)]^2
$$

**Role of $k$:** The constant $k$ is the **empirical weighting factor** that controls the **sensitivity of corner vs. edge discrimination**. A larger $k$ reduces false corners but may miss real ones. The typical range is $\mathbf{0.04 \leq k \leq 0.06}$, with $k = 0.04$ being the most widely used default in textbooks and OpenCV.

---

**(b) Explain the complete Hough Line Transform algorithm with the normal parameterization. Given three edge pixels $(2, 4)$, $(3, 6)$, and $(4, 8)$ in a 2D image, demonstrate the Hough voting procedure for $\theta \in \{0, \pi/4, \pi/2\}$ and identify the dominant line.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hough Line Transform Algorithm:**

1. Apply edge detection (Canny/Sobel) to obtain a binary edge map.
2. Convert line representation from $y = mx + c$ (singular for vertical lines) to the **normal form** $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$.
3. Initialize a 2D accumulator $A(\rho, \theta)$ to zero with dimensions $\rho_{max} \times \theta_{max}$.
4. For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$, iterate $\theta$ in discrete steps; for each $\theta$, compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$ and increment $A(\rho, \theta)$.
5. Find local maxima in the accumulator; each peak corresponds to one detected line.

**Voting Procedure for Given Pixels:**

Using $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$:

| Pixel $(x,y)$ | $\theta = 0$: $\rho = x$ | $\theta = \pi/4$: $\rho = 0.707x + 0.707y$ | $\theta = \pi/2$: $\rho = y$ |
|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| $(2, 4)$ | $\rho = 2$ | $\rho = 1.414 + 2.828 = 4.243$ | $\rho = 4$ |
| $(3, 6)$ | $\rho = 3$ | $\rho = 2.121 + 4.243 = 6.364$ | $\rho = 6$ |
| $(4, 8)$ | $\rho = 4$ | $\rho = 2.828 + 5.657 = 8.485$ | $\rho = 8$ |

**Accumulator Table $A(\rho, \theta)$:**

| $\rho$ | $\theta = 0$ | $\theta = \pi/4$ | $\theta = \pi/2$ |
|--------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | **1 (peak tie)** | 0 | 1 |
| 4.243 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 6.364 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 8 | **1 (peak tie)** | 0 | 1 |
| 8.485 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Identification of Dominant Line:**

Observing the **column $\theta = \pi/2$**, the three points give distinct $\rho$ values: $4, 6, 8$ — each receiving **1 vote** but no peak concentration at a single $\rho$. Similarly for $\theta = 0$.

However, the three points are **collinear** (lying on the line $y = 2x$, since $\frac{4}{2} = \frac{6}{3} = \frac{8}{4} = 2$). The true peak should appear at **denser quantization**. The dominant line is $y = 2x$. Converting to normal form: $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ with $y = 2x \Rightarrow x\cos\theta + 2x\sin\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta + 2\sin\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \tan\theta = -0.5 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 2.678$ rad. This lies between $\pi/4$ and $\pi/2$ — **explaining why our coarse quantization missed the peak**. The lesson: **finer $\theta$ resolution yields better line recovery**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a) Describe the Hough Circle Transform algorithm. Explain why a 3D accumulator is required and how the Hough Gradient Method improves computational efficiency.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description:**

A circle is parameterized by three values: **centre coordinates $(a, b)$** and **radius $r$**, with the equation:

$$
(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2
$$

The Hough Circle Transform uses a **3D accumulator** $A(a, b, r)$ of dimensions $A_{max} \times B_{max} \times R_{max}$. The procedure is:

1. Apply edge detection to obtain a binary edge map.
2. Initialize $A(a, b, r) = 0$ for all cells.
3. For each edge pixel $(x, y)$ and for each candidate radius $r \in [r_{min}, r_{max}]$:
   - For each $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ in discrete steps:
     - Compute the corresponding centre: $a = x - r\cos\theta$, $b = y - r\sin\theta$.
     - Increment $A(a, b, r) \mathrel{+}= 1$.
4. Threshold the accumulator and find local maxima — each peak is a detected circle.

**Why 3D Accumulator?** Unlike a line (2 parameters $\rho, \theta$), a circle has 3 parameters $(a, b, r)$, so the parameter space is 3-dimensional and the accumulator must be 3D to capture votes across all parameter combinations.

**Hough Gradient Method (Efficiency Improvement):**

- Use the **gradient direction** at each edge pixel (computed via Sobel). The gradient direction points **radially** from the centre.
- For each edge pixel, instead of voting for all $(\theta, r)$ combinations, **only vote for centres along the gradient direction** at the specified radius. This reduces votes from $O(N_{edges} \times N_\theta \times N_r)$ to $O(N_{edges} \times N_r)$.
- After voting, **for each non-zero $(a, b)$ cell, increment the radius dimension** in proportion to the number of votes — collapsing 3D search into a 2D centre plane first.

**Result:** Memory reduced from $O(A \times B \times R)$ to $O(A \times B)$; computation reduced dramatically.

---

**(b) Compare Harris Corner Detection and Hough Transform with respect to: (i) type of features detected, (ii) invariance properties, (iii) computational complexity, (iv) typical use cases in a vision pipeline.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Harris Corner Detection** | **Hough Transform** |
|------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Feature Type** | **Local interest points** (corners/junctions). Pixel-level. | **Global geometric primitives** (lines, circles). Shape-level. |
| **(ii) Invariance** | **Rotation-invariant** (eigenvalues are invariant to rotation). **Not scale-invariant** (responds to same point at different scales differently — addressed by Harris-Laplace). | **Rotation-invariant** by construction (parameters $\rho, \theta$ cover all orientations). **Scale-invariant** if applied to appropriately scaled edge maps. **Partially illumination-invariant** (uses edges, not raw intensities). |
| **(iii) Computational Complexity** | $O(W \times H \times k^2)$ where $k$ is the window/gradient size. Efficient: single pass per pixel. | $O(N_{edges} \times N_\rho \times N_\theta)$ for lines; $O(N_{edges} \times N_\theta \times N_r)$ for circles. **Memory-intensive** for circles (3D accumulator). |
| **(iv) Pipeline Use Case** | **Early stage** — provides sparse, repeatable keypoints for matching, tracking, descriptor computation (input to SIFT/SURF/ORB-like systems), image registration, and motion estimation. | **Mid/late stage** — operates on edge maps to extract high-level shapes (lane lines, circles in traffic signs, iris/pupil boundaries), often using Harris corners as seed regions of interest to **localize the Hough search** and reduce computation. |

**Synergy in a Real Pipeline:** A modern vision system uses **Harris (or FAST/ORB) corners to localize regions of interest**, then **edge detection + Hough Transform to fit geometric primitives** within those regions — e.g., in an autonomous vehicle, Harris finds headlight candidates, Canny extracts headlight edges, and Hough Circle Transform verifies that they are circular.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- [x] **Harris Corner Detection** is built on the **second-moment matrix** $M = \sum W \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$.
- [x] Eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $M$ determine region type: **both small → flat**, **one large → edge**, **both large → corner**.
- [x] **Compact response**: $R = S_{xx}S_{yy} - S_{xy}^2 - k(S_{xx} + S_{yy})^2$.
- [x] **Empirical constant $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$** — controls edge vs. corner discrimination.
- [x] Harris is **rotation-invariant** but **NOT scale-invariant** (use Harris-Laplace for scale).
- [x] **Final steps**: Threshold $R > T$ → **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** to retain only local maxima.
- [x] **Hough Transform** converts a **global pattern detection problem in image space** into a **local peak detection problem in parameter space** using a **voting mechanism**.
- [x] **Line form**: $y = mx + c$ fails for vertical lines → use **normal form** $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$.
- [x] **Hough line parameter ranges**: $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, $\rho \in [-\rho_{max}, \rho_{max}]$ where $\rho_{max} = \sqrt{W^2 + H^2}$.
- [x] **Circle form**: $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$ — requires a **3D accumulator** $A(a, b, r)$.
- [x] **Hough Gradient Method** improves circle detection efficiency by restricting votes to the gradient direction.
- [x] **Algorithm pipeline synergy**: Harris → Edge Detection (Canny) → Hough Transform.
- [x] **Sensitivity considerations**: Finer $(\rho, \theta)$ quantization → better line recovery but higher memory/computation.
- [x] **Robustness**: Hough is robust to **noise, gaps, and partial occlusions** in edges — a key advantage over template matching.
- [x] **KTU favourite questions**: Derivation of $R$, numerical voting for Hough, comparison of Harris vs. Hough, region classification via eigenvalues.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape from X - Shape from Shading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shape from X - Shape from Shading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Shape from Shading (SfS)** is a classical computer vision technique that reconstructs the three-dimensional shape of an object from a single grayscale (intensity) image by exploiting the gradual variation of pixel brightness (shading) caused by the interaction between a known light source direction, a known surface reflectance model, and the local surface orientation.

Mathematically, SfS is formulated as the **inverse problem** of recovering the surface height function $Z(x, y)$ (or equivalently, the surface gradients $p = \partial Z/\partial x$ and $q = \partial Z/\partial y$) from the observed image intensity $I(x, y)$, subject to constraints imposed by the **Image Irradiance Equation (IIE)** and the surface reflectance model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine holding a smooth, matte-finished egg under a single desk lamp in a dark room. The egg is white and uniform in color, yet you can clearly perceive its 3D bulge purely from the **gradient of brightness** across its surface — brightest where the surface directly faces the light, gradually darkening toward the edges where the surface tilts away. Your brain effortlessly infers shape *without* stereo, motion, or texture cues. This is exactly the principle behind **Shape from Shading**: given a single static image, the spatial pattern of intensities carries enough information (under known lighting) to reconstruct the underlying 3D geometry.

Think of it this way:
- A **flat surface** uniformly lit produces a **flat gray** patch.
- A **curved surface** produces a **smooth intensity gradient** (smooth shading).
- **Edges** in shading correspond to changes in surface orientation.
- SfS works backward: from the *intensity gradient* → recover the *surface gradient*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Lambert's Cosine Law Constant**: For a perfectly matte (Lambertian) surface, reflected intensity is proportional to $\cos \theta_i$, where $\theta_i$ is the angle between the surface normal $\mathbf{n}$ and the light source direction $\mathbf{s}$.
- **Albedo ($\rho$)**: A scalar in the range $[0, 1]$ representing the fraction of incident light reflected by the surface. For a standard Lambertian object, **$\rho = 1$** (normalized white).
- **Foreshortening Factor**: $\cos \theta_v$, where $\theta_v$ is the angle between the viewer direction and the surface normal.
- **Surface Gradient Parameters**: $p = \partial Z/\partial x$ and $q = \partial Z/\partial y$ are dimensionless slopes (tangents) of the surface in $x$ and $y$ directions.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Highlight**
> KTU examiners almost always frame a Part A question asking: *"Define Shape from Shading and state the assumption on which it is based."* The single most important assumption is: **The light source direction and reflectance model are known a priori.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of SfS

The Shape from Shading pipeline operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Input Acquisition**: A single grayscale image $I(x, y)$ of a static object is captured.
2. **Assumed Knowledge (Priors)**:
   - Light source direction $\mathbf{s} = (s_x, s_y, s_z)$ — known.
   - Surface reflectance model (e.g., Lambertian) — known.
   - Albedo $\rho(x, y)$ — usually assumed uniform.
   - Camera is **orthographic** (parallel projection).
3. **Image Irradiance Equation (IIE)**: Relate observed intensity $I(x, y)$ to the surface normal $\mathbf{n}(x, y)$ via the reflectance map $R(p, q)$.
4. **Reflectance Map Computation**: For a given lighting and reflectance model, derive the closed-form expression $R(p, q)$.
5. **Normal Recovery**: Solve the nonlinear first-order PDE $I(x, y) = R(p, q)$ for the surface gradients $(p, q)$.
6. **Surface Reconstruction (Integration)**: Integrate the recovered gradient field $(p, q)$ to obtain $Z(x, y)$:
   $$Z(x, y) = \int p \, dx + \int q \, dy + C$$
   where $C$ is a constant determined by boundary conditions.
7. **3D Reconstruction**: Output the height map $Z(x, y)$ representing the recovered 3D surface.

## 2.2 The Surface Gradient Parameterization

For a surface $Z = f(x, y)$, the normal vector is:
$$\mathbf{n} = (-p, -q, 1)$$

where
$$p = \frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}, \quad q = \frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}$$

The unit normal is:
$$\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \frac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$$

This unit normal is the fundamental quantity linking image intensity to surface geometry.

## 2.3 The Image Irradiance Equation (IIE)

The core equation of SfS is the **Image Irradiance Equation**, which under orthographic projection and Lambertian reflectance becomes:
$$I(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{(1 + p \cdot s_x + q \cdot s_y)}{s_z \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

where:
- $I(x, y)$ is the observed intensity at pixel $(x, y)$.
- $\rho$ is the albedo.
- $(s_x, s_y, s_z)$ is the unit light source direction.
- $(p, q)$ are the unknown surface gradients.

> [!NOTE] **Why "Irradiance" not "Radiance"?**
> "Irradiance" $E$ refers to the power of light falling *on* the image sensor per unit area. Under the assumption of a perfectly diffuse scene and calibrated optics, image intensity is proportional to scene irradiance. This subtle distinction matters in rigorous formulations.

## 2.4 Reflectance Map $R(p, q)$

The **Reflectance Map** is the function that maps the surface orientation (encoded by $(p, q)$) to the expected image intensity for a known light source and reflectance model. It is defined as:
$$R(p, q) = \frac{1 + p \cdot s_x + q \cdot s_y}{\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

The fundamental SfS equation then becomes:
$$I(x, y) = R(p, q)$$

This is a **nonlinear first-order Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** in the unknown surface $Z(x, y)$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Formula Table** (Memorize this for the exam!)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{n} = (-p, -q, 1)$ | Surface normal vector | Expressing normal from gradients |
| 2 | $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \dfrac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$ | Unit normal | Lambertian reflectance |
| 3 | $I = \rho \, (\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{s}})$ | Lambertian IIE | Core SfS equation |
| 4 | $I = \rho \dfrac{1 + p s_x + q s_y}{s_z \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$ | Explicit Lambertian SfS PDE | Solving for $(p, q)$ |
| 5 | $R(p, q) = \dfrac{1 + p s_x + q s_y}{\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$ | Reflectance map | Image-to-orientation mapping |
| 6 | $Z(x, y) = \iint (\partial Z/\partial x) \, dx + (\partial Z/\partial y) \, dy$ | Surface from gradients | Final reconstruction |
| 7 | $\cos \theta_i = \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{s}}$ | Angle of incidence | Lambert's cosine law |
| 8 | $E = I(x, y)$ | Irradiance equals intensity | Orthographic camera model |
| 9 | $\nabla^2 Z = 0$ | Smoothness constraint (Laplace) | Regularization in variational SfS |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} = \mu_I / \sigma_I$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Image quality metric for SfS input |

> **Note on notation in tables:** The vertical bar operator (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value) is replaced with safer LaTeX spacing or bracketed forms to keep the table grid intact.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Inspection**: Detecting dents and surface defects on shiny automotive panels.
- **Medical Imaging**: Recovering the 3D shape of skin lesions, teeth, or retinal surfaces from single endoscopic/microscope images.
- **Cultural Heritage Preservation**: 3D scanning of sculptures and pottery from archival photographs.
- **Satellite/Aerial Imaging**: Estimating terrain elevation from a single aerial photograph with known sun angle.
- **Robotics**: Real-time 3D perception for navigation when stereo rigs are unavailable.
- **Forensics & Archaeology**: Reconstructing 3D evidence or artifacts from a single photograph.

## 2.7 Why is SfS an Ill-Posed Problem?

The equation $I = R(p, q)$ has **one equation with two unknowns** $(p, q)$ at every pixel. This is the fundamental ambiguity. To resolve it, **additional constraints** are introduced:

- **Brightness Constraint**: $I = R(p, q)$ (the original PDE).
- **Smoothness Constraint**: $\vert \nabla^2 Z \vert^2$ is minimized (encourages locally smooth surfaces).
- **Gradient Constraint**: Locally integrable gradients.
- **Boundary Conditions**: Known $Z$ or known normal at silhouette pixels.
- **Integrability Constraint**: $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial x}$.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Mistake**
> Students often write "SfS has a unique solution." This is **FALSE**. SfS is mathematically ill-posed — uniqueness is not guaranteed. The result depends heavily on initial conditions and chosen constraints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Image Irradiance Equation from Lambert's Cosine Law

### Goal
Derive the explicit SfS PDE $I = R(p, q)$ for a Lambertian surface under orthographic projection.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State Lambert's Cosine Law**

The radiant intensity (luminance) $L$ reflected from a Lambertian surface is:
$$L = \frac{\rho}{\pi} E_0 \cos \theta_i$$

where $E_0$ is the irradiance falling on the surface and $\theta_i$ is the angle between the surface normal and the light source direction.

**Step 2 — Express $\cos \theta_i$ as a dot product**

$$\cos \theta_i = \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{s}}{\vert \mathbf{n} \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{s} \vert}$$

For a **unit** light source $\hat{\mathbf{s}}$ and a **unit** normal $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$:
$$\cos \theta_i = \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the unit normal in terms of $(p, q)$**

$$\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \frac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$$

Let the unit light source direction be:
$$\hat{\mathbf{s}} = (s_x, s_y, s_z)$$

Then:
$$\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{s}} = \frac{-p s_x - q s_y + s_z}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$$

**Step 4 — Set up the image irradiance equation**

Under orthographic projection, image intensity $I(x, y)$ is proportional to scene radiance $L$. With the foreshortening factor $\cos \theta_v = \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{v}}$ (where $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = (0, 0, 1)$ for an orthographic camera looking down the $z$-axis), the image intensity becomes:
$$I(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{(\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{s}}) \cdot (\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{v}})}{s_z}$$

(Here, dividing by $s_z$ normalizes the light direction so that a surface perpendicular to the light gives $I = \rho$.)

**Step 5 — Expand the foreshortening factor**

$$\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the two dot products**

$$I(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{(-p s_x - q s_y + s_z) \cdot 1}{s_z \cdot (p^2 + q^2 + 1)}$$

$$I(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{s_z - p s_x - q s_y}{s_z (p^2 + q^2 + 1)}$$

> **Note on sign convention:** Some textbooks (e.g., Horn & Sjoberg) use $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = (p, q, -1)$ to keep the light direction convention simpler. With that convention, the standard KTU form becomes:

$$\boxed{\,I(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{1 + p \, s_x + q \, s_y}{s_z \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}\,}$$

This is the **canonical form** expected in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Horn's Variational Formulation

### Goal
Convert the SfS problem into an energy-minimization framework that can be solved iteratively.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the energy functional**

The total energy $E$ is composed of three terms:
$$E = \iint_{\Omega} \left[ \underbrace{(I(x, y) - R(p, q))^2}_{\text{Brightness term}} + \lambda \underbrace{(p_x^2 + p_y^2 + q_x^2 + q_y^2)}_{\text{Smoothness term}} + \mu \underbrace{(\epsilon_{xy} - \epsilon_{yx})}_{\text{Integrability term}} \right] dx \, dy$$

where:
- $p_x = \partial p/\partial x$, $p_y = \partial p/\partial y$, $q_x = \partial q/\partial x$, $q_y = \partial q/\partial y$.
- $\epsilon_{xy} = \partial p / \partial y$, $\epsilon_{yx} = \partial q / \partial x$.
- $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are weighting coefficients.

**Step 2 — Derive the Euler-Lagrange equations**

Setting the functional derivatives $\partial E / \partial Z = 0$ and applying the calculus of variations yields:

$$I - R(p, q) = 0$$

$$\nabla^2 p + \nabla^2 q = 0$$

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial p}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial q}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Iterative update scheme**

The classical solution uses a discrete iterative scheme:

$$Z^{(k+1)}_{i,j} = \bar{Z}^{(k)}_{i,j} - \frac{\lambda \cdot \left(I_{i,j} - R(p^{(k)}_{i,j}, q^{(k)}_{i,j})\right)}{\text{coefficient}}$$

where $\bar{Z}^{(k)}_{i,j}$ is the local average of the depth estimates at iteration $k$, and the bracketed term is the residual error. This is solved until convergence.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Eikonal Form of SfS

### Goal
Show that the SfS PDE reduces to an **Eikonal-type equation** under specific assumptions.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Specialize the IIE to a specific case**

Assume the light source is along the optical axis: $\hat{\mathbf{s}} = (0, 0, 1)$, and $\rho = 1$.

Then:
$$I = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the gradient magnitude**

$$\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2} = \frac{1}{I}$$

$$1 + p^2 + q^2 = \frac{1}{I^2}$$

$$p^2 + q^2 = \frac{1}{ I^2} - 1 = \frac{1 - I^2}{I^2}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Eikonal form**

The **Eikonal equation** is a first-order PDE of the form:
$$\vert \nabla u \vert = F(x, y), \quad F > 0$$

Here:
$$\vert \nabla Z \vert = \sqrt{p^2 + q^2} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - I^2}}{I}$$

This is exactly an Eikonal equation in the unknown $Z$, with speed function $F(x, y) = \sqrt{1 - I^2} / I$. It can be solved efficiently using the **Fast Marching Method (FMM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exam Tip**
> If a KTU question asks *"Show that the SfS problem can be reduced to an Eikonal equation,"* the key steps are: (1) Set $\hat{\mathbf{s}} = (0, 0, 1)$, (2) Rearrange for $p^2 + q^2$, (3) Identify $\vert \nabla Z \vert = F(I)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SfS Solution Methods

| Method | Type | Strengths | Weaknesses | Computational Cost |
|--------|------|-----------|------------|--------------------|
| **Propagation / Characteristic Strip** | PDE | Mathematically rigorous; well-posed with boundary conditions | Sensitive to noise; complex implementation | High |
| **Horn's Variational** | Optimization | Globally smooth results; handles missing data | Slow convergence; parameter tuning | High |
| **Lee & Kuo's Discrete Approach** | Discrete | Robust; can use look-up tables for $R(p, q)$ | Requires dense initialization | Medium |
| **Eikonal / Fast Marching** | PDE (geometric) | Fast; $O(N \log N)$; globally consistent | Requires monotonicity of $F$ | Low–Medium |
| **Linear Approximation (Pentland)** | Linearization | Closed-form solution; very fast | Approximation error for steep slopes | Low |
| **Photometric Stereo** | Multi-image | Exact, well-posed (over-determined) | Requires ≥3 images + known light directions | Medium |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Trend**
> A common 14-mark question in KTU ESE asks: *"Compare Shape from Shading and Photometric Stereo."* Memorize the above matrix — it covers the 7 marks of the comparison sub-part perfectly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SfS Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the full functional flow of the Shape from Shading process — from image acquisition to final 3D reconstruction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Grayscale Image I x y] --> B[Intensity Normalization: I in 0 to 1]
    B --> C{Lighting and Reflectance Model Known?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Define Light Source Vector s and Albedo rho]
    C -- No --> D1[STOP: SfS Not Applicable]
    D --> E[Compute Reflectance Map R p q]
    E --> F[Apply Image Irradiance Equation: I equals R p q]
    F --> G{Choose Solution Strategy}
    G -- Strategy 1 --> H1[Variational Method: Horn's Energy Minimization]
    G -- Strategy 2 --> H2[Eikonal Form: Fast Marching Method]
    G -- Strategy 3 --> H3[Discrete Approach: Lee and Kuo Look-up Tables]
    H1 --> I[Recover Gradient Field p x y and q x y]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Apply Integrability Constraint]
    J --> K[Integrate Gradients to Obtain Height Map Z x y]
    K --> L[Output: 3D Surface Reconstruction]
    L --> M[Post-Processing: Mesh Generation and Visualization]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style B fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style D fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style E fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style I fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style K fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B
    style L fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style M fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: Reflectance Map Visualization

The following block diagram shows how the reflectance map $R(p, q)$ transforms the gradient space $(p, q)$ into image intensity values.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Gradient Space: p q] --> Q[Unit Normal Computation]
    Q --> R[Apply Lambert Cosine Law]
    S[Light Source: s_x s_y s_z] --> R
    T[Albedo: rho] --> R
    R --> U[Reflectance Map: R p q]
    U --> V[Image Intensity: I x y]
    V --> W[Surface Reconstruction: Z x y]

    style P fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style S fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00
    style T fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00
    style U fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F
    style W fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Horn's Iterative Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[Initialize: Z at 0 and p, q at 0] --> IterStart{Iteration k}
    IterStart --> CalcGrad[Compute p and q from Z_k]
    CalcGrad --> CalcR[Evaluate Reflectance Map R p q at each pixel]
    CalcR --> CalcResid[Compute Residual: I minus R]
    CalcResid --> Update[Update Z using local average and residual]
    Update --> Check{Convergence?}
    Check -- No --> IterStart
    Check -- Yes --> Output[Final Surface Z x y]

    style Init fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B
    style CalcR fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style Update fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style Output fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading Guide for Mermaid Diagrams**
> Each box is a processing stage, each arrow is a data dependency. Color coding follows: **Blue** = Input stages, **Orange** = Physical model / reflectance computation, **Green** = Gradient/normal recovery, **Pink/Purple** = Final surface output.
