I(x + \Delta x,\ y + \Delta y,\ t + \Delta t) &\approx I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \Delta x + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \Delta y + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute Back into the Constancy Equation.**

Substituting the Taylor expansion into the brightness constancy equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x, y, t) &= I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \Delta x + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \Delta y + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subtract $I(x, y, t)$ From Both Sides.**

The leading terms cancel exactly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \Delta x + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \Delta y + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Divide by $\Delta t$.**

Recognising the chain rule and the definitions of velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{dx}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} \\
v &= \frac{dy}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing the constraint by $\Delta t$ and taking the limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta t} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Optical Flow Constraint Equation.**

Using the shorthand $I_x = \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}$, $I_y = \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}$, $I_t = \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** — the differential form of the brightness constancy assumption.

### 3.2 Derivation of Normal Flow

The OFCE gives a single equation in two unknowns. It is informative to project this constraint along the gradient direction.

**Step 1.** Let $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ be the spatial gradient vector, and $\mathbf{v} = (u, v)$ be the velocity vector. The OFCE is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Define the unit normal vector to the iso-brightness contour:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{n} &= \frac{\nabla I}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = \frac{(I_x, I_y)}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Take the dot product of $\mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{n}$ and solve for the normal flow $u_\perp$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} &= -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert} \\
u_\perp &= -\frac{I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **normal flow** — the component of motion **along** the image gradient. It is the *only* component of motion recoverable from a single pixel.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** Consider a $3 \times 3$ patch of image intensities. Frame 1 at $t = 0$ is given by
>
> $I(\cdot, \cdot, 0) = \begin{pmatrix} 50 & 60 & 70 \\ 80 & 90 & 100 \\ 110 & 120 & 130 \end{pmatrix}$
>
> In Frame 2 at $t = 1$, the centre pixel has moved by 1 pixel to the right. Frame 2 values are
>
> $I(\cdot, \cdot, 1) = \begin{pmatrix} 55 & 65 & 75 \\ 85 & 95 & 105 \\ 115 & 125 & 135 \end{pmatrix}$
>
> Verify that the brightness constancy assumption holds, and compute the OFCE components at the centre pixel.

**Solution.**

Step 1 — Read the centre pixel brightness in both frames.

The centre pixel $(x, y) = (2, 2)$ (1-indexed) has $I(2, 2, 0) = 90$ and $I(2, 2, 1) = 95$.

Step 2 — Apply brightness constancy.

If the centre pixel moved by $(\Delta x, \Delta y) = (1, 0)$, brightness constancy would require $I(2, 2, 0) = I(3, 2, 1) = 105$. Since $90 \neq 105$, the assumption does **not** hold exactly. This is why the OFCE is formulated as a *first-order* differential approximation, not an exact equality for finite displacements.

Step 3 — Approximate gradients using central differences.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x &\approx \frac{I(3, 2, 0) - I(1, 2, 0)}{2} = \frac{110 - 80}{2} = 15 \\
I_y &\approx \frac{I(2, 3, 0) - I(2, 1, 0)}{2} = \frac{120 - 60}{2} = 30 \\
I_t &\approx I(2, 2, 1) - I(2, 2, 0) = 95 - 90 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the OFCE and normal flow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x u + I_y v + I_t &= 0 \\
15 u + 30 v + 5 &= 0 \\
3 u + 6 v + 1 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_\perp &= -\frac{I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}} = -\frac{5}{\sqrt{15^2 + 30^2}} = -\frac{5}{\sqrt{1125}} \approx -0.149\ \text{pixels/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

The single equation $3u + 6v + 1 = 0$ is one constraint in two unknowns — characteristic of the **aperture problem**.

### 3.4 The Aperture Problem — Why We Cannot Solve Directly

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The OFCE $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ provides **only one linear equation** for the **two unknown velocity components** $(u, v)$. This under-determined system means the true motion cannot be recovered from a single pixel. This is mathematically and intuitively known as the **Aperture Problem**.

**Why does this happen?**
A single pixel (or any small aperture) only "sees" intensity changes along its gradient direction. Motion along an iso-brightness contour produces **no intensity change** at all and is therefore invisible to a local measurement. We need either:
- **Spatial coherence (Lucas-Kanade)**: assume all pixels in a small window share the same $(u, v)$ → over-constrained system of equations.
- **Global smoothness (Horn-Schunck)**: minimise a global energy functional with a smoothness regulariser.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Pipeline of Brightness Constancy-Based Optical Flow

The following block diagram traces the data flow from raw image pairs to the optical flow constraint equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame I at time t] --> C[Brightness Constancy Assumption]
    B[Frame I at time t plus dt] --> C
    C --> D[First Order Taylor Expansion]
    D --> E[Subtract I from both sides]
    E --> F[Divide by dt and take limit]
    F --> G[Optical Flow Constraint Equation Ix u plus Iy v plus It equals 0]
    G --> H{Is aperture problem present?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Lucas Kanade Method]
    H -- Yes --> J[Horn Schunck Method]
    I --> K[Dense Optical Flow Output u comma v]
    J --> K
    G --> L[Normal Flow u perpendicular equals minus It over magnitude of gradient I]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation of the Aperture Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[True motion vector v equals u comma v] --> Q[Component along gradient n]
    P --> R[Component along iso brightness contour t]
    Q --> S[Normal flow u perpendicular OBSERVABLE]
    R --> T[Tangential flow UNOBSERVABLE aperture problem]
    S --> U[OFCE constraint satisfied]
    T --> V[OFCE cannot resolve this]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of the OFCE Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Image Sequence Input] --> B[Gradient Computation Module]
    B --> B1[Sobel filter for Ix]
    B --> B2[Sobel filter for Iy]
    A --> C[Frame Differencing Module]
    C --> C1[Temporal gradient It]
    B1 --> D[Matrix Assembly]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Linear System A v equals b]
    E --> F{Choose Solver}
    F --> G[Lucas Kanade Least Squares over local window]
    F --> H[Horn Schunck Variational with smoothness term]
    G --> I[Velocity Field Output u v]
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map of the Brightness Constancy Equation's Role

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pixel Tracking Problem] --> B[Brightness Constancy Assumption]
    B --> C[Optical Flow Constraint Equation]
    C --> D[Two unknowns one equation]
    D --> E[Aperture Problem]
    E --> F[Need additional constraints]
    F --> G[Lucas Kanade Local Smoothness]
    F --> H[Horn Schunck Global Smoothness]
    G --> I[Final Dense Flow]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State and explain the brightness constancy assumption used in optical flow estimation. Why is it essential for motion estimation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The brightness constancy assumption states that the intensity of a scene point remains unchanged as the point moves from one frame to the next: $I(x, y, t) = I(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y, t + \Delta t)$.
- **(1 mark)** This assumption is essential because the camera does not directly measure motion — it only measures pixel intensities. To recover motion from intensity changes, we need a model that links the two, and brightness constancy provides the simplest such model.
- **(1 mark)** The assumption also enables the use of first-order Taylor series expansion to derive a linear constraint $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$, from which velocity can be computed.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "aperture problem" in the context of optical flow and explain its mathematical origin.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The aperture problem is the inability to determine the true 2D motion of a pixel from local intensity measurements alone.
- **(1 mark)** Mathematically, the OFCE $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ is a single linear equation in two unknowns $(u, v)$, leaving the system under-determined.
- **(1 mark)** Only the motion component along the image gradient (normal flow) is observable. The tangential component along iso-brightness contours cannot be measured, leading to ambiguity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Starting from the brightness constancy assumption, derive the optical flow constraint equation $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a $2 \times 2$ image patch. Frame 1: $I(1,1,0) = 10$, $I(2,1,0) = 20$, $I(1,2,0) = 30$, $I(2,2,0) = 40$. Frame 2 is identical except $I(1,1,1) = 12$. Compute $I_x$, $I_y$, and $I_t$ at the centre, and write down the OFCE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(1 mark)** State the assumption: $I(x, y, t) = I(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y, t + \Delta t)$ and list the small-motion assumption.
- **(1 mark)** Apply first-order Taylor expansion to the RHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y, t + \Delta t) \approx I(x, y, t) + I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y + I_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Substitute and cancel $I(x, y, t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 = I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y + I_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Divide by $\Delta t$ and take the limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} + I_y \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta t} + I_t = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Define $u = dx/dt$ and $v = dy/dt$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Interpret: this is a single linear equation in two unknowns, hence the aperture problem.
- **(1 mark)** State that only normal flow $u_\perp = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ is observable.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

- **(1 mark)** Use central differences to compute spatial gradients.
- **(1 mark)** $I_x \approx \dfrac{20 - 10}{1} = 10$ (using forward difference between left and centre).
- **(1 mark)** $I_y \approx \dfrac{30 - 10}{1} = 20$.
- **(1 mark)** $I_t = I(1,1,1) - I(1,1,0) = 12 - 10 = 2$.
- **(1 mark)** Form the OFCE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 u + 20 v + 2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Simplify to $5u + 10v + 1 = 0$.
- **(1 mark)** State normal flow magnitude: $u_\perp = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{10^2 + 20^2}} = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{500}} = -\dfrac{1}{5\sqrt{5}} \approx -0.089$ pixels/second.

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Style]`

**(a)** Explain the geometric meaning of the optical flow constraint equation. Define normal flow and show that it represents only the motion component along the image gradient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A camera observes a uniformly bright white ball moving across a black background. The ball's edge pixel has $I_x = 50$, $I_y = 0$, $I_t = -10$. Compute the normal flow and the full motion vector if it is known that the ball moves purely horizontally. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(1 mark)** Rewrite OFCE in vector form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** The dot product of the gradient $\nabla I$ and the velocity $\mathbf{v}$ equals the negative temporal gradient.
- **(1 mark)** This means only the projection of $\mathbf{v}$ onto $\nabla I$ is constrained; the perpendicular component is free.
- **(1 mark)** Define unit normal $\mathbf{n} = \nabla I / \vert \nabla I \vert$.
- **(1 mark)** Project $\mathbf{v}$ onto $\mathbf{n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} = u_\perp = -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Normal flow is therefore the component of motion along the gradient direction (perpendicular to iso-brightness contours).
- **(1 mark)** Mention the aperture problem: the tangential flow $v_\parallel$ along iso-brightness contours cannot be recovered locally.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

- **(1 mark)** Compute the gradient magnitude:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{50^2 + 0^2} = 50
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Compute normal flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_\perp = -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = -\frac{-10}{50} = 0.2\ \text{pixels/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Since $I_y = 0$, the gradient is purely horizontal. Normal flow is therefore horizontal motion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u = 0.2\ \text{pixels/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** Verify with OFCE: $50 \cdot 0.2 + 0 \cdot v + (-10) = 10 - 10 = 0$. ✓
- **(1 mark)** Purely horizontal motion implies $v = 0$ pixels/second.
- **(1 mark)** Full motion vector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v} = (u, v) = (0.2, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** State the direction: the ball moves to the right at **0.2 pixels/second** (the positive sign on $u$ indicates rightward motion in standard image coordinates where $x$ increases to the right).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Optical Flow / Brightness Constancy Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the small-motion assumption** when writing the Taylor expansion → 1 mark lost. Always state that the first-order approximation is valid only when $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta t$ are small.
> 2. **Not cancelling $I(x, y, t)$** explicitly in the derivation → 1 mark lost. The KTU 2024 marking scheme rewards showing the cancellation step.
> 3. **Confusing $I_t$ (temporal gradient) with $\Delta t$ (time interval)**. These are completely different quantities.
> 4. **Skipping the geometric interpretation of the OFCE** in 7-mark questions. Always mention that the equation constrains only the *normal component* of motion.
> 5. **Not defining the aperture problem** explicitly. Even in derivation questions, end with a one-line statement that the OFCE is under-determined.
> 6. **Mixing up $u_\perp$ sign conventions.** Some textbooks define normal flow with the opposite sign. Stick to the standard: $u_\perp = -I_t / \vert \nabla I \vert$.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems. Always mention **pixels/second** for $(u, v)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Brightness Constancy Assumption**: $I(x, y, t) = I(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y, t + \Delta t)$ — intensity of a moving point is preserved across frames.
- **Three required assumptions**: (1) brightness constancy, (2) small motion, (3) spatial coherence.
- **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)**: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ — derived using first-order Taylor expansion.
- **Velocity definitions**: $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$ — both in **pixels/second**.
- **Vector form**: $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0$.
- **Normal flow**: $u_\perp = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ — only motion component along the image gradient is observable from a single pixel.
- **Aperture Problem**: the OFCE gives **one equation** for **two unknowns**; tangential motion is invisible locally.
- **Resolution methods**: Lucas-Kanade (local least-squares) and Horn-Schunck (global variational smoothness) — *not* part of this topic but build on BCE.
- **Practical applications**: autonomous driving, video stabilization, action recognition, robot navigation, medical imaging (cardiac motion tracking).
- **Units to remember**: gradients in **intensity/pixel**, velocities in **pixels/second**, $I_t$ in **intensity/second**.
- **Sign convention**: in image coordinates, $+x$ is right, $+y$ is down; positive $u$ means rightward motion.
- **KTU 2024 weightage**: this is a **high-yield 14-mark derivation topic** — practice the Taylor expansion step **multiple times** before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aperture Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aperture Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Aperture Problem** is a fundamental ambiguity in computer vision that arises when an observer (or a local image operator) attempts to determine the true motion of a visual pattern through a small, localized viewing window (the "aperture"). Within a small local neighborhood, the true 2D motion vector of a feature cannot be uniquely recovered — only its component **perpendicular (normal) to the local image gradient** can be measured. The component **parallel to an edge/contour** remains locally invisible.

Mathematically, the optical flow constraint equation provides only **one scalar equation** for **two unknown velocity components** $(u, v)$, leading to an underdetermined system at every single pixel — this is the mathematical core of the aperture problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEET754, Module 3):**
> The Aperture Problem is a critical prerequisite for understanding **Optical Flow**, **Lucas-Kanade**, and **Horn-Schunck** estimators. Examiners frequently test this as a 3-mark Part-A question or as a sub-part of a 14-mark Part-B question on motion estimation.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are looking at the world through a small peephole (your "aperture") in a door. Through this tiny circular hole, you can only see a small patch of a moving object — for instance, a **straight horizontal line** moving across your field of view.

- If the line is moving **rightward** horizontally, you see the line shifting right — visible.
- If the line is moving **leftward** horizontally, you see it shifting left — also visible.
- But if the line is moving **along its own length** (say, the right half slides to the right while the left half stays put, i.e., **stretching**), your peephole cannot detect this change because every point along the line looks identical to the line itself.

Through a local aperture, a straight edge looks the same no matter how it slides *along* itself. You can only measure motion *across* (perpendicular to) the edge. This is the essence of the aperture problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Demonstration — The "Barber Pole" Illusion:**
> A rotating barber pole with diagonal stripes gives the **percept** of vertical motion (stripes appearing to scroll upward), while the actual physical motion of the surface is **horizontal**. The visual system of the brain (and any local computer vision operator) is biased by the normal flow component — exactly the aperture problem in action.

## Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

- **Normal Flow** ($u_n$): The measurable component of motion along the image intensity gradient.
- **Tangential Flow** ($u_t$): The unobservable component of motion parallel to an edge — the "invisible" part.
- **Aperture size**: Typically a small window (e.g., **3×3**, **5×5**, or **7×7 pixels**) — the smaller the window, the worse the aperture problem.
- **Intensity Gradient Magnitude** $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$: must be non-zero for any flow to be measurable (i.e., a uniform region has zero normal flow — completely unobservable motion).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: The Brightness Constancy Assumption
The foundation of optical flow is the **Brightness Constancy Assumption**, which states that the intensity of a pixel remains constant as it moves through the image over a small time interval $\Delta t$:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t, y + v\Delta t, t + \Delta t)$$

Here $(u, v) = \left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}, \dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)$ is the unknown 2D velocity vector we wish to recover.

### Step 2: First-Order Taylor Expansion
Performing a first-order Taylor expansion of the right-hand side around the point $(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x+u\Delta t, y+v\Delta t, t+\Delta t) \approx I(x,y,t) + I_x \cdot u\Delta t + I_y \cdot v\Delta t + I_t \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 3: The Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)
Subtracting $I(x, y, t)$ from both sides and dividing by $\Delta t$:

$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0$$

This is the famous **Optical Flow Constraint Equation** — a single linear equation in two unknowns. This underdetermined nature is the **mathematical manifestation of the aperture problem**.

### Step 4: The "Why" — Why This Is Underdetermined
At a single pixel, we have **1 equation** and **2 unknowns** $(u, v)$. Geometrically, the OFCE defines a **line** in velocity space $(u, v)$ — every point on this line is a valid solution. The aperture problem is therefore the geometric ambiguity of a 1D line in a 2D space.

### Step 5: The Normal Flow Solution
The component of $(u, v)$ along the image gradient direction $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ is uniquely determined. Projecting the unknown flow onto the unit gradient:

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla I}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = \frac{(I_x, I_y)}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$$

The **normal flow** $u_n$ is then:

$$u_n = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$$

This is the **only** component a local aperture can measure.

### Step 6: Resolving the Ambiguity — How Do We Get the Full Flow?
Since the normal flow is insufficient, real algorithms impose **additional constraints**:

1. **Lucas-Kanade (Local Constant Flow Assumption):** Assume $(u, v)$ is constant over a small window of $n$ pixels. This converts 1 equation × $n$ pixels into an $n \times 2$ overdetermined system solved by least squares:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_x(p_1) & I_y(p_1) \\ I_x(p_2) & I_y(p_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(p_n) & I_y(p_n) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} I_t(p_1) \\ I_t(p_2) \\ \vdots \\ I_t(p_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

   Solved as: $(u, v)^T = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b$ — but **only when $A^T A$ is invertible** (i.e., the window contains gradients in ≥ 2 directions, like at a **corner**). On a flat edge, $A^T A$ becomes rank-deficient — still suffering the aperture problem!

2. **Horn-Schunck (Global Smoothness):** Imposes a global regularization penalty $\iint (\nabla u)^2 + (\nabla v)^2 \, dx\, dy$ to enforce smooth flow fields, resolving ambiguity globally.

3. **Feature-Based Methods:** Detect distinctive keypoints (corners via Harris, SIFT, ORB) where the aperture problem does not apply because the local intensity structure breaks the 1D ambiguity.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (LiDAR + Camera Fusion):** Stereo/optical flow from cameras is ambiguous on lane markings (long edges) — engineers use corner-rich feature detectors (ORB, SuperPoint) and IMU fusion.
- **Robotics & SLAM:** ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono, and similar pipelines deliberately avoid tracking along lines because of the aperture problem.
- **Video Stabilization & Slow-Motion Interpolation:** Multi-frame motion estimation relies on corner features for the same reason.
- **Medical Imaging:** Tracking cellular motion through microscopes requires handling the aperture problem explicitly for elongated structures (axons, blood vessels).

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning / When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Brightness Constancy | $I(x,y,t) = I(x+u\Delta t, y+v\Delta t, t+\Delta t)$ | Foundation assumption for all optical flow methods |
| Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE) | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | One equation, two unknowns — **the aperture problem** |
| Image Gradient Vector | $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ | Direction of maximum intensity change |
| Gradient Magnitude | $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | Used to compute normal flow |
| Unit Normal to Edge | $\mathbf{n} = \dfrac{(I_x, I_y)}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$ | Direction along which normal flow is measured |
| Normal Flow | $u_n = \dfrac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$ | Only motion component measurable at a single pixel |
| Tangential Flow | $u_t$ — **unobservable** locally | The "invisible" component causing the aperture problem |
| Lucas-Kanade Solution | $(u,v)^T = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b$ | Local constant-flow least-squares estimate |
| Invertibility Condition | $A^T A$ must be full rank (i.e., $\lambda_{\min} > 0$ of structure tensor) | Fails on straight edges — aperture problem re-emerges |
| Structure Tensor (2×2) | $M = \sum_w \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Detects whether aperture problem is solvable in a window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** Whenever you see "uniquely determined" or "ambiguous motion" in a KTU question, the answer is rooted in the OFCE's one-equation-two-unknowns nature — quote the normal flow formula and explicitly state that the **tangential component is unobservable**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 3.1 — Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE) from First Principles

We begin with the **brightness constancy assumption** (a first-principle postulation of optical flow):

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t, y + v\Delta t, t + \Delta t) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

The right-hand side can be expanded via a **first-order multivariable Taylor series** about the point $(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x + \delta x, y + \delta y, t + \delta t) \approx I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\delta x + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\delta y + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\delta t + \mathcal{O}(\text{higher order})$$

Substituting $\delta x = u\Delta t$, $\delta y = v\Delta t$, $\delta t = \Delta t$:

$$I(x+u\Delta t, y+v\Delta t, t+\Delta t) \approx I(x,y,t) + I_x \cdot u\Delta t + I_y \cdot v\Delta t + I_t \cdot \Delta t$$

Substituting this expansion back into Equation (1):

$$I(x,y,t) = I(x,y,t) + I_x \cdot u\Delta t + I_y \cdot v\Delta t + I_t \cdot \Delta t$$

Cancel $I(x, y, t)$ from both sides:

$$0 = I_x \cdot u\Delta t + I_y \cdot v\Delta t + I_t \cdot \Delta t$$

Divide through by $\Delta t$ (assuming $\Delta t \neq 0$):

$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0 \quad \text{...(2: OFCE)}$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation** — and it is **one linear equation in two unknowns**, which is the algebraic embodiment of the aperture problem.

## Derivation 3.2 — Normal Flow Derivation

The OFCE can be rewritten as a dot product of the gradient with the velocity:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0 \quad \text{where } \mathbf{v} = (u, v), \nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} = -I_t$$

To isolate the component of $\mathbf{v}$ along $\nabla I$, project $\mathbf{v}$ onto the unit normal direction $\mathbf{n} = \dfrac{\nabla I}{\vert \nabla I \vert}$:

$$(\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}) = \frac{\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = \frac{-I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert}$$

Define the **normal flow** as this scalar projection:

$$u_n \equiv \mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

This derivation shows that **only the projection of $\mathbf{v}$ onto the gradient direction is recoverable**. The orthogonal component (tangential to the edge) is annihilated by the dot product and is **lost** — this is the geometric proof of the aperture problem.

## Derivation 3.3 — Lucas-Kanade Resolves the Aperture Problem Locally

The Lucas-Kanade method assumes the velocity $(u, v)$ is **constant within a small window** of $n$ pixels. This converts the underdetermined system into an **overdetermined** one.

For each pixel $p_i$ in the window, the OFCE gives:

$$I_x(p_i) \cdot u + I_y(p_i) \cdot v = -I_t(p_i)$$

Stacking all $n$ such equations into matrix form $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_x(p_1) & I_y(p_1) \\ I_x(p_2) & I_y(p_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(p_n) & I_y(p_n) \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 2} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}_{2 \times 1} = -\begin{bmatrix} I_t(p_1) \\ I_t(p_2) \\ \vdots \\ I_t(p_n) \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}$$

The least-squares solution minimizes $\vert \mathbf{A}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b} \vert^2$:

$$\mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

The $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is the **structure tensor**:

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} = \sum_i \begin{bmatrix} I_x(p_i)^2 & I_x(p_i) I_y(p_i) \\ I_x(p_i) I_y(p_i) & I_y(p_i)^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Invertibility Analysis (the heart of resolving the aperture problem):**

- If the window contains an **edge** (gradients in only one direction), $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ has **one large eigenvalue and one near-zero eigenvalue** → rank-deficient → $(\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1}$ is **unstable / undefined** → the aperture problem **persists**.
- If the window contains a **corner** (gradients in two independent directions), $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ has **two large eigenvalues** → full rank → a **unique solution** exists → the aperture problem is **resolved**.

This is precisely why **Harris corner detection** is so important in computer vision: it identifies regions where the aperture problem is locally resolvable.

## Analytical Matrix — Solution Methods vs. Aperture Problem

| **Method** | **Type of Constraint** | **Resolves Aperture Problem?** | **Failure Mode** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal Flow Only | Single OFCE | ❌ No — only 1 component | Tangential flow unknown |
| Lucas-Kanade | Local constancy over window | ⚠️ Partially — only at corners | Fails on edges (rank-deficient $A^T A$) |
| Horn-Schunck | Global smoothness regularizer | ✅ Globally | Over-smoothing at motion boundaries |
| Farneback (Dense Poly.) | Polynomial expansion of neighborhood | ✅ Yes — for textured regions | Sensitive to noise |
| TV-L1 Optical Flow | Total Variation + L1 robust norm | ✅ Robustly | Computationally expensive |
| Feature-Based (KLT) | Tracks Harris/SIFT corners | ✅ At corner features | Sparse output (no dense flow) |
| Deep Learning (FlowNet, RAFT) | Learned priors | ✅ Empirically very strong | Requires training data; black box |

## Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 1D image strip with intensity values $I(x)$ that is translating with velocity $v$. Show that the aperture problem prevents unique recovery of $v$ from a local measurement.

**Solution Approach:**
- Suppose $I(x) = \cos(2\pi x / \lambda)$ where $\lambda$ is the spatial wavelength.
- $I_x = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \sin(2\pi x / \lambda)$, and $I_t = -v \cdot I_x = \frac{2\pi v}{\lambda} \sin(2\pi x/\lambda)$.
- The OFCE becomes: $I_x \cdot v + I_t = 0 \Rightarrow$ identically satisfied for **any** $v$.
- Therefore, a local pixel sees the same intensity sequence for **all** velocities $v$ — this is the **aperture problem in 1D** (a corollary of 1D structure having only 1D gradient information).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU Answers:** The aperture problem is **most severe in 1D signals** (where $I_y = 0$) and **on straight edges** (where gradients are parallel). The minimum requirement for resolving it is **2D structure with gradients in at least 2 independent directions** — i.e., corners, blobs, or textured patches.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 4.1 — Geometric Interpretation of the Aperture Problem in Velocity Space

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Image Pixel with Edge"] --> B["Compute Gradient ∇I = Ix, Iy"]
    B --> C["Form OFCE: Ix·u + Iy·v + It = 0"]
    C --> D["Geometric Interpretation: A Line in u-v Velocity Space"]
    D --> E["Every Point on the Line is a Valid Velocity"]
    E --> F["Normal Flow Un = -It / |∇I| along Gradient Direction"]
    E --> G["Tangential Flow Ut is Invisible Locally"]
    F --> H["Aperture Problem: Cannot Determine Full 2D Motion"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Resolution: Lucas-Kanade / Horn-Schunck / Corner Features"]
    I --> J["Recover Unique 2D Velocity Vector"]
```

## Diagram 4.2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Optical Flow Estimation Under the Aperture Problem

```mermaid
graph LR
    IMG1["Input Frame at Time t"] --> GRAD["Spatial Gradient Computation Ix, Iy"]
    IMG2["Input Frame at Time t+dt"] --> TEMP["Temporal Derivative Computation It"]
    GRAD --> STACK["Stack A Matrix: Each Row Ix Iy"]
    TEMP --> VEC["Form b Vector: -It Values"]
    STACK --> LSQ["Least Squares Solver A^T A Inverse A^T b"]
    VEC --> LSQ
    LSQ --> INV{"Is A^T A Invertible?"}
    INV -- "Yes, Corner Region" --> OK["Full Flow u, v Recovered"]
    INV -- "No, Edge Region, Rank Deficient" --> FAIL["Aperture Problem Persists, Fall Back to Normal Flow Only"]
    OK --> OUT["Dense or Sparse Optical Flow Output"]
    FAIL --> REG["Apply Regularization, e.g., Horn-Schunck Smoothness"]
    REG --> OUT
```

## Diagram 4.3 — Comparison of Pixel Categories for Aperture Problem Susceptibility

```mermaid

    PIX["Image Pixel Classification"] --> P1["Flat Region: ∇I = 0"]
    PIX --> P2["Straight Edge: ∇I in 1 Direction"]

    PIX --> P4["Texture: ∇I in Many Directions"]

    P2 --> R2["Aperture Problem: Only Normal Flow Available"]
    P3 --> R3["Resolvable: Unique 2D Flow via Lucas-Kanade"]

```
