# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEET754, Module 3]
**Define the Aperture Problem in computer vision. Why is it mathematically inevitable at a single pixel?**

**Model Answer:**

The aperture problem is the inherent ambiguity that arises when attempting to determine the 2D motion of a visual feature using only **local** intensity measurements through a small neighborhood (aperture). At a single pixel, the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)**:

$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0$$

provides **only one linear equation in two unknowns** $(u, v)$. The system is therefore **underdetermined** — geometrically, the OFCE defines a line in 2D velocity space, and every point on this line is a valid solution. Only the component of motion **normal to the local intensity gradient** (the **normal flow**) is uniquely recoverable; the component **tangential to the edge** remains invisible. The aperture problem is mathematically inevitable because the brightness constancy assumption yields exactly one equation per pixel, which is fundamentally insufficient to solve for two velocity components.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEET754]
**What is normal flow? Derive the formula for the normal flow from the optical flow constraint equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **normal flow** is the component of the 2D optical flow vector along the direction of the image intensity gradient — i.e., the motion component **perpendicular to a local edge**. It is the only motion component that a local aperture can measure.

**Derivation:** Starting from the OFCE, $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$, we can rewrite it as $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} = -I_t$, where $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (u, v)$. Projecting $\mathbf{v}$ onto the unit gradient direction $\mathbf{n} = \nabla I / \vert \nabla I \vert$:

$$\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert \nabla I \vert} = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$$

Therefore:

$$u_n = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$$

This is the **normal flow** formula. The tangential component of $\mathbf{v}$ (orthogonal to $\mathbf{n}$) drops out during the dot product and cannot be recovered locally — this is the aperture problem.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEET754, Module 3]

**Q.A.** **(a)** Explain the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation** with all necessary assumptions. Show that the aperture problem is a direct consequence of this equation's structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the **Lucas-Kanade** method locally overcomes the aperture problem, and explain under what conditions it still **fails**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Optical Flow Constraint Equation and the Aperture Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Assumption: Brightness Constancy**
The fundamental assumption of optical flow is that the intensity of a moving point remains constant over a small time interval $\Delta t$:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t, y + v\Delta t, t + \Delta t)$$

Here $(u, v)$ is the 2D velocity of the point. This assumption holds when surface reflectance is Lambertian and illumination is steady.

**Step 2 — First-Order Taylor Expansion**
Expanding the RHS via a first-order Taylor series:

$$I(x + u\Delta t, y + v\Delta t, t + \Delta t) \approx I + I_x u\Delta t + I_y v\Delta t + I_t \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Derive the OFCE**
Subtracting $I$ from both sides and dividing by $\Delta t$:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation** — a single linear equation in two unknowns $(u, v)$.

**Step 4 — Why This Causes the Aperture Problem**
The system is **underdetermined**: 1 equation, 2 unknowns. Geometrically, the OFCE defines a **line** in $(u, v)$ velocity space. All points on this line produce identical brightness patterns within a local aperture. The **normal flow** $u_n = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ is uniquely determined, but the **tangential flow** along the edge is **invisible** to the local observer. This inherent ambiguity is the **aperture problem**.

**(b) Lucas-Kanade Method and Its Failure Modes (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Local Constancy Assumption**
Lucas-Kanade assumes that the velocity $(u, v)$ is **constant within a small window** of $n$ pixels. This turns the underdetermined 1-equation-2-unknowns problem into an overdetermined $n$-equations-2-unknowns system.

**Step 2 — Stacking into a Linear System**
For each pixel $p_i$ in the window:

$$I_x(p_i) u + I_y(p_i) v = -I_t(p_i)$$

In matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{b}$, where $\mathbf{A}$ is $n \times 2$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is $n \times 1$.

**Step 3 — Least-Squares Solution**
The unique least-squares estimate is:

$$\mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 4 — When It Works**
The matrix $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is the **structure tensor**. When the window contains a **corner** or **textured region** with intensity gradients in **two independent directions**, $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is **full rank** and **invertible** — a unique 2D flow is recovered, and the aperture problem is locally resolved.

**Step 5 — When It Fails**
If the window contains a **straight edge** or a **flat region**, all gradients $\nabla I$ point in (nearly) the same direction. The structure tensor $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ becomes **rank-deficient** (one eigenvalue is near zero), and the inverse is **unstable or undefined**. The aperture problem **persists** in such regions. This is why corner detectors (Harris, Shi-Tomasi) are used to identify reliable regions for optical flow tracking.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEET754, Module 3, Alternate Choice]

**Q.B.** **(a)** Describe the **Barber Pole Illusion** and explain how it is a manifestation of the aperture problem in human visual perception. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Horn-Schunck** and **Lucas-Kanade** methods of optical flow estimation in terms of how each handles the aperture problem. Use relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Barber Pole Illusion and the Aperture Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Description of the Illusion**
A barber pole consists of a rotating cylinder with **diagonal stripes** painted on its surface. When the cylinder rotates about its vertical axis (horizontal surface motion), observers perceive the stripes as **moving vertically upward** — a clear mismatch with the actual horizontal rotation of the surface.

**Step 2 — Connection to the Aperture Problem**
Each local region of the visual field (the "aperture" of a retinal receptive field or a local CV operator) sees a moving **diagonal edge**. The **normal flow** at every local point is **perpendicular to the edge**, which has both a horizontal and vertical component. However, the human visual system and local computer vision algorithms can only **track the normal component of motion** at each point. The **tangential component** (along the edge) is invisible.

For diagonal stripes, the normal flow is biased toward the **vertical direction**, which is why observers (and algorithms) perceive upward motion despite the true horizontal motion of the cylinder. The barber pole illusion is therefore a direct perceptual analogue of the aperture problem.

**Step 3 — Resolution in Practice**
The illusion disappears when observers can integrate information across **larger spatial extents** (i.e., see the full pole, not just a small patch) — this is the human equivalent of using **global regularization (Horn-Schunck)** or **large-context deep networks** to disambiguate the true motion.

**(b) Horn-Schunck vs. Lucas-Kanade (7 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Lucas-Kanade** | **Horn-Schunck** |
|---|---|---|
| Constraint type | Local — assumes constant flow in a small window | Global — assumes smooth flow across the entire image |
| Optimization | Local least-squares: $\mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Global variational: minimize $\iint (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \alpha^2 (\vert \nabla u \vert^2 + \vert \nabla v \vert^2) \, dx\, dy$ |
| Aperture problem handling | Resolved **locally** at corners/textured patches | Resolved **globally** via smoothness regularization $\alpha^2$ term |
| Failure mode | Fails on edges (rank-deficient $A^T A$); produces sparse flow | Over-smooths motion boundaries; computationally expensive iterative solution |
| Output | Sparse flow at feature points | Dense flow field |
| Computational cost | Low (closed-form, per window) | High (iterative Gauss-Seidel or multigrid) |

**Key insight:** Both methods impose additional constraints to escape the underdetermined OFCE. Lucas-Kanade uses **spatial constancy within a window**, while Horn-Schunck uses **global smoothness**. Each handles the aperture problem by trading off local accuracy (Lucas-Kanade) for global coherence (Horn-Schunck).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Brightness Constancy Assumption** before writing the OFCE — this is a 1-mark deduction almost guaranteed.
> 2. **Stating "the aperture problem cannot be solved"** — this is **wrong**. It **can** be resolved using additional constraints (Lucas-Kanade, Horn-Schunck, corner features). Examiners want you to show the *mathematical origin* of the problem and the *strategies* to overcome it.
> 3. **Confusing normal flow with full optical flow** — normal flow is **scalar** (one number per pixel); full optical flow is **vector** $(u, v)$.
> 4. **Skipping the structure tensor invertibility analysis** when discussing Lucas-Kanade — this is the most important conceptual point (rank deficiency on edges is the *reason* Lucas-Kanade still fails in some regions).
> 5. **Not using the correct formula symbols** — KTU examiners expect $I_x, I_y, I_t$ (not $\partial I/\partial x$, though both are acceptable).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aperture Problem Definition:** A local image operator cannot uniquely determine the 2D motion of an edge — only the **normal component** is observable.
- **Mathematical Root:** The OFCE $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ is **1 equation in 2 unknowns** — fundamentally underdetermined.
- **Normal Flow Formula:** $u_n = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ — the *only* recoverable component locally.
- **Tangential Flow:** Invisible locally — this is the "missing" piece of the aperture problem.
- **Lucas-Kanade Fix:** Imposes local constancy of $(u, v)$ in a window — works at **corners** (full-rank structure tensor), fails on **edges** (rank-deficient structure tensor).
- **Horn-Schunck Fix:** Imposes global smoothness — produces **dense** flow at the cost of over-smoothing motion boundaries.
- **Barber Pole Illusion:** A classical human-vision demonstration of the aperture problem — local normal flow dominates perception.
- **Minimum 2D Structure Required:** To resolve the aperture problem locally, the window must contain gradients in **at least 2 independent directions** (corners/texture), not just 1 (edges).
- **Engineering Practice:** Modern SLAM and tracking systems (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono, RAFT) deliberately use **corner-rich features** or **global/learned priors** to circumvent the aperture problem.
- **KTU Frequency:** This is a **high-yield Module 3 topic** — expect at least one Part-A sub-question (3 marks) and possibly a sub-part of a Part-B question (7 marks) on this concept in every exam cycle.
- **Key Mathematical Condition:** For Lucas-Kanade invertibility, both eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of the structure tensor $A^T A$ must be **strictly positive** — this is the formal algebraic condition for resolving the aperture problem locally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horn-Shunck Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Horn-Shunck Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Horn-Schunck Method (Definition)**
> The **Horn-Schunck method** is a global, variational, differential technique for estimating dense **optical flow** (per-pixel motion vectors) between two consecutive image frames. Proposed by **Berthold K.P. Horn and Brian G. Schunck in 1981**, it computes a 2D velocity field $(u, v)$ for every pixel by minimizing a combined energy functional that integrates the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** and a **global smoothness (regularization) constraint** over the entire image domain.

The method assumes two foundational hypotheses:
1. **Brightness Constancy Assumption:** The intensity (gray level) of a moving object point remains constant between successive frames.
2. **Smoothness Assumption (Gradient Constancy):** Neighboring pixels belonging to the same surface should have similar velocity vectors; the flow field varies smoothly across the image.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Intuition — "The Stretched Rubber Sheet"**
> Imagine a translucent **rubber sheet** placed over a picture on a table, where pins hold the corners down. Now suppose the picture below is slightly shifted to the right. The rubber sheet deforms smoothly to follow the motion. 
> - The **pins** represent the *brightness-constancy points* (pixels whose intensity cannot be violated by the computed motion).
> - The **elastic stretching energy** of the sheet represents the *smoothness constraint* (penalizing abrupt, chaotic motion between neighbors).
> - The Horn-Schunck algorithm is essentially the mathematical physics that finds the *minimum-energy deformation* of this sheet — a motion field that best explains the apparent pixel shifts while remaining globally smooth.

In simpler engineering terms: it is solving **two competing objectives at once** — *fit the data tightly* (matching pixel intensities frame-to-frame) and *stay smooth* (no random motion noise).

## 1.3 Key Parameters and Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $I(x,y,t)$ | Image intensity at position $(x,y)$ and time $t$ |
| $u(x,y)$ | Horizontal component of optical flow |
| $v(x,y)$ | Vertical component of optical flow |
| $I_x, I_y$ | Spatial partial derivatives of intensity |
| $I_t$ | Temporal partial derivative of intensity |
| $\alpha^{2}$ | Regularization weight (smoothness coefficient), $\alpha > 0$ |
| $\nabla^{2}$ | Laplacian operator |

> [!NOTE]
> The regularization constant **$\alpha^{2}$** is a tunable scalar. A **large $\alpha$** enforces a smoother (more rigid) flow field; a **small $\alpha$** allows the flow to fit intensity data more aggressively at the cost of noise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The Horn-Schunck framework logically proceeds through five analytical stages:

### Stage 1: First-Order Taylor Approximation (Brightness Constancy)
Assuming pixel intensity is preserved along the motion trajectory over a small time interval $\Delta t$, we can express the intensity of frame $t + \Delta t$ as a first-order Taylor expansion about frame $t$:

$$I(x + u\Delta t, \, y + v\Delta t, \, t + \Delta t) = I(x, y, t)$$

Expanding using calculus:

$$I(x, y, t) + I_{x}\, u\Delta t + I_{y}\, v\Delta t + I_{t}\, \Delta t + \text{(higher-order terms)} = I(x, y, t)$$

Dividing through by $\Delta t$ and discarding higher-order infinitesimals yields the cornerstone of motion estimation:

$$\boxed{\,I_{x}\, u + I_{y}\, v + I_{t} = 0\,}$$

This single scalar equation contains **two unknown velocity components** ($u$ and $v$) per pixel — making the problem **ill-posed (underdetermined)**. The famous *aperture problem* is precisely this ambiguity: a single intensity gradient tells us only the motion *along* the gradient direction, not perpendicular to it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Aperture Problem — Why One Equation is Not Enough**
> When viewing a moving edge through a small aperture, the local motion appears ambiguous. Only normal flow (motion along the gradient) is observable. To recover full 2D flow, we must impose an additional global prior — this is where the smoothness constraint enters.

### Stage 2: The Smoothness (Regularization) Constraint
To resolve the aperture problem, Horn and Schunck proposed that the velocity field must be **spatially smooth**. The penalty for deviation from smoothness is measured by the sum of squared partial derivatives of the flow:

$$E_{s} = \iint \left[\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)^{2}\right] dx\, dy$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Squared Derivatives?**
> Squaring penalizes large local velocity variations more heavily than small ones (analogous to elastic potential energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$), and produces a convex optimization landscape — guaranteeing a unique global minimum.

### Stage 3: The Combined Energy Functional
The two constraints are weighted and added. $\alpha^{2}$ controls the trade-off between data fidelity and smoothness:

$$\boxed{\,E(u, v) = \iint \Big[\underbrace{\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2}}_{\text{Data Term}} + \underbrace{\alpha^{2}\!\left(\lvert \nabla u \rvert^{2} + \lvert \nabla v \rvert^{2}\right)}_{\text{Smoothness Term}}\Big] dx\, dy\,}$$

### Stage 4: Euler-Lagrange Derivation
To find the stationary point, we treat the integrand as a Lagrangian density $\mathcal{L}(u, u_{x}, u_{y}, v, v_{x}, v_{y})$ and apply the **Euler-Lagrange equation**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{x}}\right) - \frac{d}{dy}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{y}}\right) = 0$$

Applying this to $u$ yields:

$$I_{x}\!\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right) - \alpha^{2} \nabla^{2} u = 0$$

and symmetrically for $v$:

$$I_{y}\!\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right) - \alpha^{2} \nabla^{2} v = 0$$

### Stage 5: Iterative Numerical Solution (Gauss-Seidel Relaxation)
The Laplacian $\nabla^{2} u$ is approximated by the difference between the local value $u$ and its neighborhood average $\bar{u}$:

$$\nabla^{2} u \approx u - \bar{u} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \nabla^{2} v \approx v - \bar{v}$$

Substituting and rearranging gives the **Horn-Schunck iterative update equations**:

$$\boxed{\,u^{\,k+1} = \bar{u}^{\,k} - \frac{I_{x}\!\left(I_{x}\bar{u}^{\,k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{\,k} + I_{t}\right)}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,v^{\,k+1} = \bar{v}^{\,k} - \frac{I_{y}\!\left(I_{x}\bar{u}^{\,k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{\,k} + I_{t}\right)}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}\,}$$

where $k$ denotes the iteration index. The recursion is initialized with $u^{0} = v^{0} = 0$ and continues until the change between iterations falls below a chosen convergence threshold $\epsilon$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t} = 0$ | **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** — fundamental scalar motion equation |
| 2 | $E(u, v) = \iint [(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t})^{2} + \alpha^{2}(\lvert \nabla u \rvert^{2} + \lvert \nabla v \rvert^{2})] dx\, dy$ | **Combined energy functional** (variational cost) |
| 3 | $I_{x}(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}) - \alpha^{2}\nabla^{2} u = 0$ | **Euler-Lagrange equation for $u$** |
| 4 | $I_{y}(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}) - \alpha^{2}\nabla^{2} v = 0$ | **Euler-Lagrange equation for $v$** |
| 5 | $u^{k+1} = \bar{u}^{k} - \dfrac{I_{x}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$ | **Iterative update for horizontal flow $u$** |
| 6 | $v^{k+1} = \bar{v}^{k} - \dfrac{I_{y}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$ | **Iterative update for vertical flow $v$** |
| 7 | $\nabla^{2} u \approx u - \bar{u}$ | **Discrete Laplacian approximation** using 4-neighbor average |
| 8 | $\text{Stop when} \quad \max(\lvert u^{k+1} - u^{k} \rvert, \lvert v^{k+1} - v^{k} \rvert) < \epsilon$ | **Convergence criterion** for iteration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discretization of Partial Derivatives (used in practice):**
> - $I_{x} \approx \tfrac{1}{4}\!\left(I_{i,j+1} - I_{i,j-1}\right)$ — central spatial difference
> - $I_{y} \approx \tfrac{1}{4}\!\left(I_{i+1,j} - I_{i-1,j}\right)$ — central spatial difference
> - $I_{t} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(I_{i,j,t+1} - I_{i,j,t-1}\right)$ — central temporal difference
> - $\bar{u} = \tfrac{1}{4}(u_{i,j-1} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i+1,j})$ — 4-neighbor average

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Horn-Schunck method is foundational in:
- **Autonomous vehicles** — estimating ego-motion and obstacle trajectories from dash-cam video.
- **Video surveillance** — tracking pedestrians and vehicles when background subtraction alone is insufficient.
- **Robotics and SLAM** — visual odometry front-ends where dense flow feeds into pose estimation.
- **Medical imaging** — motion analysis in cardiac ultrasound and microscopy.
- **Sports analytics** — quantifying player movement without specialized motion-capture suits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Optical Flow Constraint Equation

We start by mathematically defining brightness constancy. For a moving image point $(x(t), y(t))$ whose brightness does not change over a small time interval $\Delta t$:

$$I(x(t), \, y(t), \, t) = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$ using the **multivariable chain rule**:

$$\frac{d I}{d t} = \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\frac{dt}{dt} = 0$$

Recognizing that $\frac{dx}{dt} = u$ (horizontal velocity), $\frac{dy}{dt} = v$ (vertical velocity), and $\frac{dt}{dt} = 1$:

$$I_{x}\, u + I_{y}\, v + I_{t} = 0$$

This is the gradient-based optical flow equation. It is a **single linear equation in two unknowns** — hence the need for an additional constraint.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Energy Functional

Let us construct the total energy as a weighted sum:

$$E(u, v) = E_{\text{data}} + \alpha^{2} E_{\text{smooth}}$$

**Step 1: Data Term.** We square the OFCE residual to obtain a non-negative scalar penalty:

$$E_{\text{data}} = \iint \left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2} dx\, dy$$

**Step 2: Smoothness Term.** We penalize the spatial gradient magnitude of the flow field:

$$E_{\text{smooth}} = \iint \left(\lvert \nabla u \rvert^{2} + \lvert \nabla v \rvert^{2}\right) dx\, dy = \iint \left(u_{x}^{2} + u_{y}^{2} + v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2}\right) dx\, dy$$

**Step 3: Combine with Weight $\alpha^{2}$:**

$$E(u, v) = \iint \Big[\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2} + \alpha^{2}\!\left(u_{x}^{2} + u_{y}^{2} + v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2}\right)\Big] dx\, dy$$

## 3.3 Application of the Euler-Lagrange Equation

We seek the functions $u(x,y)$ and $v(x,y)$ that **minimize $E$**. The integrand is the Lagrangian density:

$$\mathcal{L} = \left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2} + \alpha^{2}\!\left(u_{x}^{2} + u_{y}^{2} + v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2}\right)$$

The Euler-Lagrange equation is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{y}}\right) = 0$$

**Computing partial derivatives with respect to $u$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u} = 2 I_{x}\!\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)$$

**Computing partial derivatives with respect to $u_{x}$ and $u_{y}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{x}} = 2\alpha^{2} u_{x}, \qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{y}} = 2\alpha^{2} u_{y}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2\alpha^{2} u_{x}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2\alpha^{2} u_{y}) = 2\alpha^{2} \nabla^{2} u$$

**Combining in the Euler-Lagrange equation (dividing by 2):**

$$I_{x}\!\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right) - \alpha^{2} \nabla^{2} u = 0$$

By symmetric reasoning for $v$:

$$I_{y}\!\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right) - \alpha^{2} \nabla^{2} v = 0$$

## 3.4 From Continuous to Discrete — Laplacian Approximation

The Laplacian $\nabla^{2} u = \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$ is approximated discretely. Using the second-order central difference:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} \approx u_{i,j+1} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i,j-1}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} \approx u_{i+1,j} - 2u_{i,j} + u_{i-1,j}$$

Summing:

$$\nabla^{2} u \approx \left(u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1} + u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j}\right) - 4 u_{i,j} = 4\bar{u} - 4u = -4(u - \bar{u})$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla^{2} u \approx -(u - \bar{u}) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad u - \bar{u} \approx -\nabla^{2} u$$

The sign convention varies in literature; the essential identity is the **substitution $\nabla^{2} u \to \bar{u} - u$** in the Euler-Lagrange equation, which leads directly to the iterative update formula.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Final Iterative Update Equation

Starting from the Euler-Lagrange equation (dividing both terms by their sign convention):

$$I_{x}\!\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right) = \alpha^{2}(\bar{u} - u)$$

Collecting $u$ on the left side:

$$I_{x}^{2} u + I_{x} I_{y} v + I_{x} I_{t} = \alpha^{2} \bar{u} - \alpha^{2} u$$

$$\left(\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2}\right) u = \alpha^{2} \bar{u} - I_{x} I_{y} v - I_{x} I_{t}$$

To express in **iterative form**, we replace the unknown $v$ on the right-hand side with the current iteration's neighbor average $\bar{v}^{k}$ (Gauss-Seidel relaxation), and $u$ with the previous iteration $\bar{u}^{k}$. The resulting update is:

$$u^{k+1} = \bar{u}^{k} - \frac{I_{x}\!\left(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t}\right)}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$

$$v^{k+1} = \bar{v}^{k} - \frac{I_{y}\!\left(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t}\right)}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the denominator $\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}$?**
> It serves two roles: (1) acts as a **numerical stabilizer** that prevents division by zero in smooth (textureless) regions where $I_{x} \approx I_{y} \approx 0$, and (2) automatically **down-weights the OFCE update** in flat regions, allowing the smoothness prior to dominate — exactly the right physical behavior.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A $3 \times 3$ patch where the central pixel $(2,2)$ has the following intensity values:
- Frame $t$: $I(2,2) = 100$
- Frame $t+1$: $I(2,2) = 102$
- $I_{x} = 2$, $I_{y} = 1$, $I_{t} = 2$ (computed from frame differencing)
- $\alpha^{2} = 1$
- Initial guess: $\bar{u}^{0} = 0$, $\bar{v}^{0} = 0$

**Iteration $k=0 \to k=1$:**

$$u^{1} = 0 - \frac{2 \cdot (2 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 0 + 2)}{1 + 4 + 1} = -\frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} = -\frac{4}{6} = -0.667$$

$$v^{1} = 0 - \frac{1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 0 + 2)}{1 + 4 + 1} = -\frac{1 \cdot 2}{6} = -0.333$$

**Interpretation:** The pixel at $(2,2)$ is moving approximately **0.67 units to the left** and **0.33 units downward** per frame. Subsequent iterations would refine these values using the newly updated neighbor averages.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Horn-Schunck Algorithm

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete data-processing pipeline** of the Horn-Schunck method, mapping the interactions between the data term, smoothness term, iterative solver, and final output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Two Consecutive Image Frames I t and I t+1] --> B[Spatial Gradient Computation]
    A --> C[Temporal Gradient Computation]
    B --> B1[Compute I x using central difference]
    B --> B2[Compute I y using central difference]
    C --> C1[Compute I t using frame difference]
    B1 --> D[Construct OFCE Residual]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> D1[OFCE Equation: I x u + I y v + I t = 0]
    D1 --> E[Energy Functional Formulation]
    E --> E1[Data Term: Squared OFCE Residual]
    E --> E2[Smoothness Term: Squared Spatial Derivatives of Flow]
    E1 --> F[Apply Euler-Lagrange Optimization]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Generate Two Coupled PDEs for u and v]
    F1 --> G[Discretization and Laplacian Approximation]
    G --> G1[Replace Laplacian with Neighborhood Average]
    G1 --> H[Initialize u superscript 0 = 0, v superscript 0 = 0]
    H --> I[Iterative Update Loop]
    I --> I1[Compute New u superscript k+1]
    I --> I2[Compute New v superscript k+1]
    I1 --> J{Convergence Check: Max Change less than epsilon}
    I2 --> J
    J -->|Not Converged| I
    J -->|Converged| K[Output Dense Optical Flow Field u v]
    K --> L[Visualization as Color-Coded HSV Image or Quiver Plot]
```

## 4.2 Information Flow Summary

The diagram above encodes the following critical interactions:

1. **Dual-branch preprocessing:** Spatial derivatives ($I_x, I_y$) and temporal derivative ($I_t$) are computed *in parallel* from the input image pair.
2. **Constraint fusion:** The OFCE (data term) and smoothness prior are merged into a single scalar energy functional.
3. **Variational optimization:** The Euler-Lagrange machinery converts the energy minimization into a system of coupled PDEs.
4. **Discretization bridge:** The continuous Laplacian is replaced by a finite-difference approximation, enabling pixel-wise computation.
5. **Fixed-point iteration:** Gauss-Seidel updates alternate between $u$ and $v$ until the maximum inter-iteration change falls below threshold $\epsilon$.
6. **Dense flow output:** Every pixel receives a $(u, v)$ velocity vector, which can be rendered as a *color-coded flow image* (hue = direction, saturation = magnitude) for human interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]**
**State and explain the optical flow constraint equation. Why is the problem ill-posed, and how does Horn-Schunck resolve it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The optical flow constraint equation is derived from the **brightness constancy assumption**, which states that the intensity of a moving pixel does not change between consecutive frames. Mathematically:

$$I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t} = 0$$

where $I_x, I_y$ are spatial intensity gradients, $I_t$ is the temporal gradient, and $(u, v)$ is the velocity vector.

The problem is **ill-posed** because one equation must solve for two unknowns $(u, v)$ at every pixel — the so-called *aperture problem*. Horn-Schunck resolves this by introducing a **global smoothness regularization term** $\alpha^{2}(\lvert \nabla u \rvert^{2} + \lvert \nabla v \rvert^{2})$ to the energy functional, which couples neighboring pixels and supplies the missing constraint.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Horn-Schunck method and the Lucas-Kanade method in terms of assumptions and computational approach.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Horn-Schunck | Lucas-Kanade |
|---|---|---|
| **Constraint type** | Global (whole image) | Local (small window) |
| **Smoothness** | Imposed globally via regularization | Implicitly assumed within local neighborhood |
| **Solution** | Iterative (Gauss-Seidel relaxation) | Closed-form weighted least squares |
| **Output** | Dense flow field for every pixel | Sparse flow at feature points |
| **Cost** | Computationally expensive | Lightweight and fast |
| **Robustness** | Sensitive to noise across large regions | Robust within local patches |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a) Derive the Horn-Schunck optical flow energy functional from the brightness constancy assumption. Clearly state the role of the regularization parameter $\alpha^{2}$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with the brightness constancy constraint. The image intensity of a physical point is invariant under motion:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t, y + v\Delta t, t + \Delta t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the first-order Taylor expansion to the right-hand side and subtract $I(x,y,t)$ from both sides:

$$I_{x} u \Delta t + I_{y} v \Delta t + I_{t} \Delta t = 0$$

Dividing by $\Delta t$ yields the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation**:

$$I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t} = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3:** Equation (1) is one equation in two unknowns. To resolve this, we impose a smoothness constraint penalizing rapid spatial variations in the flow field. The squared gradient penalty is:

$$E_{\text{smooth}} = \iint \left[\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)^{2}\right] dx\, dy$$

**Step 4:** The data-fidelity term is the squared OFCE residual:

$$E_{\text{data}} = \iint \left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2} dx\, dy$$

**Step 5:** Combine the two terms, weighted by $\alpha^{2}$:

$$E(u, v) = \iint \left[\left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2} + \alpha^{2}\!\left(\lvert \nabla u \rvert^{2} + \lvert \nabla v \rvert^{2}\right)\right] dx\, dy$$

**Role of $\alpha^{2}$:** The parameter $\alpha^{2}$ controls the **trade-off** between data fidelity and smoothness. A **large $\alpha$** produces a rigid, highly smooth flow (good for noisy images) but may underfit true motion boundaries. A **small $\alpha$** allows the flow to follow intensity data tightly, but amplifies noise in flat regions.

---

**(b) Use the Euler-Lagrange equation to derive the iterative update formulas for $u^{k+1}$ and $v^{k+1}$ used in the Horn-Schunck algorithm.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The Lagrangian density of the energy functional is:

$$\mathcal{L}(u, u_{x}, u_{y}, v, v_{x}, v_{y}) = \left(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}\right)^{2} + \alpha^{2}(u_{x}^{2} + u_{y}^{2} + v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Euler-Lagrange equation for $u$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{y}}\right) = 0$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u} = 2 I_{x}(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t})$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{x}} = 2\alpha^{2} u_{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{x}}\right) = 2\alpha^{2} u_{xx}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{y}} = 2\alpha^{2} u_{y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial u_{y}}\right) = 2\alpha^{2} u_{yy}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting and dividing by 2:

$$I_{x}(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}) - \alpha^{2}(u_{xx} + u_{yy}) = 0$$

Recognizing $u_{xx} + u_{yy} = \nabla^{2} u$, we obtain:

$$I_{x}(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}) - \alpha^{2} \nabla^{2} u = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4:** Approximate the Laplacian using the neighborhood average $\bar{u}$:

$$\nabla^{2} u \approx \bar{u} - u$$

Substituting into equation (2):

$$I_{x}(I_{x} u + I_{y} v + I_{t}) = \alpha^{2}(\bar{u} - u)$$

**Step 5:** Apply Gauss-Seidel relaxation — replace $u$ and $v$ on the right side with iteration-$k$ estimates, and solve for the new $u$:

$$u^{k+1} = \bar{u}^{k} - \frac{I_{x}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** By symmetry, the update for $v$ is:

$$v^{k+1} = \bar{v}^{k} - \frac{I_{y}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$

These equations are iteratively applied until $\lvert u^{k+1} - u^{k} \rvert < \epsilon$ for all pixels.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the two fundamental assumptions underlying the Horn-Schunck method. Describe the limitations of each assumption in real-world scenarios.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1: Brightness Constancy**
This assumes that the intensity $I(x, y, t)$ of a surface point remains unchanged between two consecutive frames, even though the point has moved. Mathematically:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t, y + v\Delta t, t + \Delta t)$$

*Limitations:*
- **Illumination changes** (shadows, headlights, flickering lights) violate this assumption catastrophically.
- **Specular reflections** on glossy surfaces alter pixel intensity based on viewing angle.
- **Occlusion and disocclusion** expose previously hidden regions whose intensity does not match the model.
- **Camera auto-exposure** (auto-gain) rescales intensity globally between frames.

**Assumption 2: Spatial Smoothness**
This assumes that neighboring pixels belonging to the same surface share similar velocities — i.e., $\nabla u$ and $\nabla v$ are small.

*Limitations:*
- **Motion discontinuities** at object boundaries are heavily over-smoothed, producing blurred flow edges.
- **Small fast-moving objects** are smeared into the background flow.
- **Depth discontinuities** in stereo or multi-layer scenes violate the single-flow assumption per pixel.

**Mitigation Strategies:** Modern variants use image-driven weighting (e.g., weighted Horn-Schunck) or robust penalty functions to handle these violations.

---

**(b) For a $2 \times 2$ image block with central pixel intensity values $I(1,1,t) = 50$ and $I(1,1,t+1) = 54$, given $I_x = 3, I_y = 4, I_t = 4$, and $\alpha^{2} = 2$, compute one iteration of the Horn-Schunck update with $u^0 = v^0 = 0$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Information:**
- $I_x = 3$, $I_y = 4$, $I_t = 4$
- $\alpha^{2} = 2$
- Initial guess: $u^0 = 0$, $v^0 = 0$
- Hence $\bar{u}^{0} = 0$, $\bar{v}^{0} = 0$

**Step 1: Compute the denominator.**

$$\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2} = 2 + 9 + 16 = 27$$

**Step 2: Compute the OFCE residual term.**

$$I_{x}\bar{u}^{0} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{0} + I_{t} = 3(0) + 4(0) + 4 = 4$$

**Step 3: Compute $u^{1}$.**

$$u^{1} = \bar{u}^{0} - \frac{I_{x}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{0} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{0} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$

$$u^{1} = 0 - \frac{3 \cdot 4}{27} = -\frac{12}{27} \approx -0.444$$

**Step 4: Compute $v^{1}$.**

$$v^{1} = \bar{v}^{0} - \frac{I_{y}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{0} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{0} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$

$$v^{1} = 0 - \frac{4 \cdot 4}{27} = -\frac{16}{27} \approx -0.593$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** After one iteration, the estimated flow at the central pixel is $(u^1, v^1) = (-0.444, -0.593)$. The negative signs indicate motion in the leftward and downward directions, with magnitude $\sqrt{0.444^{2} + 0.593^{2}} \approx 0.741$ pixels per frame. Subsequent iterations with updated neighbor averages will refine this estimate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the OFCE residual** — Many students write $E_{\text{data}} = I_x u + I_y v + I_t$ (linear). It must be the **squared** form: $(I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2$. *(−2 marks typical penalty)*
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ with $\alpha^{2}$** — The regularization weight in the energy functional is $\alpha^{2}$, not $\alpha$. The square is conventional and emphasizes its quadratic penalty role. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Missing the minus sign in the iterative update** — A common slip is writing $u^{k+1} = \bar{u}^{k} + \frac{\cdots}{\cdots}$ instead of the correct minus sign. Always re-derive from the Euler-Lagrange equation if in doubt.
> 4. **Omitting the Laplacian approximation step** — Examiners specifically test the bridge from continuous $\nabla^{2} u$ to discrete $(\bar{u} - u)$. Skipping this loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Writing $u = \bar{u} - \frac{I_x I_t}{\alpha^{2} + I_x^{2} + I_y^{2}}$ without iteration index $k$** — Always indicate the iteration number to show the recursive nature of the algorithm.
> 6. **Confusing the OFCE with the Horn-Schunck update equation** — The OFCE is the *constraint*; the update equation is the *solution algorithm*. They are not the same thing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Horn-Schunck Method**
> - ☐ **OFCE** is $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ — derived via brightness constancy and Taylor expansion.
> - ☐ The OFCE alone is **underdetermined** (1 equation, 2 unknowns) — the *aperture problem*.
> - ☐ The **smoothness penalty** is the sum of squared spatial gradients of $u$ and $v$.
> - ☐ The **combined energy** is the sum of squared OFCE residual and $\alpha^{2}$ times the smoothness penalty.
> - ☐ The parameter $\alpha^{2}$ is the **regularization weight**; larger $\alpha$ = smoother flow.
> - ☐ **Euler-Lagrange** application yields two coupled PDEs involving the Laplacian.
> - ☐ Discrete update uses $\nabla^{2} u \approx \bar{u} - u$ where $\bar{u}$ is the 4-neighbor average.
> - ☐ **Iterative updates** (Gauss-Seidel):
> $$u^{k+1} = \bar{u}^{k} - \frac{I_{x}(I_{x}\bar{u}^{k} + I_{y}\bar{v}^{k} + I_{t})}{\alpha^{2} + I_{x}^{2} + I_{y}^{2}}$$
> - ☐ Converges when $\lvert u^{k+1} - u^{k} \rvert < \epsilon$ everywhere.
> - ☐ Produces **dense optical flow** (per-pixel vectors) — unlike Lucas-Kanade which is sparse.
> - ☐ Sensitive to brightness constancy violations (illumination changes, specularities).
> - ☐ Over-smooths motion at object boundaries due to the global regularization.
> - ☐ **Initialization:** $u^0 = v^0 = 0$; **Stop:** fixed iteration count or convergence threshold.
> - ☐ **Difference from Lucas-Kanade:** Global variational vs. local least-squares; iterative vs. closed-form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lucas-Kanade Method, Structure from Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lucas-Kanade Method, Structure from Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Optical Flow — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Flow** is defined as the 2D distribution of *apparent motion velocities* of brightness patterns in an image sequence. Mathematically, it is the vector field $\mathbf{v}(x,y) = (u(x,y), v(x,y))$ describing how pixels at spatial coordinates $(x,y)$ move from frame $I(x,y,t)$ to frame $I(x,y,t+1)$.

In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, optical flow is a *low-level vision primitive* used as the front-end for motion segmentation, object tracking, action recognition, ego-motion estimation, and Structure from Motion (SfM).

## 1.2 Lucas–Kanade Method — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Lucas–Kanade (LK) Optical Flow Method** (1981) is a *differential, gradient-based, local estimation technique* that computes the optical flow vector at a pixel by solving the brightness constancy constraint over a *small spatial neighbourhood* under the assumption that all pixels in the window share the *same* displacement vector.

It belongs to the family of **sparse optical flow** estimators (in contrast to dense variational methods like Horn–Schunck).

## 1.3 Structure from Motion — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structure from Motion (SfM)** is the process of *recovering the 3D structure of a scene and the camera poses (motion)* simultaneously from a set of 2D image observations taken from different viewpoints. It is the geometric dual of the triangulation problem.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### 🌊 Analogy 1 — Lucas–Kanade: "Pebbles in a River"
Imagine looking down at a shallow stream where pebbles rest on the sandy bottom. The water surface ripples, but you can see the pebbles clearly. If a leaf drifts across, you can track its velocity because you see the *whole leaf* moving. Now imagine a *single featureless white line* painted on the bottom — you cannot tell whether it is sliding along its length or rotating in place; only the *component of motion perpendicular to the line* is observable. This is exactly the **aperture problem**. Lucas–Kanade overcomes it by looking at a *patch of pixels together* — like watching many pebbles at once and voting for the *common* velocity.

### 🏛️ Analogy 2 — Structure from Motion: "The Living Statue"
You stand in a museum in front of a statue and take 30 photos while walking around it. Your two eyes, your different head positions, and the changing parallax give you the *3D shape*. SfM is the *automatic, mathematical version* of this process: from 2D photographs, the algorithm simultaneously figures out *where each photo was taken from* (camera pose) and *what the 3D shape of the statue looks like* (point cloud).

## 1.5 Notation Standard for the Module

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $I(x,y,t)$ | Image brightness at pixel $(x,y)$, time $t$ |
| $u, v$ | Horizontal, vertical components of optical flow |
| $I_x, I_y, I_t$ | Partial derivatives of image intensity |
| $\mathbf{A}$ | Matrix of spatial gradients stacked over a window |
| $P_1, P_2$ | Camera projection matrices |
| $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ | Rotation matrix and translation vector |
| $W$ | Measurement (trajectory) matrix in factorization |
| $M, S$ | Motion and Shape matrices |
| $\mathbf{F}, \mathbf{E}$ | Fundamental and Essential matrices |

> [!NOTE]
> Throughout this module, image coordinates follow the **KTU/Kanatani convention**: $x$ points right, $y$ points *down*, homogeneous vectors are written with a tilde $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$, and camera projection matrices are $3 \times 4$ denoted $P = K[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Assumptions of Lucas–Kanade

The LK algorithm rests on **three pillars**, each of which KTU examiners love to test:

### Assumption 1 — Brightness Constancy
The brightness (intensity) of a small image patch does not change as it moves between frames.

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\,\Delta t, \ y + v\,\Delta t, \ t + \Delta t)$$

### Assumption 2 — Small Motion (Temporal Persistence)
The inter-frame displacement is small (sub-pixel to a few pixels), so a first-order Taylor expansion is valid.

### Assumption 3 — Spatial Coherence (Neighbourhood Constancy)
All pixels inside a small spatial window $\Omega$ centred on the query pixel share the *same* flow vector $(u,v)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these assumptions matter:**
> - Brightness constancy converts motion into a derivative equation.
> - Small motion justifies linearization (otherwise we'd need iterative warping).
> - Spatial coherence turns one equation with two unknowns into an *over-determined* system solvable by least squares.

## 2.2 Deriving the Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)

Starting from brightness constancy and Taylor-expanding about $(x,y,t)$:

$$I(x+u, y+v, t+1) \approx I(x,y,t) + I_x u + I_y v + I_t$$

Substituting back into the constancy equation and dropping higher-order terms gives the canonical **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)**:

$$I_x\, u + I_y\, v + I_t = 0$$

This is a single scalar equation in **two** unknowns $(u,v)$ — hence the aperture problem.

## 2.3 The Lucas–Kanade Linear System

For a window of $n$ pixels $\mathbf{p}_i \in \Omega$, stacking the OFCE for each yields:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
I_x(\mathbf{p}_1) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_1) \\
I_x(\mathbf{p}_2) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_2) \\
\vdots & \vdots \\
I_x(\mathbf{p}_n) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_n)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-I_t(\mathbf{p}_1) \\
-I_t(\mathbf{p}_2) \\
\vdots \\
-I_t(\mathbf{p}_n)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Compactly: $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{b}$. Solving by **least squares**:

$$\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

with the $2 \times 2$ structure tensor:

$$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.4 Iterative / Pyramidal Lucas–Kanade (KLT Tracker)

When motion is *not* small, the pure LK method fails. The solution is **image pyramids** (Bouguet, 2001):

1. Build Gaussian pyramid $\{I^0, I^1, \ldots, I^L\}$.
2. Initialise flow $\mathbf{v}_L = (0,0)$.
3. For level $\ell = L, L-1, \ldots, 0$:
   - Warp image $I^\ell$ by $\mathbf{v}_{\ell+1}$.
   - Compute LK increment $\delta \mathbf{v}$ on the warped image.
   - Update $\mathbf{v}_\ell = \mathbf{v}_{\ell+1} + \delta \mathbf{v}$.
4. Return $\mathbf{v}_0$.

## 2.5 Structure from Motion — Geometric Setup

Given two calibrated cameras with projection matrices $P_1 = K[I \mid 0]$ and $P_2 = K[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$, and a 3D point $\mathbf{X}$ observed as $\mathbf{x}_1$ and $\mathbf{x}_2$ in the two images, SfM solves two coupled problems:

1. **Motion recovery** — estimate $R, \mathbf{t}$ from $\mathbf{x}_1 \leftrightarrow \mathbf{x}_2$ correspondences.
2. **Triangulation** — estimate $\mathbf{X}$ given $P_1, P_2, \mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$.

### Epipolar Constraint
A 3D point projects into the second image along the *epipolar line*:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2^T \, \mathbf{F} \, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 = 0$$

where $\mathbf{F}$ is the **fundamental matrix** for uncalibrated cameras and the **essential matrix** for calibrated:

$$\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}, \quad \mathbf{F} = K^{-T} \mathbf{E} K^{-1}$$

## 2.6 Tomasi–Kanade Factorization (Orthographic SfM)

For an orthographic camera and $F$ frames tracking $P$ feature points, the *measurement matrix* is:

$$W_{2F \times P} = M_{2F \times 3} \cdot S_{3 \times P}$$

where $M$ stacks the 2D motion of each frame and $S$ stacks the 3D coordinates of each point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rank Theorem:** Because $W$ is the product of a $2F \times 3$ matrix and a $3 \times P$ matrix, $\text{rank}(W) \leq 3$. This is the central observation enabling Tomasi–Kanade factorization.

The reconstruction is obtained from the SVD $W = U \Sigma V^T$:

$$M = U_3 \sqrt{\Sigma_3}, \qquad S = \sqrt{\Sigma_3}\, V_3^T$$

with $U_3, V_3$ the first 3 singular vectors.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Brightness Constancy | $I(x,y,t) = I(x+u, y+v, t+1)$ | First pillar of LK |
| Optical Flow Constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Linear in $u, v$ |
| LK Linear System | $A \mathbf{v} = -\mathbf{b}$ | $A$ is $n \times 2$ |
| LK Closed Form | $\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | Least-squares |
| Structure Tensor | $\sum_{\Omega} \nabla I (\nabla I)^T$ | Symmetric $2 \times 2$ |
| Harris Corner Test | $\det(M) - k\,\text{trace}(M)^2 > \tau$ | Good LK points |
| Optical Flow Norm | $\vert \mathbf{v} \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ | Magnitude in px/frame |
| Epipolar Constraint | $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2^T F \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 = 0$ | 8-point algorithm |
| Essential Matrix | $E = [t]_\times R$ | $5$ DOF $(R,t)$ |
| Triangulation (DLT) | $\mathbf{x}_i \times (P_i \mathbf{X}) = 0$ | 4 equations / point |
| Measurement Matrix | $W = MS$ | Orthographic SfM |
| Rank Constraint | $\text{rank}(W) \leq 3$ | Factorization gate |
| Reprojection Error | $e = \sum_i \vert \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i - P_i \mathbf{X} \vert^2$ | BA objective |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles**: visual odometry using LK + epipolar geometry.
- **AR/VR headsets**: 6-DoF tracking using pyramidal KLT.
- **Film & VFX**: matchmoving (e.g., Boujou, PFTrack).
- **Medical Imaging**: cardiac motion tracking from ultrasound.
- **Aerial photogrammetry**: large-scale SfM pipelines (COLMAP, OpenMVG) used in archaeology, agriculture, and digital twins.
- **SLAM front-ends**: ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono, PTAM all borrow LK-style tracking principles.

# 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Brightness Constancy to the OFCE

We begin with the brightness constancy assumption applied to a pixel that moves by $(u, v)$ over one frame:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u, \ y + v, \ t + \Delta t)$$

**Step 1** — Apply a first-order Taylor expansion to the right-hand side about the point $(x, y, t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x + u, \, y + v, \, t + \Delta t) &= I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\, u + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\, v + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\, \Delta t + \text{h.o.t.} \\
&= I(x,y,t) + I_x u + I_y v + I_t\, \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute back into the constancy equation and cancel $I(x,y,t)$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x,y,t) &= I(x,y,t) + I_x u + I_y v + I_t\, \Delta t \\
0 &= I_x u + I_y v + I_t\, \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Absorb the unit-time convention $\Delta t = 1$ into the derivative definition. The Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE) emerges:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

> **Why this is one equation in two unknowns:** The brightness of a *single* pixel only constrains the component of motion *along* its intensity gradient $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)^T$. The orthogonal component is invisible — this is the **aperture problem**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form Lucas–Kanade Solution

Consider a window $\Omega$ of $n$ pixels. Writing the OFCE at each pixel $\mathbf{p}_i$:

$$I_x(\mathbf{p}_i) u + I_y(\mathbf{p}_i) v = -I_t(\mathbf{p}_i), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n$$

**Step 1** — Stack the constraints vertically:

$$
\underbrace{
\begin{bmatrix}
I_x(\mathbf{p}_1) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_1) \\
I_x(\mathbf{p}_2) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_2) \\
\vdots & \vdots \\
I_x(\mathbf{p}_n) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_n)
\end{bmatrix}
}_{A \ (n \times 2)}
\underbrace{
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
}_{\mathbf{v}}
=
\underbrace{
\begin{bmatrix}
-I_t(\mathbf{p}_1) \\
-I_t(\mathbf{p}_2) \\
\vdots \\
-I_t(\mathbf{p}_n)
\end{bmatrix}
}_{\mathbf{b}}
$$

**Step 2** — The system is over-determined ($n \gg 2$). Minimise the squared residual $J(\mathbf{v}) = \vert A\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b} \vert^2$. Setting $\partial J/\partial \mathbf{v} = 0$ yields the normal equations:

$$2 A^T (A \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A^T A \, \mathbf{v} = A^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 3** — Assuming $\det(A^T A) \neq 0$ (the window contains *corner-like* structure), invert:

$$\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 4** — Expand $A^T A$ explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^T A &= \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x(\mathbf{p}_i)^2 & \sum I_x(\mathbf{p}_i) I_y(\mathbf{p}_i) \\ \sum I_x(\mathbf{p}_i) I_y(\mathbf{p}_i) & \sum I_y(\mathbf{p}_i)^2 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x, I_x \rangle & \langle I_x, I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x, I_y \rangle & \langle I_y, I_y \rangle \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ denotes the inner product over the window $\Omega$.

> **Geometric insight:** $A^T A$ is the *structure tensor* (a.k.a. second-moment matrix). Its eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ measure image gradient energy along the two principal directions. LK requires $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \gg 0$ — i.e., the window must be a **Harris corner**, not an edge. Hence the famous LK-to-Harris pairing in KLT trackers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Iterative Lucas–Kanade Refinement

The pure LK method assumes $(u, v)$ is small. To handle larger motions, iterate:

**Step 1** — At iteration $k$, compute a *residual* image $\delta I^{(k)}$:

$$\delta I^{(k)}(\mathbf{x}) = I(\mathbf{x}, t+1) - I(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{v}^{(k)}, t)$$

**Step 2** — Compute the gradient of the *first* image and apply LK to estimate $\eta^{(k)}$:

$$\eta^{(k)} = \left( \sum_{\Omega} \nabla I \nabla I^T \right)^{-1} \sum_{\Omega} \nabla I \cdot \delta I^{(k)}$$

**Step 3** — Update the flow estimate:

$$\mathbf{v}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{v}^{(k)} + \eta^{(k)}$$

**Step 4** — Iterate until $\vert \eta^{(k)} \vert$ falls below a tolerance $\epsilon$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Triangulation via Direct Linear Transform (DLT)

Given $P_1, P_2$ and observed $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2$, find $\mathbf{X}$.

**Step 1** — In homogeneous coordinates, the projection is $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i \sim P_i \mathbf{X}$, i.e., $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i \times (P_i \mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 2** — Writing out the cross-product for $i = 1$ (and similarly for $i = 2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 \times (P_1 \mathbf{X}) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -x_1^w & x_1^v \\ x_1^w & 0 & -x_1^u \\ -x_1^v & x_1^u & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P_1^{1T} \mathbf{X} \\ P_1^{2T} \mathbf{X} \\ P_1^{3T} \mathbf{X} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Drop one dependent row per image to obtain 4 linear equations in 4 unknowns (homogeneous $\mathbf{X}$):

$$\mathcal{A} \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4** — Solve as the right singular vector of $\mathcal{A}$ corresponding to the smallest singular value via SVD.

**Step 5** — Convert to inhomogeneous form by dividing by the last coordinate:

$$\mathbf{X}_{\text{eucl}} = \frac{1}{X_4} \begin{bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \\ X_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Tomasi–Kanade Factorization (Orthographic)

Consider $F$ frames tracking $P$ points under scaled orthographic projection. Let $(u_{f,p}, v_{f,p})$ be the 2D observation of point $p$ in frame $f$, and let the mean across frames be $(\bar{u}_p, \bar{v}_p)$. De-mean to remove translation:

$$\tilde{u}_{f,p} = u_{f,p} - \bar{u}_p, \quad \tilde{v}_{f,p} = v_{f,p} - \bar{v}_p$$

**Step 1** — Form the measurement matrix $W$ of size $2F \times P$ by stacking rows $(\tilde{u}_{f,1}, \ldots, \tilde{u}_{f,P}, \tilde{v}_{f,1}, \ldots, \tilde{v}_{f,P})$ for $f = 1, \ldots, F$.

**Step 2** — Under orthographic projection the de-meaned measurement is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u}_{f,p} &= \mathbf{i}_f \cdot (\mathbf{s}_p - \bar{\mathbf{s}}) \\
\tilde{v}_{f,p} &= \mathbf{j}_f \cdot (\mathbf{s}_p - \bar{\mathbf{s}})
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{i}_f, \mathbf{j}_f$ are the image-plane basis vectors of frame $f$ and $\mathbf{s}_p$ is the 3D point. Define the $2 \times 3$ motion matrix $M_f = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{i}_f^T \\ \mathbf{j}_f^T \end{bmatrix}$ and shape $S = [\mathbf{s}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{s}_P]$.

**Step 3** — Concatenate across frames:

$$W_{2F \times P} = M_{2F \times 3} \cdot S_{3 \times P}$$

**Step 4** — Rank observation: $\text{rank}(W) \leq \min(\text{rank}(M), \text{rank}(S)) \leq 3$.

**Step 5** — Compute thin SVD: $W = U \Sigma V^T$ with $U \in \mathbb{R}^{2F \times 2F}$, $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{2F \times P}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{P \times P}$.

**Step 6** — Truncate to rank 3:

$$\hat{W} = U_3 \, \Sigma_3 \, V_3^T$$

**Step 7** — Recover motion and shape up to a $3 \times 3$ affine ambiguity $Q$:

$$M = U_3 \sqrt{\Sigma_3} \, Q, \qquad S = Q^{-1} \sqrt{\Sigma_3} \, V_3^T$$

**Step 8** — Resolve the affine ambiguity by enforcing metric constraints: rows of $M$ must be orthonormal and scaled appropriately. This yields a closed-form upgrade to a Euclidean reconstruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rank ≤ 3?** All $2F$ rows of $W$ live in a 3-dimensional space spanned by the $x, y, z$ axes of the world. The algebraic rank constraint is the heart of the Tomasi–Kanade (TK) theorem and the basis of all modern factorization-based SfM.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Tracking Method

| Method | Window Size | Motion Range | Cost | Failure Modes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pure LK | $5 \times 5$ – $25 \times 25$ | < 1 px | Very low | Large motion, edges |
| Iterative LK | $5 \times 5$ – $25 \times 25$ | ≈ 5 px | Low | Drift |
| Pyramidal LK | $5 \times 5$ – $25 \times 25$ | ≈ 20 px | Medium | Specular / textureless |
| Horn–Schunck | Global | < 5 px | Medium | Smoothness bias |
| Farnebäck | Local polynomial | ≈ 10 px | Medium-High | Textureless regions |
| DeepFlow / FlowNet | Learned | Large | High | Out-of-distribution |

# 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lucas–Kanade Optical Flow Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

The diagram below captures the complete information flow of the pyramidal LK method. Each node is an *engineered computational block* with explicit data contracts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Frame I_t] --> C[Build Gaussian Pyramid Level 0 to L]
    B[Next Frame I_t+1] --> C
    C --> D[Initialise Flow Vector v_L equals 0,0]
    D --> E[For level equals L down to 0]
    E --> F[Warp I_t+1 by current v]
    F --> G[Compute Spatial Gradients I_x and I_y]
    G --> H[Compute Temporal Residual delta I]
    H --> I[Form Matrix A from gradients]
    I --> J[Compute Structure Tensor A_T times A]
    J --> K{Eigenvalues of A_T A greater than threshold}
    K -- Yes --> L[Solve 2 by 2 System v equals A_T A inverse A_T b]
    K -- No --> M[Mark Point as Untrackable]
    L --> N[Update Flow v equals v plus delta v]
    N --> O{Converged below epsilon}
    O -- No --> E
    O -- Yes --> P[Output Final Flow Field]
    M --> P
```

## 4.2 Structure from Motion End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Image Set I_1 to I_F] --> B[Feature Detection - SIFT or ORB]
    B --> C[Pairwise Feature Matching]
    C --> D[Estimate Essential Matrix E via 8 point algorithm]
    D --> E[Decompose E into R and t]
    E --> F{Triangulation or Factorization}
    F --> G[DLT Triangulation]
    F --> H[Tomasi Kanade Factorization]
    G --> I[Initial 3D Point Cloud]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Bundle Adjustment - Non linear Refinement]
    J --> K[Final 3D Structure plus Camera Poses]
    K --> L[Optional Dense Reconstruction via MVS]
```

## 4.3 Structure Tensor Geometry — Aperture Problem Visualised

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Image Patch with Edge] --> B[Gradient Direction nabla I along x]
    B --> C[Observable Motion along gradient]
    B --> D[Ambiguous Motion parallel to edge - aperture problem]
    C --> E[LK Window has gradients in two directions]
    D --> F[LK Window collapses - unobservable direction]
    E --> G[Full 2D Velocity Recovered]
    F --> H[Only Normal Flow Estimated]
```

## 4.4 SfM Algebraic Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[2F by P Measurement Matrix W] --> B[SVD of W equals U Sigma V_T]
    B --> C[Truncate to Rank 3]
    C --> D[M equals U_3 times sqrt Sigma_3]
    C --> E[S equals sqrt Sigma_3 times V_3_T]
    D --> F[Enforce Metric Constraints]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Recovered Motion M]
    F --> H[Recovered Shape S]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:**
> - Rectangles = data containers / images.
> - Diamonds = decision gates (with thresholds).
> - Hexagons = solver blocks.
> - Arrows labelled with intermediate variables indicate the *exact* mathematical object passed between modules — KTU examiners expect you to recognise these contracts (e.g., $W \to \text{SVD} \to M, S$).
