# 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. State the brightness constancy assumption and the optical flow constraint equation. Why does the OFCE alone not yield a unique solution for $(u, v)$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Brightness constancy assumption:** The intensity of any small image region is invariant under motion between consecutive frames. Formally:
> $$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u, y + v, t + \Delta t)$$
>
> **Derivation of OFCE (Taylor expansion):** Applying a first-order Taylor expansion and discarding higher-order terms:
> $$0 = I_x u + I_y v + I_t$$
>
> **Why it is under-determined (1 mark):** The OFCE is a *single* scalar equation in *two* unknowns $(u, v)$. It only constrains the component of motion along the local intensity gradient $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)^T$. The orthogonal component — the so-called **normal flow** direction — remains ambiguous. This is the **aperture problem**, and motivates the use of spatial coherence (Lucas–Kanade) or regularisation (Horn–Schunck).

---

### Q2. Define the aperture problem and explain how the Lucas–Kanade method overcomes it. Mention the role of the structure tensor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Aperture problem (1 mark):** When the local image neighbourhood contains gradient information in only *one* direction (e.g., along an edge or bar), only the *normal component* of motion (perpendicular to the edge) can be recovered; motion along the edge direction is invisible to a small aperture.
>
> **LK's solution (1 mark):** Lucas–Kanade imposes a *spatial coherence* constraint — that all pixels in a window $\Omega$ share the same flow vector. This turns one equation in two unknowns into an over-determined system $A\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{b}$ with $n \gg 2$ equations, solved by least squares $\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$.
>
> **Role of the structure tensor (1 mark):** The matrix $A^T A$ is the **structure tensor** (second-moment matrix). Its two eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ measure the spread of gradients in the two orthogonal directions. When both eigenvalues are large, the patch is a *corner-like* region and LK yields a reliable full 2D velocity. When one eigenvalue is small, the patch is an *edge* and only normal flow can be recovered — the system becomes ill-conditioned.

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Sub-Parts)

### ⭐ Question A — Lucas–Kanade Focus (14 Marks)

#### (a) Starting from the brightness constancy assumption, derive the optical flow constraint equation. Show all Taylor-series steps. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the brightness constancy assumption in functional form:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\,\Delta t, \ y + v\,\Delta t, \ t + \Delta t)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply a first-order Taylor expansion to the right-hand side about the point $(x, y, t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x + u\,\Delta t, \, y + v\,\Delta t, \, t + \Delta t) &= I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\, u\,\Delta t + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\, v\,\Delta t + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\, \Delta t + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2) \\
&\approx I(x,y,t) + I_x\, u\,\Delta t + I_y\, v\,\Delta t + I_t\, \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equate with the left-hand side and cancel $I(x,y,t)$:

$$0 = I_x\, u\,\Delta t + I_y\, v\,\Delta t + I_t\, \Delta t$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Divide through by $\Delta t \neq 0$:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** **Geometric interpretation.** Rearrange as a dot product:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} = -I_t, \quad \text{where} \quad \nabla I = \begin{bmatrix} I_x \\ I_y \end{bmatrix}, \ \mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}$$

The OFCE therefore constrains the projection of velocity *onto the image gradient*. The *normal flow* component is:

$$\mathbf{v}_n = -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert^2} \nabla I$$

The tangential component remains **unobservable** from a single pixel — this is the aperture problem. The OFCE is the foundational equation on which all gradient-based optical flow methods (LK, Horn–Schunck, Farnebäck) are built.

---

#### (b) Derive the closed-form Lucas–Kanade solution. Explain why LK requires Harris-like corners. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a window $\Omega$ with $n$ pixels, stack the OFCE for each pixel $\mathbf{p}_i \in \Omega$:

$$I_x(\mathbf{p}_i)\, u + I_y(\mathbf{p}_i)\, v = -I_t(\mathbf{p}_i), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write this in matrix form $A\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{b}$ where:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} I_x(\mathbf{p}_1) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(\mathbf{p}_n) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_n) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -I_t(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ \vdots \\ -I_t(\mathbf{p}_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Minimise the squared residual $J(\mathbf{v}) = (A\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b})^T (A\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b})$. Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{v}} &= 2 A^T A \, \mathbf{v} - 2 A^T \mathbf{b} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow A^T A \, \mathbf{v} &= A^T \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** If $A^T A$ is invertible, solve directly:

$$\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Expand the $2 \times 2$ structure tensor:

$$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum_{\Omega} I_x^2 & \sum_{\Omega} I_x I_y \\ \sum_{\Omega} I_x I_y & \sum_{\Omega} I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

- If the window has **strong gradients in both directions** (a corner), both eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$, $A^T A$ is well-conditioned, and the inverse exists — LK recovers a *reliable* full 2D velocity.
- If the window contains an **edge**, one eigenvalue $\approx 0$, $A^T A$ is rank-deficient, the inverse is unstable, and LK recovers only the **normal flow** — the tangential direction is unobservable (aperture problem persists).

Therefore, **good LK points must be Harris corners**, not edges. This is why classical KLT trackers run a Harris detector first and use only the top corner responses as feature points.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Structure from Motion Focus (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the Structure from Motion pipeline. State the role of the essential matrix and describe the eight-point algorithm. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **SfM definition.** SfM recovers 3D scene structure and camera motion jointly from 2D image correspondences. Pipeline stages:

1. Feature detection (SIFT, ORB, SURF)
2. Feature matching across image pairs
3. Motion estimation (Essential / Fundamental matrix)
4. Triangulation / Factorization
5. Bundle adjustment

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Epipolar geometry.** A 3D point $\mathbf{X}$ projects to $\mathbf{x}_1$ in camera 1 and $\mathbf{x}_2$ in camera 2. The corresponding rays must intersect, giving the epipolar constraint:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2^T \mathbf{E} \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 = 0$$

For calibrated cameras, the **essential matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ has rank 2 and 5 DOF (3 for $R$, 2 for $\mathbf{t}$ up to scale).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Eight-point algorithm** (Longuet-Higgins, 1981):
- Stack 8 correspondences $(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1^i, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2^i)$ into a linear system $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$ where $\mathbf{e}$ is the 9-vector form of $E$.
- Solve by SVD: $\mathbf{e}$ = right singular vector of $A$ corresponding to the smallest singular value.
- Reshape to $3 \times 3$ and enforce the rank-2 constraint by zeroing the smallest singular value of $E$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Pose recovery.** Decompose $E = U \text{diag}(1,1,0) V^T$ into four $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ candidates; disambiguate using the **chirality check** — reconstructed points must lie in front of both cameras.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Triangulation.** For each correspondence, compute 3D point using the DLT or mid-point method, producing an initial point cloud.

---

#### (b) Derive the Tomasi–Kanade factorization for orthographic SfM. Explain the rank theorem and the role of the SVD. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Setup.** Consider $F$ orthographic frames tracking $P$ feature points with 2D observations $(u_{f,p}, v_{f,p})$. De-mean each point's trajectory across frames to remove camera translation:

$$\tilde{u}_{f,p} = u_{f,p} - \bar{u}_p, \quad \tilde{v}_{f,p} = v_{f,p} - \bar{v}_p$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Form the measurement matrix** $W$ of size $2F \times P$ by stacking de-meaned rows. Under orthographic projection with image-plane basis vectors $\mathbf{i}_f, \mathbf{j}_f$ and 3D point $\mathbf{s}_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u}_{f,p} &= \mathbf{i}_f^T \mathbf{s}_p, \\
\tilde{v}_{f,p} &= \mathbf{j}_f^T \mathbf{s}_p
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenating across frames gives the matrix factorisation:

$$W_{2F \times P} = M_{2F \times 3} \, S_{3 \times P}$$

where $M$ is the **motion matrix** (rows $\mathbf{i}_f^T, \mathbf{j}_f^T$) and $S$ is the **shape matrix** (columns $\mathbf{s}_p$).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Rank theorem.** Because $W$ is the product of a $2F \times 3$ matrix and a $3 \times P$ matrix:

$$\text{rank}(W) \leq \min(\text{rank}(M), \text{rank}(S)) \leq 3$$

This is the central algebraic constraint that makes factorisation possible.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **SVD reconstruction.** Compute thin SVD $W = U \Sigma V^T$ and truncate to the top 3 singular triplets:

$$\hat{W} = U_3 \Sigma_3 V_3^T, \quad U_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{2F \times 3}, \ \Sigma_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}, \ V_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{P \times 3}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Recover motion and shape** up to an arbitrary $3 \times 3$ affine ambiguity $Q$:

$$M = U_3 \sqrt{\Sigma_3} \, Q, \qquad S = Q^{-1} \sqrt{\Sigma_3} \, V_3^T$$

> **Step 6 (in commentary):** Resolve the affine ambiguity by enforcing orthonormality constraints on the rows of $M$, yielding a unique Euclidean reconstruction up to a global rotation, translation, and scale.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Confusing orthographic with perspective factorization:** perspective (Tomasi–Kanade variant or Sturm–Triggs) does *not* enjoy the clean $W = MS$ form. Always state the camera model before writing the factorisation.
> - **Forgetting to de-mean the measurement matrix:** without de-meaning, $W$ has rank $4$ (3 for shape + 1 for translation), and the SVD truncation to rank 3 becomes mathematically wrong.
> - **Inverting $A^T A$ blindly:** the inverse exists only when $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$. On an edge, the matrix is singular — this is *not* a bug, it is the algebraically rigorous statement of the aperture problem. KTU examiners reward the recognition of this degeneracy.
> - **Confusing $F$ (fundamental) with $E$ (essential):** $F$ is for uncalibrated cameras ($7$ DOF), $E$ is for calibrated ($5$ DOF). Mixing them in derivations costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Missing the global scale ambiguity:** SfM reconstructions are only defined up to a similarity transform (rotation + translation + scale). KTU frequently asks: *"Why is the scale unrecoverable from monocular images alone?"* — answer: because image projection is invariant to simultaneous scaling of $\mathbf{t}$ and the 3D scene.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **brightness constancy assumption** $I(x,y,t) = I(x+u, y+v, t+1)$ is the foundation of differential optical flow.
- ✅ Taylor expansion gives the **OFCE**: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$.
- ✅ The OFCE is **one equation in two unknowns** — the aperture problem.
- ✅ **Lucas–Kanade** adds *spatial coherence* over a window $\Omega$ and solves $\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$.
- ✅ $A^T A$ is the **structure tensor**; both eigenvalues must be large (corner condition).
- ✅ **Pyramidal LK** handles large motions via image pyramids + iterative refinement.
- ✅ **KLT** = Harris corners + pyramidal LK with affine illumination compensation.
- ✅ **Normal flow** is $\mathbf{v}_n = -\frac{I_t}{\vert \nabla I \vert^2} \nabla I$; only the gradient-aligned component of motion.
- ✅ **SfM** = joint recovery of 3D structure + camera motion from 2D image correspondences.
- ✅ The **epipolar constraint** $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2^T E \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 = 0$ reduces correspondence search to a 1D line.
- ✅ **Essential matrix** $E = [\mathbf{t}]_\times R$ has 5 DOF; recovered via the **eight-point algorithm** with SVD.
- ✅ **Triangulation** solves $\mathbf{x}_i \times (P_i \mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{0}$ via DLT + SVD.
- ✅ **Tomasi–Kanade factorization** uses $W_{2F \times P} = M_{2F \times 3} S_{3 \times P}$ with $\text{rank}(W) \leq 3$.
- ✅ Reconstruction is unique only **up to a similarity transform** (rotation + translation + scale).
- ✅ **Bundle Adjustment** jointly refines all camera poses and 3D points by minimising total reprojection error $\sum_i \vert \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i - P_i \mathbf{X} \vert^2$ using non-linear least squares (Levenberg–Marquardt).
- ✅ Common KTU one-liners: *"OFCE is rank-deficient", "LK fails on edges", "SfM has a global scale ambiguity", "Trifocal tensor generalises epipolar to three views"*.
- ✅ Practical trackers (KLT, ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono) all descend from the LK + epipolar pipeline studied here.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU mnemonic — "B-S-S" for LK:**
> **B**rightness constancy → linearise → **S**patial coherence (window) → **S**tructure tensor must have two big eigenvalues. Memorise this and you cannot lose the 7-mark derivation question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scene Analysis, Examples of Real-Time Applications: In-Vehicle Vision System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET754 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scene Analysis, Examples of Real-Time Applications: In-Vehicle Vision System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Scene Analysis – Formal Definition

**Scene Analysis** is the high-level phase of computer vision in which a 2D image (or a stream of images) is interpreted to recover a *structured, semantic, and geometric description* of the depicted environment. It moves beyond "what pixels are present" to "what is happening, where, and how the elements relate."

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, scene analysis encompasses:

- **Semantic understanding** – assigning class labels to pixels, regions, or objects.
- **Geometric understanding** – recovering 3D structure (depth, pose, layout) from 2D projections.
- **Temporal understanding** – tracking how the scene evolves across frames.
- **Contextual / relational understanding** – modeling interactions between objects (the *who–what–where–why* of a scene).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> Scene analysis is the *interpretation* layer of computer vision. The pipeline progresses as:
> **Image Acquisition → Pre-processing → Low-level (edges, corners) → Mid-level (segments, features) → High-level (objects, 3D, semantics) → Scene Understanding.**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Brain of a Robot Tourist"

Imagine a foreign tourist stepping into a busy Indian market. Their brain instantly performs **scene analysis**:

1. *What* objects are there? (stalls, vehicles, people, signs)
2. *Where* are they in 3D space? (closer/farther, left/right)
3. *How* are they related? (a person is *riding* a bike, the bike is *next to* a car)
4. *What is happening*? (traffic congestion, a vendor selling fruit)

A computer-vision system does the same, but with **cameras** instead of eyes, **matrices** instead of neurons, and **millions of parameters** instead of memory.

## 1.3 The Sub-Tasks of Scene Analysis (Taxonomy)

| Sub-Task | Output Type | Example Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **Image Classification** | Single label | "Is this a highway or an urban street?" |
| **Object Detection** | Bounding boxes + class | "Where are the cars and pedestrians?" |
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Per-pixel class label | "Which pixels are road, sky, building?" |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Per-pixel + instance ID | "Separate each individual car." |
| **Depth Estimation** | Per-pixel depth map | "How far is the pedestrian?" |
| **Pose Estimation** | 3D joint/keypoint locations | "Where is the driver's head tilted?" |
| **Scene Graph Generation** | (object, relation, object) triples | "Person *riding* bicycle, bicycle *on* road." |
| **3D Scene Reconstruction** | Point cloud / mesh / occupancy grid | "What does the road layout look like in 3D?" |
| **Tracking** | Object IDs across frames | "Follow that pedestrian across frames." |
| **Action/Event Recognition** | Activity label | "Is the driver about to change lane?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Scene Graph:**
> A **Scene Graph** is a structured representation $G = (V, E)$ where each vertex $v_i \in V$ is an object (with attributes: class, bounding box, mask) and each edge $e_{ij} \in E$ is a predicate describing a relationship (e.g., *holding*, *above*, *inside*).

## 1.4 In-Vehicle Vision System – Formal Definition

An **In-Vehicle Vision System (IVVS)** is a real-time, safety-critical embedded perception system mounted on a vehicle that uses cameras (and often fused with **LiDAR**, **radar**, **ultrasonic**, and **IMU** sensors) to perceive the vehicle's surroundings and the driver's state, enabling Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Driving (AD) functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (must know for KTU viva):**
> - **Latency** $\leq$ **100 ms** end-to-end (some functions require $\leq$ **30 ms**)
> - **Frame rate** $\geq$ **30 FPS** (often 60 FPS for high-speed scenarios)
> - **Operating temperature**: **$-40^\circ C$ to $+85^\circ C$**
> - **Detection range**: up to **250 m** for forward-looking cameras
> - **Functional Safety**: ISO **26262** ASIL-B to ASIL-D
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scene Analysis – Operational Pipeline

A modern scene-analysis pipeline executes the following stages:

1. **Acquisition** – Synchronized multi-camera capture (front, surround, in-cabin) with timestamps from the vehicle CAN bus.
2. **Calibration & Rectification** – Compute intrinsic ($K$) and extrinsic ($[R \mid t]$) parameters; remove lens distortion.
3. **Pre-processing** – Denoising, HDR synthesis (handling tunnels/exits), motion deblurring.
4. **Low-level Feature Extraction** – Edges (Canny, Sobel), corners (Harris), optical flow (LK, Farneback).
5. **Mid-level Processing** – Feature matching, stereo correspondence, super-pixel segmentation.
6. **High-level Inference** – Deep CNN/Transformer models produce detections, segmentations, depth.
7. **Tracking & Fusion** – Multi-object tracking (SORT, DeepSORT, Kalman filter) + sensor fusion (EKF/UKF).
8. **Scene Reasoning** – Scene graphs, occupancy grids, intention prediction.
9. **Decision Output** – Warnings, braking, steering commands (sent to ECU).

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Pinhole Camera Model

A 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^\top$ projects to a 2D pixel $\mathbf{x} = (u, v, 1)^\top$ via:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the intrinsic matrix $K$ is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

with $f_x, f_y$ as focal lengths (in pixels), $c_x, c_y$ as principal point, and $\gamma$ as skew (often 0).

### 2.2.2 Stereo Depth from Disparity

For a rectified stereo pair with baseline $B$ and focal length $f$:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

where $d = x_L - x_R$ is the horizontal disparity. The **disparity resolution** at distance $Z$ is:

$$\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d$$

> **Engineering insight:** Disparity is *inversely proportional* to depth → near objects have large disparity and easier to detect; far objects have tiny disparity and are noisy.

### 2.2.3 Epipolar Geometry

For two views, corresponding points $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{x}'$ must satisfy the **epipolar constraint**:

$$\mathbf{x}'^\top F \mathbf{x} = 0$$

where $F$ is the $3 \times 3$ **fundamental matrix** (rank 2). The corresponding form using calibrated cameras uses the **essential matrix** $E$:

$$E = [t]_\times R, \quad \hat{\mathbf{x}}'^\top E \hat{\mathbf{x}} = 0$$

with $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = K^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ being the normalized coordinates.

### 2.2.4 Optical Flow (Lucas–Kanade)

Assuming **brightness constancy** and **small motion**, the optical flow $(u, v)$ at pixel $(x, y)$ satisfies:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

Solved over a $W \times W$ window with the **Lucas–Kanade** linear system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = - \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x I_t \\ \sum I_y I_t \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.2.5 Detection Quality Metrics

- **Intersection over Union (IoU)** between predicted box $B_p$ and ground truth $B_{gt}$:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$$

- **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** across $C$ classes and IoU thresholds:

$$\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{AP}_c$$

- **Precision & Recall**:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole projection | $s\mathbf{x} = K[R\mid t]\mathbf{X}$ | $K$: intrinsic; $[R\mid t]$: extrinsic |
| 2 | Stereo depth | $Z = fB/d$ | $f$: focal length (px), $B$: baseline (m), $d$: disparity (px), $Z$: depth (m) |
| 3 | Depth resolution | $\Delta Z = Z^2 \Delta d / (fB)$ | Units: m |
| 4 | Epipolar constraint | $\mathbf{x}'^\top F \mathbf{x} = 0$ | $F$: $3 \times 3$ fundamental matrix |
| 5 | Optical flow (LK) | $A^\top A \cdot \mathbf{v} = -A^\top \mathbf{b}$ | $A = [I_x, I_y]$, $\mathbf{v} = [u, v]^\top$ |
| 6 | IoU | $\text{IoU} = \vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert / \vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert$ | Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ |
| 7 | mAP | $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{C}\sum_c \text{AP}_c$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | Kalman Predict (state) | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B_k u_k$ | $F_k$: transition matrix |
| 9 | Kalman Update (gain) | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^\top (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^\top + R_k)^{-1}$ | $H_k$: measurement model, $R_k$: noise |
| 10 | Homography (DLT) | $\mathbf{x}' = H \mathbf{x}$, $\;H = K (R - t n^\top/d) K^{-1}$ | For planar scenes, $H$ is $3 \times 3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of $Z = fB/d$:** This is the *only* equation that turns two cheap cameras into a depth sensor. It is the bedrock of every ADAS **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** system that estimates time-to-collision as $TTC = Z / \dot{Z}$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **ADAS Level 1–2** (driver-assist): Vision supplies lane, sign, and obstacle cues.
- **ADAS Level 3+** (conditional automation): Vision becomes a *primary* perception modality; redundancy with LiDAR/radar is mandatory.
- **Cabin Monitoring**: Vision detects drowsiness, gaze distraction, seat-belt status, and even occupant pose.
- **V2X integration**: Vision outputs are broadcast to other vehicles and infrastructure for cooperative perception.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From 3D Point to 2D Pixel (Pinhole Model)

We derive the projection equation step by step.

**Step 1 — World to camera transformation.**
A 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^\top$ in homogeneous coordinates is mapped to the camera coordinate frame via the rigid-body transformation composed of rotation $R$ and translation $t$:

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^\top & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_w, \quad \mathbf{X}_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c, 1)^\top$$

*Interpretation:* $R$ aligns the world axes to the camera axes; $t$ shifts the origin to the camera's optical center.

**Step 2 — Perspective projection (ideal pinhole).**
The pinhole projects $\mathbf{X}_c$ onto the image plane at $Z_c = f$ by dividing out the $Z$ coordinate:

$$x_i = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_i = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

In homogeneous form with the canonical projection matrix $P_0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_i \\ y_i \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Incorporate intrinsic parameters.**
Real cameras have a sensor with offset $(c_x, c_y)$ and possibly different pixel pitch along axes, so we replace $f$ with $f_x, f_y$ and add the principal point:

$$\begin{aligned} u &= f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \\ v &= f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y \end{aligned}$$

This is the **intrinsic matrix** $K$ acting on the normalized point.

**Step 4 — Compose extrinsic and intrinsic.**
The full projection is the chain of linear maps:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{K}_{\text{intrinsic}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{extrinsic}} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Account for lens distortion (radial + tangential).**
The ideal coordinates are perturbed by:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{dist} &= x \left( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6 \right) + 2p_1 xy + p_2(r^2 + 2x^2) \\ y_{dist} &= y \left( 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6 \right) + p_1(r^2 + 2y^2) + 2p_2 xy \end{aligned}$$

where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$, and $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are radial, $p_1, p_2$ tangential distortion coefficients.

> **Physical meaning:** Steps 1–4 are linear and exact. Step 5 is a *non-linear* correction derived from a Taylor expansion of the lens's geometric aberration.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stereo Depth $Z = fB / d$

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider two cameras with parallel optical axes, separated by baseline $B$. Both have focal length $f$ and are perfectly rectified.

**Step 2 — Triangulation.** A 3D point $P$ at depth $Z$ projects to the left camera at $x_L = f X / Z$ (with $X$ measured from the left optical axis) and to the right camera at $x_R = f (X - B) / Z$ (since the right camera is shifted by $B$).

**Step 3 — Form the disparity.** Subtract the two equations:

$$x_L - x_R = f \frac{X}{Z} - f \frac{X - B}{Z} = \frac{f B}{Z}$$

Defining disparity $d \equiv x_L - x_R$:

$$d = \frac{fB}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Z$.** Inverting gives the **depth–disparity relation**:

$$Z = \frac{f B}{d}$$

**Step 5 — Depth uncertainty.** Differentiating with respect to $d$ (treating $f, B$ as constants):

$$\frac{dZ}{dd} = -\frac{fB}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{fB}$$

Hence the absolute depth error is:

$$\vert \Delta Z \vert = \frac{Z^2}{fB} \vert \Delta d \vert$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Doubling the baseline $B$ halves the depth error; doubling the focal length $f$ halves the error; doubling the distance $Z$ *quadruples* the error. This is why far-field depth is *intrinsically* harder.

## 3.3 Derivation: Optical Flow Constraint (Horn–Schunck / LK)

**Step 1 — Brightness constancy assumption.** A moving point retains the same intensity over a short interval $\Delta t$:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y, t + \Delta t)$$

**Step 2 — First-order Taylor expansion** of the right side around $(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y, t + \Delta t) \approx I + I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y + I_t \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Subtract** $I$ from both sides and divide by $\Delta t$:

$$I_x \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} + I_y \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta t} + I_t = 0$$

Define $u = \Delta x / \Delta t$ and $v = \Delta y / \Delta t$:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** — one equation in two unknowns — hence the **aperture problem**.

**Step 4 — Lucas–Kanade resolution.** Assume the flow is *constant* in a $W \times W$ neighborhood. Stack the OFCE for all $N = W^2$ pixels into a least-squares system:

$$\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} I_x(\mathbf{p}_1) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ I_x(\mathbf{p}_2) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x(\mathbf{p}_N) & I_y(\mathbf{p}_N) \end{bmatrix}}_{A} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{- \begin{bmatrix} I_t(\mathbf{p}_1) \\ I_t(\mathbf{p}_2) \\ \vdots \\ I_t(\mathbf{p}_N) \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{b}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve via normal equations** $A^\top A \mathbf{v} = -A^\top \mathbf{b}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = - \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x I_t \\ \sum I_y I_t \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solvability condition.** The $2 \times 2$ matrix $A^\top A$ must be invertible, i.e., both eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$. This requires the neighborhood to contain a *corner* — a *texture-rich* region — not a uniform patch or a single edge.

> **Vehicle use-case:** LK optical flow is the classical method behind **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** visual odometry and **visual SLAM front-ends** (e.g., ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Scene Analysis Methods

| Method | Input | Output | Strength | Weakness | Vehicle Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Segmentation (FCN, DeepLab)** | RGB | Per-pixel class | Dense, pixel-precise | Slow, class set fixed | Free-space / drivable area |
| **Object Detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN)** | RGB | Bboxes + class | Fast, reliable for known objects | No pixel mask | Vehicle / pedestrian detection |
| **Instance Segmentation (Mask R-CNN)** | RGB | Bboxes + masks | Distinguishes individuals | Heavier than detection | Counting pedestrians / cars |
| **Depth (MonoDepth, MiDaS)** | Mono RGB | Depth map | Single camera, cheap | Scale ambiguity | Obstacle proximity |
| **Stereo Depth (SGM, AANet)** | Stereo pair | Depth map | Metric, real-time | Baseline-limited FOV | AEB, ACC |
| **Optical Flow (LK, RAFT)** | 2 frames | Per-pixel motion | Ego-motion, scene flow | Computationally heavy | Ego-motion, motion segmentation |
| **Pose Estimation (OpenPose, HRNet)** | RGB | Skeleton joints | Driver monitoring | Occlusion sensitive | DMS (driver monitoring) |
| **Lane Detection (UFLD, SCNN)** | Front RGB | Lane curves | Robust to markings | Fails on worn roads | LKA, LDP |
| **Scene Graph (MotifNet, RelTR)** | RGB | Relational graph | High-level reasoning | Complex, slow | V2X, intent prediction |
| **3D Reconstruction (NeRF, 3D Gaussian Splatting)** | Multi-view RGB | Neural scene | Photo-realistic | Not real-time | HD-map generation offline |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Depth from Stereo

**Problem.** A vehicle's stereo camera has $f = 1200$ pixels, baseline $B = 0.30$ m. A pedestrian's feet are detected with disparity $d = 24$ pixels. The same pedestrian's head is detected with disparity $d = 18$ pixels. Compute the depth of the feet and head, and the pedestrian's approximate height in meters.

**Step 1 — Depth of feet.**

$$Z_{\text{feet}} = \frac{f B}{d_{\text{feet}}} = \frac{1200 \times 0.30}{24} = \frac{360}{24} = 15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Depth of head.**

$$Z_{\text{head}} = \frac{f B}{d_{\text{head}}} = \frac{1200 \times 0.30}{18} = \frac{360}{18} = 20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Height in world coordinates.** The head is *farther* than the feet by $\Delta Z = 20 - 15 = 5$ m. But that is *not* the height — the disparity difference arises from the *vertical pixel offset* between feet and head, not directly from height. The correct method uses the back-projection:

The head's vertical pixel coordinate $v_{\text{head}}$ relative to the principal point maps to a $Y$ world coordinate:

$$Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot Z}{f_y}$$

Using the same $f = 1200$ and assuming the head is, say, 60 pixels above the feet in the image and $c_y = 540$:

$$Y_{\text{feet}} = \frac{(540 - 540) \cdot 15}{1200} = 0 \text{ m}$$
$$Y_{\text{head}} = \frac{(480 - 540) \cdot 20}{1200} = \frac{-60 \times 20}{1200} = -1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Height** = $|Y_{\text{head}} - Y_{\text{feet}}| = 1.0$ m — physically reasonable for a pedestrian.

> **Examiner's note:** Students often confuse *disparity difference* with *height*. Always triangulate *each keypoint* separately in 3D, then compute height as the $Y$-distance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End In-Vehicle Vision Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Multi-Camera Array Front Wide Narrow Side Cabin] --> B[Synchronization Timestamping]
    B --> C[Calibration and Rectification]
    C --> D[Preprocessing HDR Denoise Deblur]
    D --> E[Deep CNN Backbone ResNet EfficientNet ViT]
    E --> F1[Object Detection YOLO]
    E --> F2[Semantic Segmentation]
    E --> F3[Depth Estimation]
    E --> F4[Lane and Free Space]
    E --> F5[Driver Monitoring Pose Gaze]
    F1 --> G[Multi-Object Tracker SORT DeepSORT]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Sensor Fusion LiDAR Radar EKF UKF]
    H --> I[Scene Graph and Intent Predictor]
    I --> J1[ADAS AEB ACC LKA]
    I --> J2[Autonomy Planner]
    I --> J3[HMI Display HUD]
    J1 --> K[Vehicle Actuators Brake Steer Throttle]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> L[Driver]
    L --> M[Feedback Driver Monitoring]
    M --> G
```

## 4.2 Scene Analysis – Hierarchical Reasoning Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]
**Differentiate between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation. Give one automotive use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Semantic Segmentation** assigns a class label to *every pixel* but does *not* distinguish between separate instances of the same class. Example: all car pixels are labeled "car" uniformly. *(1 mark)*
- **Instance Segmentation** assigns a class label *and* a unique instance ID to each detected object, producing individual masks for "car-1", "car-2", etc. *(1 mark)*
- **Automotive use case**: Semantic → free-space / drivable-area detection. Instance → counting and tracking individual vehicles or pedestrians in a crosswalk. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]
**List and briefly explain any three functional blocks of an in-vehicle vision system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Image Acquisition Block** – Multi-camera setup (front wide, narrow, surround, in-cabin) with CMOS sensors and hardware synchronization. *(1 mark)*
2. **Perception Block** – Deep CNN models running on automotive SoC (NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ) for detection, segmentation, depth. *(1 mark)*
3. **Decision & Actuation Block** – Translates perception output into AEB, ACC, LKA commands; ISO 26262 functional safety ensures fail-operational behavior. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. An internal *choice* is allowed (either (a) OR (b) attempt is often optional in some KTU variants; here we provide both for completeness).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam – Model, Module 4]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical in-vehicle vision system. Discuss the role of multi-sensor fusion in such a system.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An in-vehicle vision system is a real-time, safety-critical perception platform. It transforms raw camera frames into a structured understanding of the road environment, the vehicle, and the driver.

**Architecture (4 marks):** [Refer to the block diagram from Section 4.1]

The system consists of:

1. **Sensor Layer** – Front wide-angle (120°), front narrow (30°), front stereo, four surround-view, rear, and in-cabin driver-monitoring cameras. Each camera connects to the central ECU via automotive Ethernet or FPD-Link.
2. **Pre-processing Layer** – Synchronization (timestamped via PTP), HDR synthesis (handling tunnel exit), lens distortion correction using the intrinsic matrix $K$ and distortion coefficients.
3. **Perception Layer** – Deep CNN backbone (e.g., EfficientDet, YOLOv8, BEVFormer) produces:
   - 2D/3D object detection (cars, pedestrians, cyclists)
   - Semantic/instance segmentation (lane, road, sidewalk)
   - Monocular or stereo depth
   - Lane curvature and free-space boundaries
4. **Tracking & Fusion Layer** – Multi-object tracking (SORT, DeepSORT) maintains object IDs; an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) fuses camera with LiDAR and radar, exploiting the complementary strengths of each sensor.
5. **Reasoning & Output Layer** – Scene-graph generation, intention prediction, occupancy grid; outputs are warnings, control signals to brake/steer, or HD-map updates.

**Role of Multi-Sensor Fusion (2 marks):**

- **Camera** provides *semantic* richness (texture, color, signs) but fails in low light, fog, and at long range.
- **LiDAR** provides *metric* 3D depth, robust to lighting, but is sparse and expensive.
- **Radar** provides *velocity* (Doppler) and works in adverse weather, but has low resolution.
- **Fusion** → the system becomes *complementary* (no single failure mode is fatal) and *redundant* (ASIL-D safety), satisfying ISO 26262.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Modern ADAS platforms (e.g., Tesla FSD, Mobileye SuperVision, Waymo Driver) are *vision-centric* with radar/LiDAR as redundancy, achieving 360° perception at < 100 ms latency.

#### Q.A (b) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam – Model, Module 4]
**Derive the depth–disparity relationship $Z = fB/d$ for a rectified stereo camera. A stereo pair has $f = 1400$ px and $B = 0.25$ m. An obstacle appears with disparity $d = 35$ px. Compute the depth and the depth error if disparity quantization is $\Delta d = 0.5$ px.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** [See Section 3.2 for full step-by-step]

- **Step 1:** Set up two parallel cameras, baseline $B$ apart, same focal length $f$, rectified.
- **Step 2:** A 3D point $P$ at depth $Z$ projects to $x_L = fX/Z$ in the left image and $x_R = f(X-B)/Z$ in the right image.
- **Step 3:** Subtracting gives $d = x_L - x_R = fB/Z$.
- **Step 4:** Inverting yields $Z = fB/d$.
- **Step 5:** Differentiating gives $|\Delta Z| = (Z^2 / fB) |\Delta d|$.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Depth:**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{1400 \times 0.25}{35} = \frac{350}{35} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Depth error:**

$$|\Delta Z| = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} |\Delta d| = \frac{10^2}{1400 \times 0.25} \times 0.5 = \frac{100}{350} \times 0.5 = 0.1429 \text{ m} \approx 14.3 \text{ cm}$$

**Interpretation (bonus):**
A 0.5 px disparity error becomes 14.3 cm depth uncertainty at 10 m. This *quadratic* growth with distance justifies why far-field stereo (≥ 50 m) demands sub-pixel matching (e.g., SGM, ELAS) and larger baselines.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Q.B (a) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam – Model, Module 4]
**Explain the optical flow constraint equation. Show how the Lucas–Kanade method solves the aperture problem. Why does LK fail at uniform regions, and how is it overcome in modern scene-analysis pipelines?**

**Model Solution:**

**Optical Flow Constraint (2 marks):**
Assuming brightness constancy, a 1st-order Taylor expansion yields:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This is *one* equation in *two* unknowns $(u, v)$ — the **aperture problem**.

**Lucas–Kanade Solution (2 marks):**
Assume flow is locally constant in a $W \times W$ window. Stack the constraint for all $N = W^2$ pixels and solve the normal equations:

$$A^\top A \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = -A^\top \begin{bmatrix} I_t \end{bmatrix}$$

$$A^\top A = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Why LK Fails (1.5 marks):**
- In a **uniform region** (constant intensity), $I_x = I_y = 0$ → $A^\top A$ is the **zero matrix** and cannot be inverted. The flow is undefined.
- In a **single edge**, $A^\top A$ has *one* large and one small eigenvalue → only the *normal flow* (perpendicular to the edge) is recoverable, not the tangential flow.

**Modern Overcomes (1.5 marks):**
- **Coarse-to-fine (image pyramid) LK** to handle large motions.
- **Deep optical flow networks** (FlowNet, RAFT, GMA) learn flow end-to-end, naturally handling textureless regions via learned priors.
- **Hybrid LK + learning** (e.g., DeepLK) uses a CNN to predict the local affine warp before LK refinement.
- **Multi-modal fusion**: combine flow with stereo, IMU, and wheel odometry to fill the gaps.

**Application (bonus):** LK flow underpins classic visual odometry in **monocular SLAM** (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono) used in autonomous driving.

#### Q.B (b) [7 Marks] – [KTU University Exam – Model, Module 4]
**Discuss the role of scene graphs in high-level scene understanding. How are they constructed in modern deep-learning pipelines, and what are their limitations for real-time in-vehicle deployment?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **scene graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a directed graph where vertices are objects (with attributes: class, bbox, mask) and edges are predicates (e.g., *holding*, *next to*, *above*).

**Role in Scene Understanding (2 marks):**
- Encodes *relational* context — a pedestrian is *walking on* the *sidewalk* and *facing* the *crosswalk*.
- Enables *commonsense reasoning*: e.g., a person on a sidewalk will likely *not* enter the lane.
- Bridges perception to *planning*: planning modules query the graph for "what is around me" and "what will happen next."

**Construction Pipeline (2.5 marks):**
1. **Object Detection** (Faster R-CNN) → set of bounding boxes and classes.
2. **Object Feature Extraction** (RoIAlign + GloVe word embeddings for class).
3. **Edge Prediction** – a Graph Neural Network (GNN) such as **MotifNet, RelTR, or SGTR** scores every possible predicate between every pair of objects.
4. **Refinement** – message passing between nodes refines the relation predictions.
5. **Output** – triplets $\langle o_i, r, o_j \rangle$ such as $\langle \text{Pedestrian}, \text{crossing}, \text{Road} \rangle$.

**Limitations for In-Vehicle Real-Time Use (1.5 marks):**
- **Latency** – SGG models take 200–500 ms per frame; far above the 30–100 ms ADAS budget.
- **Long-tail predicates** – rare relations (e.g., "occluding") are poorly predicted.
- **Open-vocabulary limitation** – training sets (Visual Genome) have only ~150 object classes and ~50 predicates; vehicles encounter thousands of edge cases.
- **Robustness** – graph predictions are brittle to occlusion, and the auto-regressive decoding can cascade errors.
- **Mitigation** – production systems use *compact* scene representations: **BEV (Bird's Eye View) feature maps** and **temporal scene tensors** (e.g., Waymo's MotionLM) instead of full scene graphs.

**Conclusion (optional, 0.5 mark):** Scene graphs remain a research-grade representation; production autonomy relies on BEV-based neural reasoning and end-to-end driving models (UniAD, VAD).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks on Module 4 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing semantic vs. instance segmentation** – Many students write "semantic segmentation" when the question is clearly about instance segmentation (or vice versa). Always clarify the *instance distinction* in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** in the depth formula – Marks are deducted if you write $Z = fB/d$ without stating the units ($f$ in pixels, $B$ in meters, $Z$ in meters). Always annotate.
> 3. **Mixing up optical flow vectors $(u, v)$ with image coordinates $(x, y)$** – $(u, v)$ is *velocity*; $(x, y)$ is *position*. Examiners spot this immediately.
> 4. **Forgetting the brightness-constancy assumption** in OFCE derivations – You must explicitly state the assumption *before* taking the Taylor expansion.
> 5. **Not mentioning ISO 26262 / ASIL ratings** when discussing ADAS – A 1-mark 'key term' question often asks for the safety standard; stating it adds value.
> 6. **Ignoring the rectified-stereo assumption** in $Z = fB/d$ – If the cameras are not rectified, the equation is invalid; the epipolar constraint $\mathbf{x}'^\top F \mathbf{x} = 0$ must be used instead.
> 7. **Writing code in an exam that doesn't ask for code** – The KTU pattern wants *conceptual* answers, not Python snippets. You lose marks for irrelevant code blocks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Scene Analysis** = the *interpretation* layer of CV; it produces **semantic**, **geometric**, **temporal**, and **relational** understanding.
- ☐ **Core sub-tasks**: classification, detection, semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, depth, pose, tracking, scene graph, 3D reconstruction.
- ☐ **Pinhole model**: $s\mathbf{x} = K[R \mid t]\mathbf{X}$ — the universal camera projection equation.
- ☐ **Intrinsic matrix** $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — calibrate once per camera.
- ☐ **Stereo depth**: $Z = fB/d$ — disparity is *inversely* proportional to depth; error grows as $Z^2$.
- ☐ **Epipolar constraint**: $\mathbf{x}'^\top F \mathbf{x} = 0$ — fundamental matrix has rank 2.
- ☐ **Optical flow (LK)**: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$, solved via least-squares; needs texture-rich windows.
- ☐ **Scene graph**: $G = (V, E)$ — objects as nodes, relations as edges; research-grade, not yet real-time.
- ☐ **In-vehicle vision system**: multi-camera (front wide, front narrow, stereo, surround, cabin) → ECU → perception → fusion → ADAS output.
- ☐ **Performance targets**: $\leq$ 100 ms latency, $\geq$ 30 FPS, $-40^\circ$C to $+85^\circ$C, ISO 26262 ASIL-B/D.
- ☐ **Sensor fusion rule of thumb**: Camera (semantic) + LiDAR (metric 3D) + Radar (velocity) = complementary and redundant.
- ☐ **Key algorithms to remember**: YOLO, Mask R-CNN, DeepLab, MiDaS, RAFT, ORB-SLAM, BEVFormer, UniAD.
- ☐ **Common KTU exam keywords**: *drivable area, ego-motion, time-to-collision (TTC), functional safety, Bird's-Eye View, occupancy grid*.
- ☐ **Pitfall formula**: never use `|x|` (vertical bar) inside markdown table cells — write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ to avoid breaking KTU's renderer.
